Building a brighter future for all
We remain committed to helping the nation prosper and
to improving living standards for all Australians. We have
continued to support our customers and communities
through changing economic conditions, while keeping
the Bank safe and strong.
As we look forward, we have refreshed our strategy
to focus on supporting Australia’s economic growth
– remaining at the centre of our customers’ financial
lives, helping them access housing, grow and protect
their wealth, and start, run and grow their business.
Our purpose continues to inspire us to deliver
for our customers and the nation.

Acknowledgment of Country
Commonwealth Bank of Australia respectfully acknowledges the Traditional Owners of the Lands
across Australia as the continuing custodians of Country and Culture. We pay our respects to First
Nations peoples and their Elders, past and present.
Our registered office is located on the Lands of the Gadigal People.

Financials are presented on a continuing operations basis, except the Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio which includes discontinued operations.
Cash NPAT represents net profit after tax and non-controlling interests before non-cash items including hedging and IFRS volatility, and gains or losses
on acquisitions, disposal, closure, capital repatriation and demerger of controlled businesses. This is management's preferred measure of the Group's financial
performance. All figures relate to the full year ended 30 June 2025 and comparisons are to the year ended 30 June 2024. For data sources and definitions,
see Glossary on pages 438–451.

Significant judgements

Sustainability-related information includes non-financial information and refers to environmental, social and governance
topics and themes that are important to our stakeholders and to CBA, as they have the potential to impact stakeholders
and CBA’s financial prospects in the short to medium term. While some topics are traditionally linked to sustainability,
others are included based on their perceived potential to materially impact our stakeholders and CBA’s financial prospects.
For significant judgements in relation to our financial statements see relevant Notes on pages 229–364 in the Financial report.
For important climate-related information on assumptions related to our climate disclosures, see pages 389–429 in the
Climate disclosures appendix.

Our operating context

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Information included in this report relates to the Group. Financial information is consolidated and includes those entities over
which we have control. Non-financial information considers key stakeholders in our value chain predominantly in Australia,
unless stated otherwise. This report covers the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 (this year), or the Group’s 2025
financial year (FY25). It also includes information about material subsequent events occurring after 30 June 2025 and before
the Board’s approval of the report on 13 August 2025. The presentation currency is Australian dollars (AUD) with amounts
disclosed rounded to the nearest billion dollars, unless otherwise stated.

SUSTAINABILITY

This year our climate disclosures are included in this report, not in a separate
Climate Report. Our climate disclosures focus on decision-useful information
regarding the Bank’s approach to climate-related risks and opportunities.
We will continue to evolve our climate disclosures in line with new mandatory
climate reporting requirements that will apply for future reporting periods.

CREATING VALUE

For more information on our materiality assessment, see pages 72–73.

OVERVIEW

Our Annual Report includes material information on the
strategic priorities, corporate governance and risk management
of the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) and its
subsidiaries (together the Group or the Bank), as well as our
financial and non-financial performance. The material themes
in this report are informed by our sustainability materiality
assessment detailed on pages 72 to 73. We continue to
evolve our reporting to align with changes in legislation,
market practice and feedback from our stakeholders.

Each year we conduct a qualitative sustainability materiality assessment to identify and disclose against the
sustainability-related topics most relevant to our stakeholders in our value chain and to the Bank. The assessment
considers sustainability-related impacts, risks and opportunities that have the potential to influence CBA’s ability to create
value over the short to medium term (1–5 years). This year’s assessment reviewed a range of sustainability topics, which
were prioritised using our 5x5 risk matrix and exercised significant judgement. The prioritised topics were then grouped
into overarching themes that informed the structure of this report. Theme selection involved significant judgement,
guided by the results of our materiality assessment.

MESSAGE FROM OUR CHAIR AND CEO

7
COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Meaningfully delivering
on our purpose

OVERVIEW

Our strategy considers diverse
sustainability-related impacts, helping
us address environmental, social, and
governance risks and opportunities.
We are making decisions today with
a focus on long-term outcomes, even
when they come at a short-term cost.

This year we extended our
commitment to regional Australia,
keeping all regional branches open until
at least mid-2027, and converted 15
Western Australian Bankwest branches
to be CBA-branded.

Our customers need banking to
become simpler, safer and better.
Improvements to our risk culture and
risk management, combined with
disciplined balance sheet and capital
management, are essential to support
the economy during crises and deliver
good shareholder outcomes.
Learn more about our strategic priorities
on pages 16–25.

Delivering for our
customers
Being the trusted financial partner
for Australian retail and business
customers remains key to our
strategy and long-term success.

During the year we have constructively
participated in national policy
discussions and advocated for changes
that support safe, secure and reliable
banking services.
To keep pace with technological
change, we invested $2.3 billion in
modernising our systems, migrating
data, and building world-class
engineering and AI capabilities.
With persistently high levels of fraud,
scams, cyber threats and financial
crime, we have invested over $900
million this year to help protect our
customers. We have also reduced
customer fraud and scam losses
by 76% since peak losses in late
2022. We continue to work with
other organisations and Government
to build national resilience against
these threats which continue to
impact too many customers.
Learn more about our approach to
sustainability on pages 68–99.

We continue to support customers
with a range of money management
tools and educational materials.
For customers facing financial
difficulty, we have initiated
more than 139,000 tailored
payment arrangements.

Supporting
a prosperous, resilient
and optimistic nation

To serve a broad range of customers we
are focused on delivering a better, safer
and more efficient banking experience.

A strong and resilient financial
system is required to support
a strong economy.

Maintaining strong capital and prudent
balance sheet settings enables us
to maintain necessary investment
to deliver resilient and safe banking
services, and support our customers
and the economy. We carefully consider
funding, credit and liquidity needs
and monitor non-financial risks
such as operational resilience,
compliance with laws and regulations,
and broader environmental and
social risks.
Australia needs a financial system
that works for all stakeholders and
inspires optimism about the future.
We continue to share customer
insights and other perspectives
to help inform policies that can
support a strong financial system
and economy.

Delivering sustainable
performance
Our financial results reflect our
customer focus and disciplined
execution of our strategic priorities.
Our strong financial position enables
us to continue supporting our
customers and deliver a sustainable
dividend. We have declared a
final dividend of $2.60 per share,
fully franked.
Learn more about our financial
performance on pages 26–33.

Outlook
We know many of our customers are still
cautious with global volatility creating
uncertainty. The Australian economy
has remained resilient with strong
fundamentals. Looking ahead, there are
many reasons to feel optimistic about
Australia’s future including modest
economic growth, a healthy labour
market and easing interest rates.
Our role is to support our customers
and the nation. We will stay focused on
investing in our business and executing
our strategy to bring our purpose to
life. We will continue to work with key
stakeholders to advocate for issues that
are in the national interest, and policy
settings that help sustainably support
services that Australians value.

Thank you
On behalf of the Board and Executive
Leadership Team, we would like to
thank our people for their care and
commitment to our customers, our
customers for trusting us with their
banking, and to you, our shareholders
for your continued support.

Paul O’Malley
Chair

Matt Comyn
Chief Executive Officer

We want to help build Australia’s
future economy by supporting
business growth and productivity.
Productivity is key to improving living
standards and its benefits need to
be shared fairly so all communities
can prosper. We are committed
to allocating capital intentionally,
ensuring banking services remain

Many Australians and Australian
businesses trust us with their
transaction banking and deposit
relationship. We want to deliver
experiences our customers value and
continually deepen our relationships
with them. Ongoing investment in
leading technology, artificial intelligence
(AI) and our people enables us to deliver
personalised banking at scale.

Financial services play a vital role in our
customers’ everyday lives. Disruptions
and outages in banking services prevent
customers from making payments or
running their businesses. We also play
an important role to support economic
growth by lending to businesses.

Learn more about our operating context

However, we know there is much
more to do. Our operating context
has shifted, and we expect the
next decade to bring complex
geopolitical, reputational and
competitive challenges. To better
navigate the future, we have
refreshed our strategy to continue
positioning the Bank to deliver for
our customers, communities, the
nation and our shareholders.

We offer the largest branch and ATM
network, Australia-based call centres and
the nation’s most popular banking app.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Australia’s economy remains resilient
and relatively well positioned. Our
country is still an attractive place
to live, work and invest. We face
headwinds which highlight the need
for long-term economic reform
and call for a confident vision for
growth, fiscal responsibility with
future generations in mind, and
supply-side reform in areas like
housing and energy. Australia also
needs to maintain sovereignty of
nationally important industries,
including Australia’s financial system.
Governments and industries need to
work together to address challenging
domestic issues and deliver a stronger
future for Australia.

We have made progress in our strategy
to support customers through
changing economic conditions. This
year we lent $42 billion to businesses
to help them grow and assisted over
140,000 households in buying a home.
We have strengthened our focus on
customer experience and consistently
invested in leading technology
and data capabilities. Importantly,
we have continued to become a
safer organisation with greater risk
maturity, transparency and discipline.

accessible, and advocating for a strong
financial system and an economy
that works for all Australians.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Rising geopolitical tensions,
global economic challenges, and
technological changes are causing
many of our customers to feel financial
pressure and uncertainty about their
futures. Community concerns about
housing and energy affordability, as
well as intergenerational inequality,
continue to grow.

Refreshing our strategy

SUSTAINABILITY

As Australia’s leading retail and
business bank, we play an important
role in supporting our customers
and helping the nation prosper.
We are committed to improving
living standards for all Australians.
By remaining a strong, safe and
resilient bank, we can continue
lending to our customers through
economic cycles, investing for the
future, and delivering sustainable
returns for our shareholders.

CREATING VALUE

Our purpose, building a brighter future for all, reflects our
enduring commitment to Australia. It inspires us to be better for
our customers, people and communities, and shows our optimism
for the future. By maintaining our balance sheet strength and
executing our strategy we can deliver on our purpose.

Our stakeholders
Why CBA?

Leading franchise: We are Australia’s leading transaction and deposit
bank for both households and businesses.

We seek to build a brighter future for our
customers, our people, communities and the
broader economy. To do this consistently, and
attract the capital required, we need to deliver
sustainable shareholder returns.

Strong balance sheet and risk management: We maintain a resilient
balance sheet and conservative capital, liquidity and funding settings,
as well as sound provision coverage.

Learn more about how we create value on
pages 12–13 and our financial performance
on pages 26–33.

Sustainable returns to shareholders: We have consistently
delivered sector leading return on equity and a sustainable
fully-franked dividend.

Understanding our stakeholders’ needs and
expectations allows us to consider their different
views and deliver balanced stakeholder outcomes.
Key stakeholders are individuals and groups in our
value chain whose interests are materially affected
or could be affected by the Bank’s activities.
We aim to improve our trust and reputation by
putting customer needs first and making a broader
contribution to the community.
Learn more about our approach to stakeholder
engagement on pages 70–71.

Our businesses

We are exposed to financial, non-financial and
strategic risks through the products and services
we offer. These risks are managed through our
Risk Management Framework. Our material
risks are those which require extra focus, due to
their potential to materially impact the Bank, our

now and in the future.

OVERVIEW

for shareholders and other stakeholders.

2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Who we are

10

11
COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Our operating context

Maintaining our position as Australia’s leading bank requires a consistent focus on our customers – building
trusted relationships and continuing to invest in technology, data, and insights to deliver personalised and
value-added experiences.

Advances in AI, data analytics and digital infrastructure are transforming the banking landscape. These
technologies offer opportunities to enhance customer experience, streamline processes, and improve risk
management and operational efficiency. At the same time, they introduce new risks – including cyber security
threats, scams, privacy concerns and the use of AI.

During the year inflation continued to ease within the Reserve Bank of Australia’s (RBA) target range allowing
the RBA to start reducing official interest rates. While economic growth continues, rising public sector
expenditure and low levels of productivity growth highlight the growing need for long-term economic reform.

Rapidly adopting technology such as AI to modernise existing industries and build new industries is critical for
our economy. Careful consideration will be needed to incentivise productive investment, build sovereign capability
and retain economic benefits in Australia for all Australians.

We maintain a conservative balance sheet, with capital, funding, and credit settings carefully managed
to ensure resilience and ongoing support for our customers and the broader economy.

We continue to invest in modernising our technology platforms and strengthening cyber resilience to safeguard
operations and provide secure, reliable services. We are committed to managing technology risks and engaging
regulators to support safer and responsible innovation. Ongoing investment in technology, data, and our
people is essential to maintaining trust, building long-term strength, and staying ahead of technology-led change.

Heightened geopolitical risks

Trust and reputation

The global geopolitical environment continues to change with heightened tensions and policy uncertainty
weighing on trade, energy and investment markets. Increasing global trade restrictions, strategic
competition and regional conflicts are disrupting supply chains, limiting access to key technologies and
reshaping capital flows. Cyber threats and attacks continue to evolve, requiring heightened vigilance by
people and businesses.

Trust is fundamental to long-term value creation and building strong customer relationships. Many Australians and
Australian businesses trust us with their transaction banking and deposit relationship. This requires a continued
focus on being safe and strong. As a bank, we are inherently exposed to reputational risk with issues such as data
privacy, conduct and governance having the potential to quickly erode stakeholder confidence.

For more information on our strategy, see pages 16–25.
For more information on what matters most to our stakeholders, see pages 70–71.
For more information on how we are managing our risks, see pages 58–67.

Expectations continue to rise around transparency, the responsible use of data and AI, and the delivery of fairer
and responsible outcomes. Stakeholders are also increasingly focused on how we support Australia’s economy and
contribute to financial inclusion and broader community outcomes.
The Bank has an important role to play. We are working closely with government and industry to support more
sustainable outcomes for our customers and communities. These efforts are fundamental to maintaining financial
system stability and resilience, and to supporting the long-term health of the Australian economy and society.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Australia’s banking system remains resilient. We actively assess downside scenarios through robust stress
testing, scenario analysis, and balance sheet risk management to strengthen operational resilience and
safeguard against potential shocks. The Bank is well positioned to navigate external challenges and continue
supporting our customers and the broader economy.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Australia’s economy has continued to show resilience, supported by strong population growth along
with healthy labour market conditions. While overall conditions remain strong, some household budgets,
particularly for younger Australians, renters and mortgage holders, continue to face pressures from elevated
interest rates, rising essential costs and housing affordability challenges.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Evolving technology

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Macroeconomic and cost-of-living pressures

SUSTAINABILITY

Across the Bank, we regularly assess the external environment to understand stakeholder views
and evolving themes. This helps us refine our strategic priorities, respond to risks and opportunities,
and support informed decision making to deliver sustainable outcomes for our stakeholders.

Competition in the banking sector remains elevated, driven by rising customer expectations, digital innovation and
industry participants pursuing greater market share. Customers are increasingly seeking personalised, high-value
experiences and seamless service. Pricing pressure remains evident across deposits and lending, where customer
demand for value and changing channel dynamics contribute to a highly competitive environment. We continue to
see large technology firms expand their presence, especially in payments and small business segments. As more
customers engage through digital channels, delivering efficient, high-quality service at scale to all Australians
is essential. Continued investment in technology and innovation helps us to meet these expectations while
maintaining our operational resilience.

stakeholders and evolving trends. This can influence our
strategic priorities, decision-making processes and overall
risk management approach.

FINANCIAL REPORT

This year we refreshed our strategy to better respond to our
changing operating context. We have emphasised building
Australia’s future economy to improve living standards
for all Australians, remaining at the centre of our customers’
financial lives and keeping our customers safe.

Learn more on pages 18–19.

to build tomorrow’s bank today for our
customers. Since then, we have made progress
in supporting our customers, investing in our
people and technology, and becoming a safer
and stronger bank.

CREATING VALUE FOR OUR STAKEHOLDERS

19

For Australia’s economy to prosper, Australian businesses need to grow.
We are deeply focused on supporting businesses to drive productive growth in
our economy. By helping bring capital to Australia, CBA can lend to our business
customers to support job creation and contribute to Australia’s productivity.

Helping the nation prosper and
improving living standards for
all Australians.

To support sustainable access to banking services, we extended
our commitment to keep all regional CBA branches open to support
communities in regional Australia to the end of July 2027.
Our continued focus on balance sheet strength and our conservative
approach to managing capital and funding provides capacity to support
our customers and the nation, while still delivering sustainable returns
to shareholders.
For more information on how we are supporting our customers and communities,
see pages 74–79.

$880m+
mistaken and scam payments
prevented with NameCheck
since 2023

Additionally, policy areas such as productivity, affordable housing and tax reform
also influence the strength of Australia’s financial system and economy. CBA
will continue to contribute to the national conversation on these and other policy
issues to help shape Australia’s prosperity for future generations.

For more information on how
we are supporting Australia’s
transition, see pages 104–107.

Contributing to national fraud and scams resilience
We continue to contribute to nationwide initiatives to better protect Australians from fraud and scams.

$42bn
lent to businesses
to help them grow

Technology solutions are making a positive impact. NameCheck has been used over 110 million times and prevented
more than $880 million mistaken payments and scams since July 2023. CBA is leading industry efforts to combat scams
by working with telecommunications companies and digital platforms to help better protect Australians. We have shared
over 18,500 malicious URLs, SMS, phone numbers, websites and social media accounts with the Anti-Scam Intelligence
Loop since July 2024.
CBA has also joined BioCatch Trust, a global first inter-bank, behaviour and device-based, fraud and scams
intelligence-sharing network. We have also partnered with Macquarie University and its AI-focused spinout, APATE.AI,
whose AI-powered conversational anti-scam bots help extract scam intelligence and receive scam phone calls.
For more information on how we are helping to protect our customers from fraud and scams, see page 76.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Keeping customers’ accounts safe and secure remains a priority. We invested
more than $900 million this year to help protect our customers from
fraud, scams, cyber threats and financial crime. Our continued investment
helped reduce customer fraud and scam losses by over 30% this year.
We acknowledge there is still more to be done. We remain actively engaged
with governments, regulators, banks and other industries to support a
whole-of-ecosystem approach to combat fraud, scams, cyber threats and
financial crime.

customers served
from 17.6m FY24

We actively engage governments, banks and industry partners on issues that
require coordinated effort and shared accountability. Our aim is that our banking
services continue to be safe, secure and reliable. This includes combatting fraud,
scams, cyber threats and financial crime; maintaining access to cash; improving
the efficiency of Australia’s cash system; and seeking regulation that keeps pace
with technology and shifts in customer preferences.

To deliver this, we need efficient
regulation to help accelerate
energy supply and contribute to
productivity. With appropriate
sharing of costs and benefits, we
believe Australia can transition
to net zero by 2050 and create
a brighter future for all.

FINANCIAL REPORT

We maintained our focus on supporting customers with a range of options,
which included improved access to hardship assistance and over 139,000
tailored payment arrangements for those most in need. More than three
million customers each month use our digital money management tools to
help them manage their finances.

18.2m

We have a positive vision for growth focused on delivering balanced outcomes for

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

The past few years have been challenging for Australian households and
businesses, with higher inflation and cost-of-living pressures. We remain
committed to supporting our customers, communities and the nation when
they need us most.

Australia’s financial system has served us well, supporting economic stability,
resilience and prosperity. As a business that does well when the Australian
economy does well, we are committed to helping improve living standards for
all Australians. The Bank has a role to play in constructively participating in
long-term strategic policy discussions and advocating for changes that serve
the national interest.

zero by 2050, maintaining
energy affordability will be
key so that Australians can

access to affordable and reliable

to supporting our economy.

SUSTAINABILITY

Our digital innovations are making it easier for businesses to access lending. Over
750,000 customers are eligible to access capital more easily through our BizExpress
assisted or online pathways, with $1.2 billion funded this year. Additionally, over
400,000 customers are eligible for pre-approved commercial lending to access
funding to support business growth. To help businesses get started, we have
expanded the number of customers eligible for online bank guarantees to 30%.

CREATING VALUE

We are committed to building deeper and more meaningful relationships with
our customers, understanding their needs and providing tailored support. To help
manage cash flow, our Capital Growth Account continues to provide businesses
flexibility, with around 50% of customers using this capability.

Australia’s transition to a
net zero economy by 2050 is
underway. Meeting Australia’s
2030 emissions reduction
target requires significant
investment. The energy sector
continues to be the biggest
contributor to Australia’s
domestic emissions. We believe
that affordably decarbonising
Australia’s electricity grid
remains the most important step
for Australia’s transition.

OVERVIEW

Helping build
Australia’s
future economy

Our relationship-led business banking strategy has resulted in our continued
growth, with one in four Australian businesses choosing CBA as their main financial
institution. Business transaction accounts grew by 7% this year to 1.3 million and we
process the largest share of payments domestically. We also facilitated $286 billion
of international payments for our business customers this year.

2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Delivering more personalised digital experiences

22

CREATING VALUE FOR OUR STAKEHOLDERS

23

Leading in technology and AI

To meet our customers’ changing needs, we continue to invest to personalise
and differentiate our offering. We seek to exceed our customers’ expectations
every time they bank with us.

Supporting our people to use data, AI and technology to deliver
exceptional customer experiences more safely and securely.

Onboarding a new customer is often the first and an
important interaction with the Bank. Our focus is to
provide a safe and seamless path to the service they need,
while also preventing bad actors from creating accounts.
Eligible customers can now have immediate access to
their card in their digital wallet.

Investing in AI and delivery
Our strategy has consistently invested in technology, data
and analytics to deliver more personalised experiences
for our customers and make things easier for our people.
Our first major launch was in 2015, with our AI-driven
customer engagement engine (CEE). CEE runs over 2,000
real-time machine learning models and processes over
157 billion data points, supporting next best conversations
across all channels of banking. As the pace of technological
change increases, we are investing more in GenAI to help
deliver more personalised customer experiences.

Our Australian-first GenAI powered retail and business
customer messaging is delivering faster, more reliable
support, reducing call centre wait times and driving an
approximately 20% increase in customer interactions
through unassisted channels. We also use AI to
proactively alert customers of suspicious transactions,
with up to 3,000 confirmed fraudulent cases per day.
For non-fraud disputes, our GenAI model provides
customers with a tailored conversation to collect all
required information and speed up dispute resolution.

We continue to invest in the security and resilience of our technology
and strive to provide services that are reliable, safe, and available whenever
and wherever our customers need them. We have taken steps to improve
the reliability and availability of our systems to deliver more updates while
reducing impact on customers.

With many Australians preferring a simple digital-only banking experience, we have transformed Bankwest to become
a digital bank. Bankwest’s product offering is now simpler with a focus on home loans and everyday banking.
This year we relaunched Bankwest with a new brand, website and mobile app experience. Our new digital assets are
designed to be simpler and more intuitive. We also updated the user interface of our award-winning digital broker portal.

While closing our Bankwest branch network and converting 15 branches to be CBA-branded, we maintained consistent
growth, acquiring over 90,000 new-to-bank customers this year. We also minimised impact to our people by presenting
branch employees with job opportunities across the Bank. For around 2,000 Bankwest customers who we identified
as being reliant on branches, we have supported them with exploring alternative ways to do their banking. Bankwest
customers still have access to cash services via CBA IDMs/ATMs nationwide for depositing and withdrawing cash.

As the competition for talent intensifies, we continue to find ways to attract
and retain world-class AI and engineering talent. To deliver our strategy we are
investing in AI and engineering talent, increasing the overall share of engineers
in our technology workforce and supporting them to deploy change faster.
Our focus is to create exceptional customer experiences, partnering with
global technology leaders and empowering our people with the skills, tools and
platforms to build new products and features in a safe and responsible way.
This year we hired over 2,000 engineers and now have over 9,000 engineers
who bring valuable technology skills to CBA. We have also embedded 10
distinguished engineers in strategic areas to guide critical initiatives, and
shape our technology strategy and workforce planning. To help accelerate our
AI ambitions, we have also appointed six distinguished AI scientists.
For more information on our approach to responsible AI, see page 93.

The Hub is providing our teams with
the opportunity to spend three weeks
in Seattle, focused on solving clearly
defined customer and business
challenges. During this time, CBA
technology teams work with global
technology leaders including Amazon
Web Services and Microsoft to solve
the business problem. Teams are
expected to define a customer-centred
opportunity, then develop a solution
using AI technology.
To date, 84 people have completed
the program, resulting in nine
innovation initiatives that improved key
business processes such as call centre
automation, customer onboarding and
tooling modernisation.
The investment in time, people
and capability is supporting us to
build a culture of innovation and
collaboration, using new technologies
like Agentic AI. This empowers our
people to deliver better outcomes
and experiences for our customers.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Bankwest is well positioned to meet the expectations of digital-savvy customers who expect leading digital and broker
banking services. To support this, we introduced single and multi-use virtual cards in the Bankwest app. Our investments
in technology and modern cloud-based call centre platforms are helping us engage new-to-bank customers.

Building world-class capability
in engineering and AI

To strengthen our technology and
AI leadership and reimagine customer
experiences, we have established a
Tech Hub in Seattle.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Modernising our technology and data platforms is critical to delivering
better customer, risk and resilience outcomes. We have accelerated
our modernisation efforts. Our ambition is to continue building modern
technology by reducing system interdependencies and enhancing our
enterprise data platform to support our important customer interactions.
This work aims to improve our operational resilience and make it faster
to deliver change. We have successfully migrated our on-premise
data platforms to the cloud, and are continuing to invest in building
microservices enabling a simpler, more modern technology estate.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Modernising technology and data

SUSTAINABILITY

We have accelerated our efforts to strengthen our data,
analytics and AI foundations, modernising our data estate
by adopting cloud technologies that improve our ability
to deliver and scale analytical and AI outcomes. Over
the past year, our people have been using GenAI tools
to deliver better customer outcomes sooner. Our GenAI

assistant for business bankers has handled more than
250,000 questions, delivering responses over three times
faster than traditional measures. AI has also boosted
software engineers’ output. Over 70% of our engineers
are using AI-powered tools daily to help them produce
30% more code changes.

CREATING VALUE

We have prioritised key customer experiences in our retail
and business bank. To help us focus on what matters most
to our customers, we introduced a new approach to gather
direct customer feedback. Whether it be opening an account,

using tools to help manage personal or business finances,
or making payments easier – we want our customers to
have a positive experience.

OVERVIEW

Our ambition is to deliver excellent experiences every time
our customers interact with us. To do this we must provide
seamless banking, deliver service excellence and listen to our
customers’ experiences and expectations so we can continue
to improve.

Protecting customers through
leading risk management

Delivering
simpler, safer
and better

OVERVIEW

We continue to invest in our ambition to become a safer organisation with
leading risk management, transparency and discipline. Risk management
is key to maintaining our customers’ trust that we will keep their money
and data safe. We must sustain a sound risk culture while enhancing
how we use information to improve customer experiences.

CREATING VALUE

Investing in leading risk management supports our efforts to protect our
customers and improve risk outcomes. We are focused on using technology,
including AI, to deliver better outcomes, especially in relation to financial crime,
fraud and scams. We process more than 20 million payments daily, using
thousands of fraud and decisioning rules in real-time to detect, defend and
deter. We also send up to 35,000 fraud alerts per day from the CommBank app.
If a customer confirms it is fraud, our systems automatically secure the card to
protect the customer from further fraudulent activity.

SUSTAINABILITY

Keeping our customers’ personal information safe remains a focus. We are
updating our Group Privacy Statement to improve transparency around
automated decision making and the personal information we use, following
reforms to the Privacy Act 1988. We continue to focus on strengthening
our personal information management through regular reviews of our risk
framework and updates to internal policies.
For more information on how we are managing our risks, see pages 58–67.
For more information on how we are helping to protect our customers from fraud
and scams, see page 76.

Keeping the Bank safe and
strong positions us to support
customers when needed.

Through disciplined balance sheet and capital management, we continue
to maintain our key balance sheet settings and capital levels to mitigate
macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty. We aim to both support and
lend to customers during challenging times, invest in our business and pay
dividends to our shareholders.

$2.3bn
investment spend in
strategic priorities

Our consistent disciplined focus on generating organic capital enables us to
invest in our efforts to keep banking services safe and secure, and to improve
our customers’ banking experience. We continue to focus on our core banking
businesses in Australia and New Zealand. During the year we completed the sale
of our shareholdings in Hangzhou Bank and Vietnam International Commercial
Joint Stock Bank, and we may consider other divestments over time. This has
released capital that can now be allocated to our core banking activities.

25%

For more information on our financial performance, see pages 26–33.

deposit funding ratio

improvement in recovery
time of critical incidents

The cyber security landscape is constantly evolving, driven by both technological
innovation and emerging threats. This requires ongoing vigilance, preparedness
and investment in protection capabilities. The Bank applies a cyber security
risk management framework aligned with industry standards and maintains an
information security capability in accordance with domestic and international
regulatory obligations. We are focused on building world-class cyber capabilities,
prioritising the protection of critical assets, instilling discipline to deliver securely
at speed, and helping safeguard Australians through partnerships with industry,
community and Government. We continuously scan the external environment for
emerging threats and look for innovative ways to proactively keep our customers
and Bank information safe.
For more information on how we are managing our risks, see pages 58–67.
For more information on how we are helping to protect our customers and the
Bank from cyber threats, see pages 89–90.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

78%

Stronger operational resilience provides foundations to deliver services safely
and reliably. We have strengthened processes to meet Australian Prudential
Regulation Authority (APRA) Prudential Standard CPS 230 Operational risk
management (CPS 230). The standard aims to improve operational resilience
with requirements for operational risk, business continuity and service
provider management.

We continue to modernise the way
we manage services and integrate
security into our development
processes. Streamlined and
automated processes and approvals
have accelerated delivery, with
over 10,200 successful system
updates in March – our highest
to date – and our fastest average
recovery time for resolving issues.
We are focused on understanding
critical operations, improving our
systems uptime and restoring
services quickly. Major incidents
trigger retrospectives to identify
root causes, learn and prevent
recurrence. We have seen some
promising outcomes from our
resilience focus so far. This year
we have increased the pace of
technology changes, reduced
the amount of critical incidents
by approximately 30% and have
improved recovery time of critical
incidents by 25% – enabling faster,
safer delivery.

FINANCIAL REPORT

The Bank takes a long-term disciplined approach to balance sheet and
capital management. We carefully consider future impacts to funding, credit
and liquidity needs. Decisions to strengthen our balance sheet may reduce
short-term earnings but provide strength and stability to enable us to support
our customers. During the year we have maintained strong provision coverage
and conservative funding metrics. Our deposit funding ratio remained strong
at 78%, reflecting our stable and resilient funding base. Structural hedges
continue to support our long-term strategy, and our capital position remained
well above regulatory minimums, with a CET1 ratio of 12.3%.

Financial services are essential to our customers’ everyday lives. Service
outages can prevent customers accessing their money, making payments
or running their business, and can negatively impact the economy. With one
in three Australians and one in four Australian businesses calling us their
main financial institution, our banking services need to be secure, resilient
and reliable. Our focus is on getting the basics right and fixing things quickly
when we get it wrong.

Improving resilience

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Keeping the Bank’s balance sheet strong positions us well to support our
customers and the economy, and deliver sustainable returns for our shareholders.

Delivering secure, resilient
and reliable banking

The full year dividend of $4.85 per share, fully franked, reflects the Bank’s strong
capital position. Our aim is to deliver sector-leading returns and a sustainable
dividend. To deliver sustainable dividends we seek to:

shareholders hold CBA
shares directly, over
13 million hold CBA
shares indirectly through
their superannuation

• Generate organic capital through cash NPAT;
• Target a full-year payout ratio of 70–80% of cash NPAT; and
• Maximise the use of our franking account by paying fully franked dividends.
The dividend payout ratio was 79% of the Bank’s cash earnings for the full financial year.
The Dividend Reinvestment Plan (DRP) continues to be offered to shareholders.
No discount will be applied to shares allocated under the plan for the final dividend.
The DRP is anticipated to be satisfied in full by an on-market purchase of shares.

Total shareholder return (TSR) combines
both share price appreciation and
dividends paid. It represents the total
return to shareholders over time.

Return on equity (ROE) measures the
Bank’s profitability. It represents the
net profit generated as a percentage of
the equity shareholders have invested.

Earnings per share (EPS) measures
the Bank’s earnings growth. It is
calculated by dividing net profit after
tax by the number of shares on issue.

OVERVIEW

Our customer-focused franchise and consistent execution
continue to deliver sustainable returns for our shareholders.

Delivering strong and sustainable fully franked dividends benefits many Australians. We have over
800,000 direct shareholders and more than 13 million Australians benefit from holding CBA shares
indirectly through their superannuation. We have delivered a strong payout ratio of 79% near the
upper end of our target range. By maintaining strong performance and prudent balance sheet
settings, we are able to fund balance sheet growth, invest for the future and pay a sustainable
dividend over the long term.

We have seen steady growth in our household deposits and our retail transaction accounts
have increased by 4%. More than 97% of our home loans are linked to a transaction account.
We now have over 1.3 million business transaction accounts, a growth of 7%, with around
90% of business loans linked to a transaction account. Our business lending increased 11%
with strong performance across a number of sectors.

APRA (Level 2)

APRA sets minimum capital requirements to ensure banks hold sufficient capital to protect
deposit holders against unexpected losses. The Group has a strong capital position with
a Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 12.3% as at 30 June 2025, well in excess
of the minimum regulatory requirement of 10.25%. The Bank's CET1 ratio was supported
by organic capital generation from earnings and represents a $10.1 billion surplus to the
APRA minimum regulatory requirement.

The liquidity coverage ratio (LCR) represents the level of high-quality liquid assets available
to meet short-term obligations in a liquidity stress scenario. The Group’s average LCR for
the quarter ended 30 June 2025 was 130% which was significantly above the minimum
regulatory requirement of 100%.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Accelerated investment

The deposit funding ratio represents the proportion of total funding made up of customer
deposits. Customer deposits are considered the most stable source of funding. The strength
of our banking business has allowed us to maintain the highest share of stable household
deposits in Australia. Ensuring we are well funded has been critical to our ability to continue
supporting our customers and helping grow the Australian economy.

Operational excellence
Loan impairment expense

Cash NPAT

Cash NPAT increased 4% reflecting growth in operating income and lower loan impairment
expense, partly offset by the impact of inflation on operating expenses. Cash NPAT is
management’s preferred measure of the Group’s financial performance. It excludes non-cash
items that are non-recurring in nature and not considered representative of the Group’s
ongoing financial performance. Statutory NPAT includes non-cash items.

Loan impairment
expense

Net interest income increased due to volume growth in home and business lending
and a higher net interest margin. Net interest margin (NIM) is an important measure of our
financial performance. It represents the return on our interest earning assets (e.g. home loans)
after accounting for the costs of funding these assets (e.g. deposits). NIM increased 9 basis
points primarily driven by a reduction in the level of lower yielding liquid assets holdings.

Full-time
equivalent staff (FTE)

Operating expenses increased 6% driven by higher staff, information technology costs and
other expenses, partly offset by lower occupancy and equipment expenses. Staff costs
increased 7% driven by wage inflation and higher average FTE. Staff increases were due
to additional resources for the delivery of our strategic priorities and were partly offset
by productivity initiatives.

Consumer arrears show the proportion of our consumer
credit portfolio where customers have fallen behind on
their contractual loan repayments. Home loan and personal
loan arrears increased as some customers continue to be
impacted by cost-of-living pressures. Our home lending
portfolio remains well secured and 85% of home lending
customers remain in advance of scheduled repayments.

Retail Banking Services (RBS) provides simple and convenient banking products and services to personal
and private bank customers, helping them manage their everyday banking needs, buy a home or invest
for the future. RBS includes the retail banking activities under the Bankwest and Unloan brands.

Institutional Banking and Markets (IB&M) is helping to build Australia's future economy
by providing domestic and global financing and banking services to large corporate,
institutional and government customers.

OUR APPROACH TO CORPORATE GOVERNANCE

37
COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Message from
our Chair

Board strategic priorities

Being the ‘Bank for all Australians’
is at the heart of our strategy.
However, there is a real cost to
providing financial services including
branches and ATMs, our people, call
centres, cash transit and regulatory
compliance. To sustainably deliver good
outcomes for our customers, people
and communities, we also need to
generate shareholder returns.

Learn more about how we manage risk
on pages 58–67.

Executive oversight
Your Board plays a key role in
appointing the CEO and ensuring
appropriate succession plans.
Attracting, developing and retaining
Executive talent with the right skills
and values, at all levels, is needed to
deliver our strategic ambition and
enhance franchise value.

Learn more about our approach to
remuneration on pages 182–218.

Board effectiveness
Through robust agenda planning,
we continue to pay careful attention
to the time given to different
issues for discussion to enable the
Board to effectively deliberate and
resolve matters.
Having a balanced mix of skills,
experience and perspectives to
address current and emerging risks

Closing
Being the Chair of CBA, responsible
for steering the Bank during uncertain
times, is a significant responsibility.
It would not be possible without the
support of my fellow Directors, and
hard work of our dedicated leadership
team and people who want to exceed
our customers’ expectations. I extend
my thanks to our loyal and diverse
customers who choose to bank with us,
the communities we support, and you,
our shareholders.
Historically, in challenging times, the
strength of CBA has provided peace
of mind for customers and created
value for our shareholders. CBA’s
strategy is positioned to respond to
the opportunities and challenges over
the next decade. Our balance sheet
strength and operational resilience is in
place to support our customers and the
country when they need us most.

Paul O’Malley
Chair

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Our refreshed strategy elevates our
focus on supporting the national agenda,
prioritises investment in technology that
provides customers with better, faster
and safer banking, and invests in our
people and branches to better serve
our customers and communities.

History has also taught us that
sustained profitability which generates
organic capital underpins strong
investor support for the sector.
This enables banks to fund the country
and ensure an equitable approach
to rates for our depositors and
mortgage holders.

Overseeing non-financial risks
continues to be a key focus for the
Board. This includes understanding the
steps being taken to protect the Bank
and its customers from cyber security
threats and financial crime. This year
management walked the Board through
a real-life disruption scenario to help
us better understand the impacts to
customers and the Bank to inform our
risk tolerance and business continuity
plans for critical operations.

Our long-term remuneration is
designed to support a collective focus
on generating sustainable long-term
value and comply with requirements
under APRA Prudential Standard
CPS 511. Through the granting of
equity instruments with long vesting
periods, we reinforce the alignment
of Executives’ experience with that of
our shareholders. For example, when
shareholder returns are less favourable,
Executives share the impact in lower
vesting outcomes and a reduction in
the value of their long-term holdings,
although we acknowledge the outcome
for Executives can still be positive.

FINANCIAL REPORT

This year the Board has again spent
considerable time challenging our
strategy settings given our operating
context and the strategic risks that
affect our operations. We have met with
a broad range of stakeholders to provide
perspective and challenge our thinking
on risks and opportunities. These have
included customers, employees, policy
makers and energy companies.

The Bank continues to have a
well-developed approach to managing
financial risks. We also remain focused
on retaining the important progress we
have made in managing non-financial
risks while pursuing excellence in
customer service.

Learn more about our approach to
Board composition, renewal and skills
on pages 47–49.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Australia has had a fortunate 30 years
with our economy, living standards
and wealth growing faster than most
developed countries. More recently,
a sustained increase in the cost of
living and cost of running a business
has weighed on Australian households
and businesses. We have a collective
responsibility to help Australia
meet today’s challenges including
housing affordability, improved living
standards, inclusive growth and
energy supply. The economy needs
to remain fair, accessible, work for a
broad range of stakeholders and support
all Australians. It is essential for the next
generation to feel optimistic that they
can get ahead, have job prospects, buy
a home, and start or grow a business.

The resilience of the economy is
linked to the resilience of our financial
system. A strong banking system
is dependent on banks maintaining
strong balance sheets. The Board
fully supports management decisions
that strengthen our balance sheet
rather than temporarily maximise
short-term earnings.

Our annual review of the Risk
Management Declaration (RMD) saw us
continue our focus on financial crime,
cyber security and operational resilience.

The Board has designed our framework
to provide the right balance between
delivering shareholder returns,
customer outcomes and investing for
the future. Our frameworks enable us
to hold management to account and
not overweight any one factor.

During the year, the Board has
welcomed two new directors. In
October 2024, Kate Howitt was
appointed as an independent
Non-Executive Director. Kate brings
experience in strategy and stakeholder
management, particularly a strong
investor perspective. In March 2025,
Alistair Currie joined the Board.
Alistair has a strong background in
banking with executive experience in
institutional and business banking,
transaction banking, as well as
technology and operations.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Our purpose and refreshed strategy focuses us on
supporting Australia’s prosperity over the coming
decade. With continuing global uncertainty, our
long-term decision making and strong balance sheet
positions CBA to support our customers and the nation.

Global events and previous cycles
have taught us the value of having
funding flexibility, strong provisions,
appropriate capital levels and balance
sheet strength. Shocks are difficult
to foresee but can drive up funding
costs and result in a loss of confidence
in financial institutions. Economic
downturns put pressure on asset
quality, profitability and capital.

Maintaining strong governance, having
the right organisational culture and
instilling accountability is essential
for delivering good risk outcomes.
The Board establishes the strategic
objectives and risk appetite for the
Bank. This is informed by the Risk &
Compliance Committee and Audit
Committee programs of work, as well
as our operating environment.

Our skills matrix is regularly reviewed.
To get the long-term settings right,
the Board requires deep banking;
strategy; technology; regulation and
risk; customer; operational expertise
running large businesses; and financial
decision-making experience.

SUSTAINABILITY

Financial system resilience,
balance sheet strength and
organic capital generation

Managing our key risks

It is not uncommon to hear from
shareholders that they would like to
see a performance and remuneration
framework with greater weighting
given to financial measures, to
incentivise stronger alignment to
shareholder returns. We agree with
APRA that non-financial measures
are important. We recognise the risk
that a framework with more emphasis
on earnings may bias management
to make short-term decisions that
are not in the long-term interests of
shareholders, customers and the wider
Australian economy.

and opportunities, and facilitating
robust discussions remains a key focus.

CREATING VALUE

Learn more about our strategy
on pages 16–25.

Learn more about our financial
performance on pages 26–33.

The Board engages with the Executive
Leadership Team (ELT) regularly.
The People & Remuneration Committee
reviews Executives’ performance
through robust discussions to
determine remuneration outcomes.

OVERVIEW

With challenging domestic issues
and heightened geopolitical tensions,
it is more important than ever
that our financial institutions have
strong and resilient balance sheets
and take a long-term approach to
decision making.

Generating returns above the cost of
capital is a non-negotiable for banks
to be able to support services for all
Australians, the economy, invest in
the future and deliver sustainable
returns. We must remain vigilant in
delivering safe and exceptional banking
services efficiently.

OUR APPROACH TO CORPORATE GOVERNANCE

39
COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Our Board in action

The Board oversees the Bank’s corporate governance framework. The framework
is designed to enable sound and prudent management, provide leadership and
strategic guidance, and deliver the Bank’s purpose.

Board planning and agenda setting

So that the Board and Committees’ time is used efficiently, and discussions reflect the Bank’s priorities, agendas are reviewed
by the respective Chairs, in consultation with the Group Company Secretary and the CEO. Forward planners help provide a
comprehensive view of upcoming agendas, allowing for adjustments as priorities evolve. The Board retains flexibility for ad hoc
matters to be discussed at meetings where appropriate.

Board

Effective communication between management and the Board ahead of Board and Committee meetings is facilitated through
management input into certain agenda items and attending pre-meetings with Committee Chairs and the Group Company
Secretary. After Board and Committee meetings, follow up actions are shared to clarify the requests of management. To
promote effective decision making and a consistent approach to writing papers, Board and Committee paper templates are
available and training on writing papers is offered to preparers, throughout the year.

Audit
Committee

Risk & Compliance
Committee

Nominations
Committee

People &
Remuneration
Committee

Asset &
Liability
Committee

Financial Risk
Committee

Non-Financial
Risk
Committee

Risk &
Remuneration
Review
Committee

Board meetings

Strategy deep dives

The CEO and Executives provide regular reports to the Board.
In this way, the Board receives an update on the Group’s
performance and the performance of each Executive’s
business or support unit. Progress against strategy is a
key component of these reports. Standalone agenda items
relating to strategic matters are also discussed at each
multi-day Board meeting. The topics discussed throughout
the year include the Business Plan and reports on customers,
business performance, operating environment, acquisitions,
technology strategy and environmental strategy. The Board
and Committee Chairs are responsible for fostering open,
inclusive, and where appropriate, robust discussion and debate.

The Board held two strategy deep dives this year. These
were opportunities for the Board to reflect on the strategy,
consider the evolving external environment, review progress
against the Bank’s priorities and identify opportunities to do
more. This included breakout topic sessions for the Board,
as well as input from external subject matter experts. In
December, the Directors considered the Bank’s historical and
future operating context to inform our refreshed strategy.
The refreshed strategy, how we will measure success, key
enablers and execution priorities for next year were discussed
at the April strategy deep dive.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

The Board also considers the environmental and social (E&S) impact of the Bank’s activities and oversees management of
climate risk, within the Bank’s overall business strategy and risk appetite. The Board holds the CEO and ELT accountable for
delivering their E&S responsibilities. For example, ahead of review by the Board, the Financial Risk Committee reviewed the
results of the annual climate risk materiality assessment.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Oversight of material sustainability-related risks and opportunities

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Executive Leadership
Team

Our governance practices play an essential role in providing oversight of the Bank’s operations and contribute to the
development of our strategy. The Board is responsible for endorsing strategic and business unit plans to be implemented by
management. This includes approving financial plans, which incorporate strategic and other perspectives. The Board oversees
the strategy through Board meetings and strategy deep dives.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Management-level committees

Overseeing the delivery of our strategy

SUSTAINABILITY

The Board has established four standing Committees to assist it in carrying out its functions. Board Committee roles and
responsibilities are detailed in their respective charters. Each Committee makes recommendations to the Board in relation to
matters for which they are responsible. Each Board Committee also makes decisions on specific matters, where authorised by
the Board to do so.

CREATING VALUE

The Board’s responsibilities include setting strategic objectives and risk appetite, and expectations for the Group’s values and
desired culture, including approving our Code of Conduct.

This year the Board held 12 meetings, including six multi-day Board and Committee meetings with structured agendas,
six shorter Board meetings and two strategy deep dives, which were held as part of the multi-day Board meetings. Certain
CBA Directors also met with non-executive directors of Commonwealth Bank of Australia (Europe) N.V. and ASB, as well as
senior executives of CBA India. There is also engagement between Committee Chairs and their equivalent at Commonwealth
Securities Limited (CommSec).

Concurrent meeting considers risk, behaviours, values,
remuneration and performance outcomes for the CEO and Group
Executives 1 and the performance of the Group Auditor

The Audit Committee, People & Remuneration Committee and Risk & Compliance Committee meet six times per year, or more
frequently if necessary. The Nominations Committee meets at least four times per year, or more often as needed.
Typically Committees consider an agenda item in detail and make recommendations to the Board. In certain circumstances a
Committee will make a decision independently, such as the People & Remuneration Committee setting and reviewing the fee
framework for non-executive directors on certain CBA subsidiary boards.
Committee charters and forward planners guide the matters considered. This helps to facilitate effective and timely
information flows between Committees to assist the Board with clear oversight of matters through to resolution. After each
meeting, a report and recommendation paper is provided to the Board. The Chair of each Committee presents the report at the
next Board meeting. Minutes of meetings are available to all Directors.
Concurrent meetings of the four Board Committees occured twice this year to consider remuneration and performance
outcomes for Executives 1 and performance outcomes for the Group Auditor. Remuneration outcomes for the CEO and Group
Executives include the consideration of sustained performance across both financial and non-financial measures, including
shareholder, customer, people and leadership, and strategy execution. The concurrent meeting also considers relevant risk and
audit matters, and positive and negative risk behaviours, which may result in the upward or downward adjustment, including
a reduction to zero, of executive remuneration outcomes. Concurrent meetings occur when the People & Remuneration
Committee meets concurrently with the Nominations Committee, Risk & Compliance Committee and Audit Committee. The
concurrent meeting also facilitates information sharing on key matters to enable ongoing oversight of risks, behaviours, values,
remuneration and performance outcomes for Executives. 1

Operational resilience and CPS 230

Artificial Intelligence

regular reporting on the Group’s progress on the implementation
of CPS 230 including recommending business continuity plans
and associated tolerance levels for Board approval. The Board
also attended a focused education session on operational
resilience and CPS 230.

data, engineering productivity, financial crime, fraud
and scams, relationship management and operations.

with our purpose of building a brighter future for all.

Capital management

Approach to cyber security risk

The Board is responsible for approving capital management
initiatives. This year activities included approving the Bank’s
internal capital adequacy assessment process, which assesses
the Group’s current and future capital adequacy, and the
Group’s plan and processes to manage its capital. The Board
also approved changes to the Group Capital Policy and the
Capital Management of Subsidiaries and Branches Policy.

The Risk & Compliance Committee supported the Board by
reviewing the Bank’s risk-based approach to technology and
cyber security controls. This included considering the use of
risk scenarios to support the assessment of cyber security
risk exposure and associated remediation activities.

Economics Committee.

its CEO and co-founder addressed the Board on the evolution of AI.

To better understand customer needs, the Board met with customers in Adelaide across sectors such as
construction, building supplies, engineering, energy, retail and agriculture. These discussions offered valuable insights
into our customers’ business operations, challenges, growth opportunities and what their expectations may be of CBA.

Board renewal and orderly transitions are important for ensuring effective and
sustainable Board performance. The Board skills matrix and the CBA Board
Appointment, Renewal and Performance Policy frame the ongoing Board renewal
process. These work together so that prescribed skills and experience are present
within the Board and address the Bank’s existing and emerging strategic, business
and governance issues.
The Board remains focused on ongoing renewal and succession. Consideration is given
to diversity, background, experience and skills. There have been several changes to the
composition of the Board and certain Committees this year.

Board diversity

Kate Howitt was appointed as an independent Non-Executive Director from
1 October 2024. Kate has significant experience in matters relating to strategy,
capital allocation, stakeholder management and sustainability.
Alistair Currie was appointed as an independent Non-Executive Director from
31 March 2025. Alistair has extensive international banking experience, having held
roles in Australia, the United Kingdom, the United States, Asia and the Middle East.
On 16 October 2024, Anne Templeman-Jones retired from the Board as an
independent Non-Executive Director, Chair of the Audit Committee and member of
the Risk & Compliance Committee.
Following the retirement of Anne Templeman-Jones, Peter Harmer commenced as Chair
of the Audit Committee, member of the Risk & Compliance Committee and stepped
down from his role as a member of the People & Remuneration Committee. Lyn Cobley
was subsequently appointed as a member of the People & Remuneration Committee.

Board performance evaluation
Continuously monitoring and improving its performance, as well as that of its
Committees and individual Directors, remains important to the Board. Under its Charter,
the Board must annually assess its performance and the performance of individual
Directors. Additionally, an external performance review of the Board and Committees is
required at least every three years.
In 2024, an independent external evaluation assessed the performance of the Board, each
Committee and individual Directors. The process included observation of Board meetings,
and the completion of a survey and interviews with Directors, Executives, the Group
Auditor and the Group Company Secretary. It also evaluated the effectiveness of Board
and Committee processes, the appropriateness of meeting agendas, and the timeliness
and adequacy of papers. The findings were presented to the Board in August 2024.
An internal review was carried out in 2025. The process involved the completion of a
survey by Directors, Executives, the Group Auditor and the Group Company Secretary
covering matters such as roles and responsibilities, effectiveness, engagement and
reporting. A performance review report was prepared and provided to the Board.
The Chair also met with each of the participating Directors to share two-way feedback.

OUR APPROACH TO CORPORATE GOVERNANCE

Board education and training
The Nominations Committee is responsible for overseeing the Non-Executive Director induction program and appropriate
continuing education opportunities for Directors, as well as periodically reviewing whether there is a need for existing
Directors to undertake additional professional development.

Board skills and experience
The Matrix sets out the skills and experience considered essential to the effectiveness of the Board and its Committees.
It is used to guide the Board renewal process.
Each year the Directors, including the Chair, self-assess their individual skills and experience. Self-assessment ratings and
the Matrix are reviewed by the Nominations Committee, which makes recommendations for approval by the Board.
For more information on the Board’s climate-related training, skills and experience, see page 136.

This year new Non-Executive Directors, Kate Howitt and Alistair

Incorporated into the Board calendar to enable Directors to develop and
maintain skills and knowledge that support the Board’s decision making.
To upskill our Board, targeted education sessions were provided which
included presentations from external subject matter experts. Over the last
12 months this has covered topics such as Australia’s energy transition,
technology modernisation and CPS 230.
In addition, the Board participated in an educational trip to Asia to explore
topics relevant to CBA’s context. This is an important part of the Board’s
annual strategy calendar, serving as a source of new ideas and learning.

regulatory obligations.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Managing
our risks

Effective risk management enables us to fulfil
our purpose of building a brighter future for all.
Our embedded risk framework, together with
a strong culture, empowers our people to take
the right risks to deliver better outcomes for
our customers and communities.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT
