ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Risk management in action

FINANCIAL REPORT

In this section

60

MANAGING OUR RISKS

61

How we make decisions

The Board-approved risk appetite statement (RAS)
establishes the level of risk we must operate in to
deliver our strategy.

Risk policies and procedures
Our risk policies and procedures outline
the principles and practices to help us
to identify and assess our material risks.
They help us translate the RAS into our
daily business activities.

Our Risk Management
Framework enablers

Our RMF is comprised of systems,
structures, policies, processes and
people. They work together to help
identify, assess and mitigate our
internal and external sources of risk.

Risk
infrastructure
and data

Risk infrastructure and data
Our risk infrastructure provides the
systems, tools, models and data required
for the effective management of our
material risks.

Risk measurement
We use multiple techniques to identify and measure risks the Bank
is exposed to. These include stress testing and scenario analysis;
risk and control self-assessments; compliance self-assessments;
emerging risk assessment; systems and models to quantify risks
and identify risk exceptions; assurance of the RMF in the form of
Line 1 controls testing; Line 2 assurance reviews and framework
approvals; Line 3 internal audits; and the external audit program.

Stress testing
Stress testing and scenario analysis are important risk management
tools. They enable the Board and senior management to better
understand, quantify and manage risks. They also inform how we
may respond to risk events occurring and any potential weaknesses
in our actions during a crisis. The results of these stress tests
are important for strengthening our risk management approach.
Stress testing results also inform our risk appetite and assist
us in identifying the acceptable levels of risk we will assume
in our operations.

We implement measures to manage and control risks within
appetite such as: detective and preventative controls; limits;
delegated authorities and review and approval processes; policies
and procedures; and issue and incident management. The 3LoA
model organises our risk accountabilities by separating the roles
for managing the Bank’s risks.

For more information on the Bank’s RMF and risk types, see Note 9 on pages 303–344 in the Financial report.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Manage and control risks

FINANCIAL REPORT

These reviews of the RMF as well
as our annual RMD to APRA, set out
our plans to strengthen the RMF
in support of our strategy.

Risk
accountabilities
and skills

Risk
governance
and reporting

The Three Lines of Accountability model
(3LoA) organises our accountabilities to
manage the Bank’s risks by separating the
roles. Line 1 own and manage the risks
whereas Line 2 develop risk frameworks
and provide assurance. Line 3 provide
independent assurance over how
effectively risks are being managed. The
3LoA include skilled employees within each
line and are supported by risk capabilities,
and performance and remuneration
frameworks. Our remuneration
framework supports accountability by
incorporating risk assessment as gate
openers and modifiers in employee and
executive remuneration.

Our risk culture reflects our beliefs and behaviours within the Bank
and determines how we identify, measure, monitor and respond
to risks. These behaviours guide decision making and good risk
management, and embody our values and leadership principles.
This helps our actions align to our core beliefs. To sustain and
improve our risk culture, senior management and the Board form
a view of the Bank’s risk culture through an annual Board risk culture
assessment. This is supported by the Group risk culture response
plan, which addresses areas of focus identified by the Board.
It is further reinforced by self-reflections on business unit risk
culture to drive improvement at the team level.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

These material risks and our
approach to managing them are set
out in our RMF. The RMF is reviewed
annually by the ELT and the Board.
The RMF is also independently
reviewed every three years by an
external reviewer, supported by
internal and external audit programs.

Risk accountabilities and skills

Risk culture

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Risk policies
and
procedures

We categorise the material risks
the Bank is exposed to as strategic,
financial and non-financial risks.
Each material risk type has its own
specific framework or requirements
to manage the risk.

The Board has ultimate responsibility for the
Bank’s risk governance, including the RMF
and its implementation by management.
The Board is supported by its four
Committees as well as management-level
committees that oversee our material risks.
Management regularly provides information
to the Board which allows material risk
positions to be monitored against the RAS
and policy limits.

SUSTAINABILITY

The RMF enablers facilitate the effective
identification, measurement, monitoring
and response to our material risks.

Risk governance and reporting

The RAS is reviewed and approved annually by the Board in line with
our operating context, and is adapted for new and changing risks
present in our operations. The Board-approved RAS helps embed
a culture focused on disciplined risk management to enable smart
risk taking. The RAS also includes key risk indicators for our material
risks. This provides signals for building levels of risk and encourages
management to take action and avoid a breach of appetite.
In addition to governance of RAS performance through Board and
management-level committees, our Group policies, procedures,
delegations and limits translate and embed the RAS into our daily
business activities.

CREATING VALUE

Our Risk Management Framework
(RMF) outlines the Bank’s expectations
on how we identify, measure, monitor
and respond to our risks.

Risk appetite
OVERVIEW

Our Risk Management
Framework

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Risk management in action

62

MANAGING OUR RISKS

63

The Board has ultimate responsibility for the Bank’s risk governance,
including the RMF and oversight of its operation by management.

Board Risk & Compliance Committee

The Risk & Compliance Committee meets six times
per year, or more frequently if needed, with the Chair
of the Committee reporting to the Board after each
Committee meeting. The Committee also supports
the Board’s concurrent meetings. It provides updates
on relevant risk outcomes and matters at the concurrent
meetings to support the assessment of remuneration
outcomes for the CEO and Group Executives and
performance outcomes of the Group Auditor.

Chaired by: Chief Executive Officer

Meets: Weekly
or as required

The ELT is responsible for making specific
recommendations to the CEO and agreeing common
actions addressing strategy, business performance,
people leadership and culture, and risk and compliance
management and control. The ELT may establish
committees to assist it in carrying out its functions.

The ELT is accountable for developing and executing the
Bank’s strategy and supports the Board by advising on
strategic risks and opportunities, including those related
to technology. This year the ELT increased its focus on
accelerating the modernisation of the Bank’s technology
estate to strengthen technology stability, security and
operational continuity. Modernisation also supports the use
of new tools, technologies and AI, and delivery of enhanced
customer experiences. The ELT has set the strategic
direction, allocated the required capacity and capability, and
maintained oversight of progress against execution priorities.

Financial risks

Spotlight

Financial Risk Committee

Group Credit Risk Framework

Chaired by: Chief Risk Officer

Meets: Monthly

The Financial Risk Committee (FRC) assists and advises
the CEO on the governance, optimisation and effective
management of the Bank’s financial risks in accordance
with the RAS and RMF.

Asset and Liability Committee
Chaired by: Chief Financial Officer

Meets: Monthly

The Asset and Liability Committee (ALCO) oversees
the Group’s balance sheet settings and the effective
management of the Bank’s non-traded market
risk, interest rate risk, liquidity, funding, capital
and structural foreign exchange risk.

The FRC supports the Board by overseeing the Bank’s
financial risk frameworks with oversight of the governance,
key credit risk principles and policies that apply to the Group.
On a continuing basis during the year, various discussion
papers are submitted to the FRC for review in response
to emerging financial risks and changes to the credit
environment, including but not limited to, global geopolitical
developments that may impact the Bank and its customers.
The committee assesses whether to make adjustments to
our risk appetite and/or the Group Credit Risk Framework.

Spotlight

Each year the Board makes a RMD to APRA to meet the requirements of APRA’s CPS 220, confirming
the RMF remains fit for managing our material risks, or outlines steps to strengthen the RMF. Lines 1 and
2, with an opinion from Line 3 and collective agreement by the ELT, form a recommendation on the RMD
to the Board Risk & Compliance Committee. This supports the Directors to determine whether the RMF
is adequately designed and operating effectively in all material respects. This year particular attention
was given to protecting our customers through strengthening our RMF to help reduce fraud, scams, cyber
threats, financial crime, and business disruption and to support the Bank’s responsible use of AI. This focus
reflects the evolving threat landscape and reinforces our commitment to strengthening the resilience
of our organisation to deliver our strategy.

Non-Financial Risk Committee

Operational resilience and CPS 230

Chaired by: Chief Executive Officer Meets: Bi-monthly
The Non-Financial Risk Committee (NFRC) advises
the CEO on the governance, optimisation and effective
management of the Bank’s non-financial risks in
accordance with the RAS and RMF.

Risk & Remuneration Review Committee
Chaired by: Chief Risk Officer

Meets: Quarterly

The Group Risk & Remuneration Review Committee advises
the Group Chief Risk Officer on risk considerations for
determining remuneration outcomes in line with the Group
Remuneration Policy. This assists the Group Chief Risk
Officer to support the Chief Executive Officer and Board
to determine remuneration outcomes across the Bank.

The NFRC discussed requirements of CPS 230, including
Board and management accountabilities, and plans to
achieve compliance. Leading up to the effective date
of 1 July 2025, the Committee reviewed the Business
Continuity Plan summaries, including associated
tolerances of the Bank’s critical operations ahead of review
by the Board Risk & Compliance Committee.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Non-financial risks

FINANCIAL REPORT

Risk Management Declaration

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

It is important for directors to spend the right amount
of time on the right issues. This year the Committee’s
focus remained on the management of financial
risks such as funding and liquidity, and non-financial
and strategic risks, including cyber security, fraud

and scams, operational resilience, privacy, data,
environmental and social, artificial intelligence and
financial crime compliance.

Technology modernisation

Executive Leadership Team

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

The Board Risk & Compliance Committee reviews
and oversees the RMF and adherence to the Bank’s
Board-approved risk appetite. The Committee monitors
our risk profile against changes in our operating context.
Management provides regular reports to the Committee
to provide oversight of current and emerging risks, the
effectiveness of our control environment and our risk
management practices.

Spotlight

SUSTAINABILITY

For more information on our corporate governance structure and committees, see page 38.

Strategic risks

CREATING VALUE

Committees assist the Board to fulfil its roles and responsibilities. Committees meet to consider and discuss
specific matters in detail and, where appropriate, make recommendations to the Board for resolution. Processes
are in place to support effective and timely information flows between Committees and provide the Board with
clear oversight of matters.

At management level, risk governance is undertaken by a structured hierarchy of individual accountabilities, personal delegations,
management committees and forums across the Bank. Management regularly monitors financial, strategic and non-financial
risk exposures against the Bank’s RAS and policy limits. This information is provided to the Board Committees in the form
of Board papers, which are prepared and shared with the Board Committees on a bi-monthly basis. Following management
committee meetings, papers are shared with Board Committees for noting, approval or discussion. This flow of information
is essential for management committees to effectively support the Board.

OVERVIEW

The Board is supported by the Risk & Compliance Committee, Audit Committee, Nominations Committee and
People & Remuneration Committee, as well as specific management-level committees that oversee material
risks. Each year the Board assesses the maturity of our RMF in managing our material risks through our RMD,
and adjusts the RMF as required. Together with the setting of risk appetite, this helps the Bank maintain a risk
management framework that is fit for our business size, operating context and delivery of our strategic priorities.
Our RMD to APRA allows the Board to reflect on the framework’s design and operating effectiveness and adjust,
where needed.

MANAGING OUR RISKS

65

While remaining vigilant to the risks we need to manage today, we also
look ahead to identify potential future challenges. We aim to uphold the
risk management standards expected by our customers, communities,
regulators and shareholders, both now and in the future.

Our material risks are those the Bank places extra focus on to mitigate,
as they could significantly impact the Bank, our customers, communities
and shareholders in the near term.

Risk context

Financial risk
Risk type:

The emerging risk themes the Board and ELT focused on this year are outlined below.
Material themes:

Geopolitical conflict and
macroeconomic uncertainty

Regulatory reforms

Australia’s economy needs affordable and reliable energy.
Replacing coal-fired power with firmed renewable energy
is crucial to meet Australia’s renewable energy targets. With
90% of coal-fired power stations expected to retire by 2035,
delays in replacement generation could increase electricity
price volatility, affect reliability and have broader economic
impacts. Credit risks could also be heightened for the
Bank, as well as disruptions to our operations. We continue
to engage with Government on the energy transition and
support retail and business customers finance existing
technologies, such as rooftop solar, batteries and electric
vehicles to help reduce their emissions. This also helps
reduce reliance on large-scale utility investments.

Cyber security threats

our customers place in us to
management

• We continue to improve the Bank’s privacy processes across
all jurisdictions in which we operate.

Risk type:

• Policies and standards are in place to manage customers’
personal information, according to our privacy obligations.

The cyber security risk
environment for Australian
financial services is complex due
to the availability of affordable
and user-friendly attack tools,
marketplaces for stolen and
compromised credentials,
and the speed with which
vulnerabilities are exploited.

Growing societal pressures impacting our
customers and communities
The Bank continues to face heightened expectations from
governments, regulators and the community to respond to
key challenges including cost-of-living pressures, housing
affordability and crisis support. Many Australians are feeling
the impact of rising fraud and scams activity, as well as
ongoing affordability concerns. We remain focused on
supporting our customers through these challenges and
continue to invest in strengthening our fraud and scams
infrastructure to help keep them safe.

Learn more about our approach to financial risk management in Note 9
on pages 303–344 in the Financial report.

• We continue to invest in enterprise data platforms to manage
the quality of our data, and retain and dispose of data.
Learn more about our approach to cyber, privacy and data management
on pages 89–91.

• Cyber security is an ever-evolving field, requiring constant
improvement and adaptation.
• We invest in our capability to prevent, detect and respond
quickly to identified threats. This effort extends across our
various cyber control domains to safeguard customer data
and enable secure transactions.
• Additionally, we play a role in supporting the national security
ecosystem. Our goal is to improve system-wide resilience
by securing infrastructure, collaborating with industry and
the National Office of Cyber Security, and participating in
the Federal Government’s Executive Cyber Council. Our
participation in industry exercises aims to test system-wide
resilience and response capabilities.
Learn more about our approach to cyber, privacy and data management
on pages 89–91.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

The Bank anticipates cyber attacks to persist and grow
as adversaries use new technologies such as GenAI to
exploit vulnerabilities, including third parties and cloud
environments. These could lead to compromised customer
data, critical systems disruptions and potential breaches of
our obligations. We continue to strengthen our cyber security
risk management through dedicated teams that monitor
and respond to potential cyber security threats, applying
learnings from the Board and senior management crisis
management exercises, and a focus on enhancing our cyber
security control environment to address emerging threats.

• We maintain a conservative balance sheet with a high
proportion of funding from customer deposits and excess
liquidity. We perform stress tests to prepare for a range of
economic scenarios.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Innovation driven by emerging and disruptive technology,
including AI, continues to rapidly evolve. As we pursue
our strategy, which includes a focus on technology and
AI, we continue working hard to effectively manage risks
from change implementation that could lead to service
disruptions, poor customer experiences, fraud and scams,
conduct issues, cyber security threats, and data and privacy
issues. As a Bank, we invest in governance and risk initiatives
to support our AI goals, including for responsible AI.

• We regularly assess credit settings, pricing and customer credit
profiles in light of changing and emerging risks. Our loan loss
provisions are carefully managed to support the Bank to manage
this risk appropriately.

Emerging technology

Energy transition in Australia

Macroeconomic pressures and
the rising cost of living could
negatively impact financial
risk. A slowdown in economic
growth could lead to market
volatility, higher unemployment,
and an increase in borrowers
unable to meet their financial
commitments with the Bank.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Our operations face risks from global political events, such
as elections and policy changes, civil unrest, international
competition and conflicts. These disruptions can increase
social pressures domestically; affect unemployment,
housing supply, and market volatility; and escalate credit and
operational risks for financial institutions. Failure to prepare
for these geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties
could heighten our exposure to various financial and
non-financial risks, which we monitor and manage through
our RMF.

Upcoming regulatory reforms could significantly impact
us and our customers, spanning areas like Anti-Money
Laundering/Counter-Terrorism Financing (AML/CTF), cyber
security, scams, privacy, payments licensing, AI and climate
disclosures. To prepare for new regulation, we frequently
evaluate our obligations including those under the new Scam
Prevention Framework Act 2025 (Cth), which will be further
supported by industry specific codes, not yet released. The
Bank is also improving its security infrastructure for fraud
and scams, and forming partnerships to better handle scam
phishing and reporting within the industry ecosystem.

Our response

SUSTAINABILITY

Our emerging risk profile is updated annually through a review process with the ELT and the Board. This process aligns with
the annual review of the Bank’s strategy. The emerging risk process evaluates the adequacy of the Bank’s mitigation strategies
to prevent emerging risks from materially impacting the Bank. In cases where we may have limited ability to influence
emerging risks, it may be more challenging to implement effective mitigative actions. This may require us to take additional
steps to address these risks.

For more information, including a full list of our material risks, see Note 9 on pages 303–344 in the Financial report.

CREATING VALUE

Emerging risks are risks that are either newly developing or already exist, but their effects have not yet been substantially
realised. These risks are often constantly evolving and have the potential to impact the Bank and our customers in the
medium to long term (over 12 months). It is essential to respond to these risks now to minimise their potential future impact.
Emerging risks are often driven by new trends in our operating environment, such as competition, new technologies, regulation
or evolving customer expectations.

The Bank faces various risks through the products and services we offer. We categorise these risks as strategic, financial and
non-financial based on the nature of their impacts. Each year the Board approves the material ‘Level 1’ risk types in the RMF,
which establishes the risks needing immediate management and control. The material risks outlined below have been our
primary focus this year.

Digitisation,

Understanding this year’s material themes

Stakeholders reaffirmed that a supported and engaged
workforce is essential to CBA’s value creation. CBA plays a
key role in shaping employee engagement and wellbeing,
and fostering a workplace culture that is safe, respectful
and inclusive. As digitisation and AI reshape the workforce,
the need for reskilling and upskilling is growing, though
stakeholders also noted people skills remain vital for
customer-facing roles.

With the shift to digital,
stakeholders expect us to
consider potential adverse
impacts, such as bias and job
losses on our people, customers
and the broader community.

Digitisation, innovation and AI
can significantly enhance value
creation and financial performance.
Responsible use of AI can drive
operational efficiency and enable
secure, personalised banking.

Climate change is impacting the
frequency and scale of natural
disasters. The physical and
transition impacts of climate
change can affect our customers,
communities and the nation.

Our exposure to climate-related risks
and opportunities primarily comes
from our lending to customers.
Compared to other material topics, the
financial impact of climate change is
less immediate and significant for CBA,
and is not deemed to be financially
material to the Bank.

89–91

I

The new Australian Sustainability Reporting Standards
present an opportunity for us to enhance how we
address material sustainability issues, while improving
the transparency, consistency and comparability of our
sustainability disclosures over time.
Our report is structured around material themes that
reflect our most significant sustainability topics for the year.
The theme ‘Banking strategy, operations and operating
context’ is not presented as a standalone material theme.
While core to the Bank’s strategy and financial performance,
we have not classified it as a sustainability topic and it is not
expected to be assessed as a separate sustainability theme
in future years. Given the theme is inherently material to
the Bank, our approach and activities are disclosed in other
sections of this report.
For more information on material topics related to our material
themes, see pages 74–93.
For more information on how our material themes are mapped
to material risks, see pages 65–67.

Significant judgement was applied to determine material topics and themes.

Key
I

Impact materiality: Related topics considered material from an impact perspective, where stakeholders identified topics that have potential to
significantly impact the Australian economy, environment and people. Related topics for each theme are detailed on subsequent chapter pages.
Financial materiality: Related topics considered material from a financial perspective, where CBA has identified topics that have potential to
impact CBA’s ability to create value in the short to medium term (1-5 years), using the Bank’s 5x5 risk matrix. Related topics for each theme are
detailed on subsequent chapter pages.

continue to be top priorities for both stakeholders and
CBA. This year underlying topics such as fraud and scams,
cost of living, payments, customer experience and housing
affordability gained importance. Access to banking also
rose in importance, driven by an increased focus on cash
and branch availability in regional Australia.

internal subject matter experts.

Financial materiality

CREATING VALUE

The assessment considers CBA’s operating
environment and uses insights from our stakeholder
engagement. Internal subject matter experts,
representing key stakeholder groups, assess proposed
topics based on their direct engagement with
stakeholders. Each topic is evaluated for its relevance
to Australia’s economy, environment and people, and
CBA’s impact on or ability to influence the topic (impact
materiality), as well as the topic’s potential impact on
CBA’s financial performance (financial materiality).

experience,

impact

Impact materiality

OVERVIEW

Our annual materiality assessment seeks to
identify sustainability topics that are most relevant
to our stakeholders and CBA. Insights from the
assessment are shared across the Bank and with
the Board, to help inform decision making and our
sustainability disclosures.

We want to help continuously improve living standards for Australians and are
committed to building the financial capability and wellbeing of our customers and
communities. Our Financial Fitness program, focused on money management,
continues to grow through new content and expanded delivery across multiple
channels. Practical resources are available on the CommBank website and through
branch seminars for our customers, communities and our people.
To improve customer outcomes, particularly in vulnerability, inclusivity and
accessibility, we implemented changes in line with updates to the Banking Code
of Practice and other industry guidelines. We also continue to engage the CBA
Community Council to understand the lived experience of our diverse customers
to help inform fairer customer outcomes in product, service and process design.

We currently hold the #1 NPS ranking across major banks in the Consumer, Business and Digital categories, and have held this
ranking across all for at least 11 months. This year our Institutional NPS ranking has dropped to #2 after previously holding the
#1 ranking for 24 consecutive months. There is still more work to do to improve our NPS scores. Detailed reports on the drivers of
NPS can be accessed by our people to understand what is influencing the absolute scores. This change highlights the importance
of focusing on continuously improving our service and prioritising the customer in everything we do.

Supporting customers in severe weather events

Saving and spending tools

commbank.com.au/supportforyou

Cost of living hub

commbank.com.au/costofliving

Financial hardship and support options

commbank.com.au/hardship

Using customer feedback and internal data, we developed the Disaster Resilience Toolkit. The toolkit introduces a suite
of tools and resources to help support our customers before, during and after a weather-related event. We also take
steps to avoid adding unnecessary stress. For example, our Business Banking team temporarily removed automated late
payment reminders for around 14,000 customers in communities affected by natural disasters this year. We also donated
$100,000 to Rural Aid to fund the delivery of critical resources and services to more rural Australian communities
impacted by severe weather events.
See more about emergency and disaster assistance at commbank.com.au/naturaldisasters

• Fraud and scams

More recently, we have passed on interest rate cuts in full to provide relief to our
variable rate home loan customers. Home loan customers may also change their
mortgage direct debits and make adjustments aligned with their circumstances via
the CommBank app or NetBank.

Measuring customer experience

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Most relevant risks

Ongoing cost-of-living challenges continue to put pressure on many of our
customers and communities. We continue to support our customers with a range
of options including improved access to hardship assistance through NetBank and
the CommBank app. For those most in need of support, we offer tailored payment
arrangements with interest-only payments and repayment deferrals, if needed.
Our hardship and extra care teams are trained to provide tailored and empathetic
support to our customers.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

• Customer experience

We are also exploring ways to gain deeper customer insights. We have now
entered our second year of a program that to-date has observed financial
behaviours and decisions inside the homes of over 70 young adult Australians
across 10 locations. Early insights indicate young adults often rely on non-bank
tools to manage finances, revealing unmet needs with their primary bank.

Feedback from customers, particularly
those unable to visit a branch, also
led us to digitise the lodgement
process for Power Of Attorneys and
Financial Management Orders. This
inclusive shift has improved efficiency
and convenience, with over 90% of
lodgements now made online.

SUSTAINABILITY

• Cost of living

Supporting in all the ways we can

We are prioritising key experiences across consumer and small business
banking and are embedding a new approach to collecting direct customer
feedback. This approach seeks to provide a more complete and consistent
understanding of customer experiences, helping us better focus on
outcomes for our customers.

In response to customer dissatisfaction
around fraudulent transactions, we
have increased fraud alerts to 35,000
per day and integrated them with a
self-service tool. We now identify the
majority of fraudulent transactions and
enable customers to lodge disputes in a
matter of minutes without the need to
call or message us or go to a branch.

CREATING VALUE

• Access to banking

We use customer experience surveys to capture feedback, monitor
trends and understand how customers engage with us. We also monitor
operational metrics, including complaints, disputes, wait times and
service availability, providing useful indicators of areas requiring attention.
These metrics together with the Net Promoter Score (NPS), an external
measure of customer advocacy, give us a benchmark to improve on.

Our customers expect simple, seamless
experiences. While we aim to get things
right every time, when things go wrong,
how we respond matters. Feedback and
complaints are valuable opportunities to
listen, learn and improve.

Customer experience,
support and
community impact

76

OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY

77

As Australia’s largest bank, it is important that we provide tailored and accessible solutions
to promote the financial inclusion of our customers and communities.

Improving access to banking
We aim to provide products and services that meet the diverse needs of our customers and provide equitable access to help
them achieve their financial goals. We recognise there are systemic barriers to banking and financial inclusion experienced by
some members of the community, and we are committed to understanding and removing these barriers so more Australians
can access banking confidently, independently and with dignity.
We are fortunate to have one in two new migrants to Australia trust us with their banking needs and recognise the unique
challenges they face. This year we updated and improved our Moving to Australia online resources, which provide practical
guidance on opening bank accounts, managing money and accessing digital tools – designed to support a confident start
to life in Australia.
To better support our customers who hold eligible government concession cards, we offer a basic bank account, our
Streamline Basic account, which has no monthly account keeping, overdraw or dishonour fees.
Improving financial inclusion for First Nations customers and communities remains a priority. Alongside our branch teams and
Indigenous Customer Assistance Line, Indigenous Business Banking team, and Indigenous banking concierge offer support
tailored to community needs.

To further strengthen account protection, we introduced a new multi-factor
authentication process for NetBank, requiring customers to confirm login
attempts via the app. This added security layer helps prevent unauthorised
access, even in cases where a user’s password has been comprised.

We are also focused on building strong financial awareness and habits in children. Kit, our pocket money app for kids, used
by over 100,000 customers, helps improve financial capability through fun and interactive learning. This year we introduced
linking between interest-earning CommBank Youthsaver accounts and Kit, allowing kids to transfer and see their money grow.
We also offer Minor Trust accounts through CommSec, allowing parents to hold investments on behalf of their children to help
build their financial future.

For more information on cyber, privacy and data management, see pages 89–91.

Have regular conversations about
scams with family and friends.
Stay informed about the latest
scam tactics.
Remember to implement and
educate others on three simple
steps to help prevent scams:

2. Check: Ask someone you trust
or contact the organisation the
message claims to be from.

if you suspect a scam.

scam-tips

We also aim to help more regional Australians stay safe online.
We offered regional Australians access to more than 600 ‘Staying Safe
Online’ education sessions this year. By partnering with local community
groups to expand accessibility to the program, we delivered more than
300 of these free sessions in community centres in regional towns
allowing more people to attend, no matter who they bank with.

Recognising and supporting customers with hidden disabilities
As part of the Bank’s ongoing commitment to deliver more inclusive products and experiences, we joined the Hidden
Disabilities Sunflower program. A discreet symbol, worn as a pin, lanyard or wristband, allows people to voluntarily share with
others that they have a disability or condition that may not be immediately apparent, and that they may need extra support.
We provide training to our retail frontline teams to help them recognise and support customers with a hidden disability or
non-visible condition, and we encourage our customers to wear the symbol if they feel comfortable to do so. The Hidden
Disabilities Sunflower program complements our Equal Access Toolkit, used in all CBA branches and includes practical tools to
support customers who may need an adjusted experience to complete a task in branch independently. We have also recently
launched a new webpage for our neurodivergent customers.
See more about neuroinclusive banking at commbank.com.au/neuroinclusivebanking

the phone number. Change your

may have it, and make sure you
pick something long and unique.

To deepen our relationship with regional customers and communities we
continued our regional growth initiative. This initiative connects business
bankers with our branch network to support regional advocacy, customer
engagement and brand awareness. CBA also sponsored the National Bush
Summit, a national campaign advocating for regional and rural Australia,
addressing important issues like cost of living, fraud and scams, and the
future of farming and agriculture.

We continue to support regional Australia with over 40% of our branch
network located in regional areas. We maintained the biggest branch
network in regional Australia and extended the CBA-branded regional branch
closure moratorium until the end of July 2027. Our Bank@Post partnership
with Australia Post also helps our customers, especially those in rural and
regional areas, to continue having access to face-to-face banking services.
There are more than 3,300 Bank@Post outlets across Australia, including
in over 1,100 communities where there is no bank branch of any kind.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Research commissioned by CBA revealed that while 90% of Australians
believe that talking about scams with loved ones can raise awareness,
only 8% felt comfortable having a discussion with family about being
scammed. To help bridge this gap, CBA launched a new education
campaign that aims to encourage conversations about the dangers of
scams between generations. By addressing intergenerational knowledge
gaps, it seeks to empower all Australians, particularly older generations,
with the information and tools needed to help them stay safe.

How can I help?

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

We actively engage with customers to provide information about
evolving fraud and scams threats. This year the Bank reached out to
customers through NetBank, the CommBank app, news articles, social
media, in-branch community sessions and campaigns.

Continuing to support our regional communities

SUSTAINABILITY

We also introduced a feature in the CommBank app that allows customers
to see all digital wallets their payment details have been linked to and
remove any they do not recognise. More than 1.3 million customers
have used this feature since launch in August 2024. We have seen a
30% reduction in customer scam losses year-on-year supported by
initiatives including behavioural security, NameCheck, CallerCheck and
CustomerCheck.

CREATING VALUE

More customers than ever are choosing to engage with us through the
CommBank app. We are balancing transaction speed with the need for
a streamlined, safe and secure digital payment experience. New warnings
for first-time payments through NetBank and the CommBank app now
provide enhanced alerts for customers. Increased alerting helped to
reduce customer-reported fraud by 40% over the year.

OVERVIEW

Fraud, scams, cyber threats and financial crime
continue to impact many Australians. We have more
work to do and remain focused on investing to help
protect our customers and communities.

Enabling First Nations economic aspirations

We are committed to supporting our communities as we seek to build a brighter future for all.

Helping address financial abuse

Supporting community organisations

$4m

We want to improve access to basic banking services for remote First Nations customers and communities to support
their financial inclusion. Our teams, supported by First Nations employees, work with local organisations to share
the program’s purpose, better understand community needs and offer banking support. Since the program’s launch
in March 2024, we have supported over 2,300 customers across 22 locations covering 32 remote communities in
Australia, including Wurrumiyanga in the Tiwi Islands, and Pormpuraaw and Kowanyama in the Cape York Peninsula.
During our visits, our people help open accounts, including for children under the age of 14, help restore access to
banking services, resolve identification issues, and assist with fraud and other enquiries.
See more about our First Nations Reach Program at commbank.com.au/firstnations

We continue to support the Growing Football
Fund in partnership with Football Australia.
This year an additional 118 community clubs
and associations were awarded grants to fund
initiatives aimed at driving women and girls’
participation in grassroots football. The Growing
Football Fund is on track to achieve its ambition of
supporting the attraction and retention of 50,000
female participants by 2026, with record growth

focused on upskilling community coaches. This
year we extended and expanded our partnership

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

This year we awarded 175 community grants to community organisations
across Australia, doubling the grant value to $20,000 from $10,000 per grant
in previous years. Five regional grant recipients were each awarded an additional
$100,000 to amplify their impact in the community. One of these recipients was
Farm Angels. They were awarded a total of $120,000 towards their mission to
support Australian farmers by providing vital relief and hope during challenging
times, aiming to improve mental health outcomes in rural areas.

First Nations Reach Program

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Through the CommBank Staff Foundation, volunteering and other charitable
and employee giving initiatives, we have provided support to hundreds of
community organisations. CBA supports workplace giving and contributed
$2 million to the CommBank Staff Foundation, matching all donations from
our people. The Foundation gives our people the opportunity to show their
support to long-standing charitable partnerships including Can4Cancer, where
we had over 8,400 participants in 2024 raising funds for cancer research. Our
people are also empowered to support organisations they care about through
our annual community grants program. The grants program helps relieve cost
pressures for community organisations such as those supporting mental health,
victim-survivors of domestic violence, children’s health, migrants and refugees,
and more.

See more about our FY26-28 Reconciliation Action Plan at commbank.com.au/reconciliation
SUSTAINABILITY

We support selected not-for-profit organisations delivering
innovative responses to financial abuse recovery through our
Next Chapter Innovation program. This year we launched
the second cohort of the Next Chapter Innovation program,
specifically focused on helping to address financial abuse
in First Nations communities. We have provided more than
$1 million to date through Next Chapter Innovation together
with a range of non-financial support, including executive
support and mentoring, to help accelerate innovative
responses to financial abuse.

Since 2008, we have improved our understanding of the role we can play in
reconciliation. We recognise that initiatives are more effective and impactful
when First Nations peoples are leading solutions and are involved in the design
and delivery of initiatives. We have worked with our Indigenous Leadership
Team and Indigenous Advisory Council to develop a set of principles that
aim to strengthen our engagement with First Nations peoples. Over the next
three years, we will work to apply these principles to the way we deliver our
reconciliation priorities as set out in the RAP.

CREATING VALUE

The Financial Independence Hub (FIH) delivered by Good
Shepherd Australia New Zealand and funded by CBA,
continues to provide free, confidential and ongoing support
for people who have experienced financial abuse, regardless
of who they bank with. Since inception in 2020, the FIH

has supported 12,589 participants to help them feel more
confident with money, regain control over their finances and
plan for the future. We have reaffirmed our partnership with
Good Shepherd Australia New Zealand from July 2024 until
June 2028 to continue supporting FIH participants on their
road to long-term financial independence.

OVERVIEW

Our Next Chapter program is our long-term commitment
to help address domestic and family violence (DFV) and
financial abuse in Australia. Our Next Chapter team
continues to provide free and confidential support to victim
survivors of DFV and financial abuse, no matter who they
bank with. This year the Next Chapter team provided
support through over 18,500 individual interactions.
We also continued to block transactions due to abusive
language, stopping over 320,000 transactions this year.
Our AI model continues to identify over 3,600 cases each
year which are further reviewed by our Next Chapter team.

The Bank has a longstanding commitment to reconciliation, having launched
our first Reconciliation Action Plan (RAP) in 2008. Through the RAP program,
Reconciliation Australia supports organisations to take meaningful action
to contribute to reconciliation. Through our FY26-28 RAP, we seek to be a
trusted partner to First Nations peoples as they achieve their social, cultural
and economic aspirations. Our RAP focuses on strengthening relationships
with First Nations communities, providing meaningful career opportunities,
supporting First Nations business growth, and improving access to banking
products and services. In recognition of our contribution to reconciliation, our
FY26-28 RAP received Elevate status from Reconciliation Australia.

We are always looking for ways to enhance our people’s experience at work and
understand how we help our people deliver the greatest impact for our customers,

through various channels and forums, including employee research panels, focus
groups and surveys. Our people and culture surveys include an annual culture

receive insights about culture, employee experience and the core behaviours to
deliver better customer outcomes. In our most recent survey, people engagement
was 85%, an increase from last year’s result of 84% in May 2024.

Attracting talent and developing key pipelines

Reflecting the customers and communities we serve

To help us deliver our strategy we continue to prioritise talent attraction and retention, particularly for critical skills in
technology. We are committed to creating a culture of continuous learning so that our people can continue to grow and
develop, and we remain a workplace of choice for key talent. To attract and retain the right people, we are differentiating
our experience by providing personalised progression with pathways to upskill, reskill and grow careers.

We recognise the value in diversity of thought and having a team that reflects the customers and communities we serve. We want
to create an environment where everyone feels included, safe and respected at work. As we work towards this, we are focused
on initiatives which strengthen inclusion and prevent behaviours and norms that can normalise disrespect and lead to inequality.
Our business unit inclusion and diversity councils and seven employee diversity networks support the execution of our strategy
by providing feedback and lived experience.

Fostering employee wellbeing
We are committed to providing a positive and supportive environment where our people feel safe
and respected at work. Our protect, care and thrive model helps guide our decision making.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Upskilling and reskilling our people

640+

See more about how we are reducing our gender pay gap in our CBA Employer Statement.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

We continue to invest in community skilling that helps build
strong, diverse talent pipelines for the Bank and Australia. We
continued our partnership with Year 13, which offers free and
easily accessible programs for young Australians. This year we
launched a career tools program on human skills that are resilient to
technological advancements and will become even more important
for employability. With the pace of technological change and AI
advancement, the program aims to help high school students
identify the skills they have, learn how to articulate those key skills
and build on them.

Upskilling our people

SUSTAINABILITY

Our ‘AI for All’ and ‘AI Deep Dive’ micro learning series continues to help
our people learn what AI is and how to use it responsibly. There have
been over 100,000 module engagements on AI this year, with 98%
of survey respondents saying they have a better understanding of AI.
However, human skills such as leadership, empathy, good judgement
and smart risk taking remain key focus areas in our skilling strategy.

We are working towards achieving 47-50% gender equality in Executive Manager and above roles, having reached 45.1%
this year. Our goal of 3% Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander representation in our domestic workforce remains a focus,
with current representation at 1.4%. We are also aiming for 40% cultural diversity in Executive Manager and above roles,
with representation at 39.0% this year.
The Bank remains committed to improving gender equality outcomes. We continue to see a lower proportion of women
in senior and higher-paying technical specialist roles, which is reflected in our Workplace Gender Equality Agency (WGEA)
median gender pay gap of 26.8% and average gender pay gap of 21.3% as at 31 March 2025. We will continue to work
to reduce our gender pay gap across the Bank and continue taking action to accelerate progress.

Investing in future skills
Technology and customer expectations continue to reshape our ways
of working. We want our people to learn, build and apply the skills
that prepare us for the future of work. We continue to empower our
people with scaled access to the latest AI products, including more
than 17,500 of our people granted access to Copilot. With more of
our people now accessing AI tools in their role, it is important that we
help them to use the technology responsibly and effectively to drive
better customer outcomes. Our approach to upskilling and reskilling
remains focused on the technical, behavioural and leadership skills
required to deliver our strategy and manage risk.

This year we reviewed our 2025-2028 Diversity, Equity and Inclusion strategy. We reset and aligned our diversity goals across
gender equality, Indigenous workforce representation and cultural diversity. While we have made progress, there is more to do.
We have set 2028 as the target date for these refreshed goals.

CREATING VALUE

We are focused on developing talent pipelines, including through our graduate program and our career comeback program.
This year we welcomed 350 graduates into the program with over 200 in technology and have expanded our career comeback
program into engineering and financial crime. Since 2021, 43 individuals have been placed in our career comeback program
with 97% offered employment upon completion. Building awareness and early engagement with students remains a focus,
sponsoring community initiatives targeting potential talent through Girls in Tech, She Codes, Day of AI, ABCN Tech Programs,
Vogue Codes and CSIRO Young Indigenous Women in STEM programs. Recognising the importance of diverse talent pipelines,
we continue to explore alternative pathways to secure entry-level technology talent. We have adopted the T-EDI Standards, a
data-driven set of principles to support inclusion in technology. We have also made a commitment with the NSW Digital Skills
and Workforce Compact to hire 20% of entry-level technology talent through alternate pathways by 2030.

Given the significance of environmental, social and governance issues, the
Board has responsibility for overseeing efforts to improve the experience and
outcomes of CBA customers, monitoring our culture and considering the material
environmental and social impacts of our activities. The Board oversees the Group’s
Risk Management Framework (RMF) and approves the risk appetite statement
(RAS), which embeds a culture focused on disciplined risk management. The Board
also approves a range of key Group policies and frameworks. Some of these include
the Remuneration Policy, Code of Conduct, Diversity Equity and Inclusion Policy,
E&S Policy and Framework, and Work, Health and Safety policies.
Our frameworks help embed our policies and procedures across the organisation.
All employees undertake compulsory annual training relevant to their role.
This supports business decisions and fairer customer and community outcomes.

Effective governance is essential for delivering our strategy and helps drive better
outcomes for customers, shareholders and financial system stability. Since the
2018 APRA Prudential Inquiry we have focused on our governance, culture and
accountability. We continue to strengthen policies, systems and processes to
deliver improved outcomes for stakeholders.

Providing responsible products
and services

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

• Modern slavery

Managing our business responsibly

See our Code of Conduct at commbank.com.au/policies

SUSTAINABILITY

• Business ethics and conduct

As we are committed to learning and getting better every day, we updated our Code of Conduct this year. The changes reflect
what we have learned from recent experiences where we did not get the right outcome for our customers. Where expectations
have changed and customers have been impacted, we may go back and undertake reviews; where we did not get the right
outcome for our customers, we will remediate to make things right. The code is designed to help strengthen both how we meet
our legal and regulatory obligations, and how we act ethically and fairly. It has been updated to explain the importance of first
asking ‘Must We?’ and ‘Can We?’ to support compliance with our legal and regulatory obligations, before asking ‘Should We?’.

CREATING VALUE

Related material topics

One in three Australians and one in four businesses trust us as their main financial institution. Caring for our customers,
communities and one another, having the courage to lead by example, and demonstrating our commitment to doing what is right
is how we earn trust. We introduced our Code of Conduct in 2018 to guide our decision making in every situation and embed our
values across the whole organisation. Since then, it has become an essential part of helping us do what is right and to help deliver
fairer and ethical outcomes for our customers, especially when we face difficult decisions.

OVERVIEW

We aim to manage our business responsibly
with a culture that requires us to do what is
right to meet our customer and community
expectations, and regulatory obligations.

OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY

Respecting human rights

Engaging with our regulators

Our E&S Framework sets out our minimum requirements
through which we seek to manage the potential impacts of
human rights in relation to the Bank’s operations, supplier
management, and financing and bond facilitation.

Domestic financial regulators play an important role in maintaining the stability, fairness and efficiency of Australia’s financial
system. A strong financial system protects consumers and contributes to the country’s prosperity. Trust and confidence in our
financial system attracts investment in Australian banks, enabling us to lend to Australian households and businesses.

It is important that payment regulation applies to popular payment
solutions such as digital wallets and buy-now-pay-later services. To
simplify the system, we support removing surcharging for both credit
and debit cards with safeguards so that small businesses are supported
and benefit from lower costs. With interchange fees supporting a range
of benefits valued by customers and merchants, appropriate pricing
and economics across the payments ecosystem is essential for a safe,
reliable, sustainable and innovative payments system.

In October 2024 CBA entered into an amended Enforceable
Undertaking with the Australian Communications and Media

Authority, after paying a $7.5 million fine for breaches of
certain provisions of the Spam Act 2003 (Spam Act). We are
committed to improving how we classify electronic customer
messages and strengthening our systems, processes and
controls to support ongoing compliance with the Spam Act.
ASIC’s Better Banking for Indigenous Consumers project
included observations on the way banks, including CBA,
can provide effective products and services to First
Nations customers. In response, CBA committed to making
discretionary payments to certain identified customers
relating to certain fees dating back to July 2019. CBA
has contacted the majority of eligible customers and
made discretionary payments. CBA has engaged with
ASIC regarding its response to ASIC’s project, including
ASIC’s concerns regarding fees paid by a broader cohort
of concession customers.

For more information on how we promote the health, safety and wellbeing
of our people, see page 83.

Speaking up
We strive to create a work environment that promotes the right behaviours. Providing our SpeakUP services supports
our people and external partners to raise concerns safely, including anonymously if desired. Support and protection
are provided to whistleblowers under the Group Whistleblower Policy and applicable laws.
The Audit Committee and an executive committee receive reporting on the operation of SpeakUP three times a
year and quarterly, respectively. This year 408 reports were made to the SpeakUP Program, up from 331 in 2024.
Of these reports, 68 were whistleblower disclosures.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Where we have not met our regulatory obligations, we
seek to fully cooperate with regulatory investigations
to remediate any harm and put things right.

A range of key programs and processes help us maintain a safe
workplace. This includes our ‘Respect Lives Here’ initiative and our
health and safety management system. ‘Respect Lives Here’ is our
prevention initiative focused on building a shared understanding
of respectful and disrespectful behaviours at work. This year we
again partnered with the University of New South Wales to survey
all of our people, listen to their experiences, and provide practical
guidance on becoming active bystanders when something does
not feel right. As part of our prevention strategy, our education
and training programs help people understand our expectations
of appropriate workplace behaviour. This includes how to identify,
report and address conduct-related issues.

FINANCIAL REPORT

This includes responsible lending obligations, which
aim to encourage prudent lending and minimise the risk
that retail customers will not be able to make their loan
repayments. Retail customer-facing employees complete
annual responsible lending training and assessments,
where required by their role. We also periodically
review, audit and assure our lending processes.

Our focus on the health, safety and wellbeing of our people
helps them reach their full potential. It also reflects the right of
all people to work in an environment that is free from unlawful
workplace conduct, such as discrimination, harassment, sexual or
sex-based harassment, bullying and victimisation. We have always
taken our obligations to keep our people safe seriously and the
positive duty for organisations is aligned to our approach.

The Bank expects appropriate standards of conduct in accordance
with our Code of Conduct at all times. We take appropriate
disciplinary action in relation to any breaches of our Conduct
Policy, the Code of Conduct or our values, up to and including
dismissal from employment.

Meeting our regulatory obligations
To meet our regulatory obligations and deliver better
customer outcomes, we have policies and procedures
in place and track our obligations to our customers.

Improving payments regulation

As part of our processes, we aim to identify human rights risks
we consider to be relevant to our business activities. Our ESG
risk assessment tool assists our bankers to identify and assess
relevant human rights risks that our customers are exposed to
as part of our commercial and corporate lending processes.

The Bank continues to monitor transactions. We use a
library of red flags and typologies to identify potential
money laundering, terrorism financing and other financial
crime offences including flags for modern slavery and
child exploitation.

CREATING VALUE

Globally, bank deregulation has emerged as a significant topic. Domestically, there is increasing investor interest in the
impact of regulation on housing affordability, access to credit, regional banking, and fraud and scams. Australia’s regulation
has served our economy well through uncertain times. Financial regulators have a key role in considering regulatory reforms
that could address domestic issues such as housing affordability and access to banking services. CBA aims to engage with
regulators on key issues, and share our customer insights and other relevant perspectives.

During the year members of our Indigenous Advisory Council,
Modern Slavery Advisory Council and Community Council
provided important connection points to help inform our
approach to key human rights issues.

OVERVIEW

We maintain transparent and constructive relationships with both financial and non-financial regulators to support strong
and consistent outcomes for our customers and the broader community. CBA engages proactively with regulators to listen
to feedback and respond in a timely manner to meet our regulatory engagement principles and obligations.

Most relevant risks

Banking is an essential service for customers, who depend on having secure and
reliable access to cash and payment systems. CBA has a role to play in protecting
our customers and contributing to financial system resilience. We understand the
importance of continued investment and resources to keep our systems resilient,
minimise disruption and protect customer data. However, as cyber threats grow
more sophisticated and frequent, the risks to our customers, communities and
operations continue to evolve.

OUR APPROACH TO SUSTAINABILITY

Cyber resilience and information security remain key
priorities for the Bank. As cyber threats become more
frequent and complex, we continue to invest in strategies,
advanced technologies such as AI and GenAI, and skilled
teams to help protect our customers, assets and data.

Tech Council of Australia and the Group of Eight Australian
Universities on the inaugural Australian Cyber Exchange
2025, which aimed to showcase and strengthen Australia’s
sovereign cyber security capabilities.
We continue to strengthen our cyber resilience by regularly
reviewing and testing our approach, as well as engaging
external firms to assess and provide feedback. We look to
integrate insights from these assessments into our risk
mitigation strategies to help enhance our protective and
defensive capabilities.
We consider cyber security a shared responsibility across
the Bank. Our people play a key role by helping build secure
processes and products, and effectively identifying and
responding to cyber threats. We have built a large and capable
security team of approximately 1,400 people and actively
support developing the next generation of cyber talent.
For more information on how we manage cyber security risk,
see pages 64–65.

We have a responsibility to protect the personal information of our
customers. We apply processes and controls to respect and protect
our customers’ personal information, aiming to prevent data breaches
and responsibly govern personal information in accordance with our
internal privacy policy. Our public Group Privacy Statement provides
transparency on how the Bank collects and handles personal information,
in compliance with privacy laws.
Following the enactment of the Privacy Act 1988 reforms in December
2024, we are working to update our Group Privacy Statement to provide
greater transparency on how we use personal information for our
automated decision-making processes, including the types of personal
information used. We continue to enhance our personal information
management processes through periodic reviews of our RMF, and
updates to internal policies and standards. For suppliers who collect
or handle customer personal information, we have processes to assess
whether their data and privacy governance, policies and incident response
measures are in line with our responsibilities.

CREATING VALUE

To help support system-wide resilience and strengthen
the national security ecosystem, we engage closely with
Government and industry. Alongside industry partners,
we contributed to a consultation process that led to the
introduction of Australia’s first standalone cyber security law
– the Cyber Security Act 2024. We also collaborated with the

Safeguarding customer privacy

OVERVIEW

This year we enhanced the resilience of our cloud
infrastructure and improved key cyber control operations.
For example, we implemented near real-time code scanning
to help detect and mitigate risks associated with sensitive
information in our code. We are adopting AI tools to help
detect and neutralise external threats more efficiently. We also
worked with our suppliers to enhance their data and system
security, helping to further strengthen our own resilience.

Keeping our customers safe

1,400

invested this year to help protect our customers against
fraud, scams, cyber threats and financial crime

of our people dedicated to security

Money mule scams are an increasing
form of financial crime in Australia,
particularly targeting students
and young people. This year CBA
partnered with media company
Campus Group to raise awareness
on the scams across 13 university
campuses nationwide. The campaign
focused on educating students
about the risks and consequences of
mule scams, helping them recognise
and avoid potential threats.

To better protect our community from financial crime, we introduced
QR Cardless functionality in the CommBank app to enable secure,
card-free ATM transactions. This feature eliminates shared credentials,
verifies identity for every transaction, and uses behavioural and geolocation
analytics to detect suspicious activity.

Faster financial crime assessments using AI in the Business Bank
We are actively exploring how AI can support our teams in combating financial crime. This year we deployed an
AI-driven solution to accelerate early detection and response in financial crime risk assessment. The model enhances
prioritisation of potential cases in the Business Bank, enabling analysts to concentrate on high-risk threats. Initial
deployments of the model have demonstrated an improvement in detection, strengthening our analytical capabilities,
and enhancing protection for customers and the broader community. This initiative reflects our commitment to
innovation and safeguarding stakeholder trust.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Protecting and serving our customers begins with how we
manage data – one of our most valuable and differentiating
assets. Access to good quality data means we can make
better decisions faster, mitigate risks, and deliver more
innovative and personalised customer experiences. It also
serves as a key input in our AI solutions. Our ambition is to
make it easier for our people to securely access data faster,
enabling them to unlock greater value from data. This year
we have made progress modernising our data technology
and used AI to reduce complexity and duplication in our data.

Alongside industry partners, we continue to contribute to the Fintel Alliance’s
efforts to combat money laundering, terrorism financing and other serious
crimes. This year we worked with AUSTRAC and other major banks to
analyse 56 million data points under $10,000, uncovering criminal patterns
that would be difficult for individual institutions to detect alone.

Raising scam awareness

FINANCIAL REPORT

Modernising our
data infrastructure

As a major financial institution, we play a key role in combatting
financial crime to help protect the integrity of the financial system, our
customers and communities. We continue to invest in detection and case
management capabilities to better assess risk across customers, channels
and products, while strengthening our AML/CTF compliance to meet
regulatory obligations in Australia and other jurisdictions. To support
identification and management of risk, we systematically review guidance
from regulators, intergovernmental bodies such as the Financial Action
Task Force, and non-government resources to understand and address
emerging financial crime trends and typologies.

+$900 million

Combatting financial crime

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Keeping our customers safe is a priority. This year we commenced rollout of multi-factor authentication (MFA) as an
added layer of protection against unauthorised access to customers’ accounts and to keep their money safe. We began
our phased rollout of MFA in April 2025 to our NetBank registered customers who are also CommBank app registered.
Once enrolled, each time a customer logs into online banking, they will need to confirm the login attempt through our
CommBank app.

• Digital innovation

• Artificial intelligence
• Business disruption and technology

• Privacy and data management

Our ambition is to responsibly harness data and emerging technologies to better
understand customer needs and deliver superior digital experiences. We empower
our people with advanced tools and insights, and enable them to focus on what
matters most – supporting customers with seamless, personalised banking
experiences. We continue to attract and retain top talent in AI and engineering.
We also remain committed to responsible AI practices that drive safety and trust
as we scale AI capabilities across the Bank.

We continue to engage with the Federal Government in shaping
AI regulation in Australia. This year we provided feedback on their
proposed mandatory AI guardrails. We also engaged over 30 community
organisations through our Community Council in meaningful dialogue about
our AI journey and approach, while also listening to their concerns and
perspectives on AI.
We regularly review our responsible AI approach, to keep pace with evolving
technology, regulations and best practices. As the AI landscape continues
to shift, we expect our approach to evolve accordingly.

#1
in AI maturity in APAC 1
See more about our approach
to AI and our AI principles at
commbank.com.au/artificialintelligence

For more information on how we use data, AI and technology to deliver safe and exceptional
customer experiences, see pages 20–23.

Refer to risk on pages 65–67.

Most relevant strategic pillars

We recently launched the AI Factory, our platform for safely testing,
developing, and scaling advanced AI models on our proprietary data. Built
on Amazon Web Services (AWS) latest infrastructure, it enables our teams
to train and fine-tune AI models faster and more efficiently. The AI Factory
helps support increased scale, performance and sophistication in GenAI use
cases by addressing Australian language and banking nuances. We can now
process data at an unprecedented scale enabling us to run a deep learning
model over 3 trillion data points across 4 billion transactions. Generating
one million data points is 50% faster and requires 90% less compute time
because of our transition from Central Processing Units (CPUs) to Graphics
Processing Units (GPUs). GPU-based models can transcribe approximately
100,000 call centre interactions in 15 hours, a task that would have
previously taken three times as long.

AI has the potential to enhance customer experiences by making products and services more personalised and easier
to use. As digitisation accelerates, the demand for electricity will continue to grow. It is important to recognise
and address the environmental impacts of AI, particularly related to energy and water use. While we purchase the
equivalent of 100% renewable electricity for our Australian operations, which include our Australian data centres
within our operational control, much of our AI compute capacity is provided by third-party providers.
We also seek to minimise water impacts in our operations and report annually on water usage in our commercial
and data centre operations. We are continuing to work with key suppliers to better understand the climate-related
impacts of services they provide to us to gain a more precise and comprehensive picture of our carbon footprint,
including with our providers of AI services.

1 Ranked by the Evident AI Index. Top five among global banks for AI maturity, including transparency of responsible AI activities.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Refer to strategy on pages 16–25.

Accelerating innovation with AI Factory

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

• Fraud and scams

Banking is becoming increasingly digital, and stakeholders expect innovation that
delivers secure, personalised and seamless experiences. We are strengthening our
digital capabilities by modernising our technology and data platforms, embedding
AI and analytics into our core services, and investing in scalable infrastructure to
support operational resilience.

We continue to establish partnerships that help us strengthen our
approach to responsible and human-centred AI development. This year we
collaborated with MIT to understand how we can enhance our AI-powered
products, refine risk practices and advance AI advocacy. The collaboration
begins with two research projects focused on establishing best-practice AI
risk mitigation strategies and designing AI-powered tools that enable better
human and AI collaboration. We also expanded our strategic partnership
with Anthropic and held a three part education series to upskill over 2,300
of our people on agentic coding and responsible AI practices.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

• Digitisation

Strengthening our digital capabilities

This year we enhanced our generative responsible AI toolkit to better assess
model performance across areas like accuracy, fairness, reliability and privacy.
We have also introduced a guide to help our developers identify the types
of problems that are best solved using GenAI. To promote safer customer
interactions with our GenAI solutions, we implemented technical guardrails
designed to detect harmful content, sensitive data and jailbreaking attempts.

SUSTAINABILITY

• Artificial intelligence

We have developed responsible AI principles. These guide how we apply
our existing risk management framework and policies to design, develop,
deploy and manage AI across the Bank. Our responsible AI toolkits provide
model owners and developers with clear, practical guidance to assess
model performance, test for bias, and help to enable fairer and consistent
customer outcomes.

CREATING VALUE

Related material topics

The adoption of AI can offer customers an enhanced banking experience,
but we recognise the risks associated with the use of AI. Our responsible
approach to AI focuses on managing these risks, while seeking to maximise
customer benefits.

OVERVIEW

Investing in technology and innovation to
deliver seamless, secure and personalised
digital experiences.
