ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

While the Group has prepared the information in this section
based on its current knowledge, understanding and in good faith,
it reserves the right to change its views in the future. Except
as required by applicable laws or regulations, the Group does
not undertake to publicly update or review any forward-looking
statements, or the assumptions on which they are based.

In this section

FINANCIAL REPORT

This section discusses scenario analysis. There are inherent
limitations with scenario analysis and it is difficult to predict
which, if any, of the scenarios discussed in this section might
eventuate. Scenarios do not constitute definitive outcomes or
probabilities, and scenario analysis relies on assumptions that
may or may not be, or prove to be, correct and may or may not
eventuate. Scenarios may also be impacted by additional factors
to the assumptions disclosed.

We believe affordable, reliable energy is critical
for Australia’s prosperity and future economy.
We remain committed to supporting Australia’s
transition to net zero by 2050.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

This section includes climate-related information and other
forward-looking statements. These forward-looking statements
are not guarantees or predictions of future climate outcomes and
metrics, financial performance or share prices. The statements
are subject to known and unknown risks, uncertainties and other
factors, many of which are beyond the Group’s control. Readers
are cautioned not to place undue reliance on such statements
in light of the significant uncertainty in climate metrics and
modelling that limit the extent to which they are useful for
decision making, and the many underlying risks and assumptions
that may cause actual outcomes to differ materially.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

This section contains climate-related metrics and exposures that
relate to the full year ended 30 June 2025 with comparisons
to the full year ended 30 June 2024, unless otherwise stated.
Financed emissions and sector-level goal and target progress are
lagged due to customer emissions reporting cadences, and are
therefore for the full year ended 30 June 2024 with comparisons
to the full year ended 30 June 2023.

102

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

103

Balancing trade-offs
to support the transition
Australia needs effective climate
change policies that reduce emissions,
provide investment certainty, support
economic growth, and minimise
increased costs for essential goods
such as food and electricity.

Harder-to-abate sectors, such as
transport, agriculture and heavy industry
are important sectors for Australia’s
future economy and need balanced,
long-term support to transition. While
these sectors make up a smaller portion
of our lending, compared to housing,
our aim is to continue working with
our customers in these sectors and
exploring ways we can help them
reduce their environmental impact.

We are supportive of an affordable
and reliable energy transition that is
inclusive and considers the needs of
Australian communities. To deliver this,
we need efficient regulation to help
accelerate energy supply and contribute
to productivity. With appropriate sharing
of costs and benefits, we believe
Australia can transition to net zero by
2050 and create a brighter future for all.

Paul O’Malley
Chair

Matt Comyn
Chief Executive Officer

Our goals and targets remain
aligned to 1.5°C scenarios. We
believe this continues to be the
right setting against which to
track our progress. We remain
committed to achieving our
goals and targets and continue
to monitor progress. Although
we reassessed the feasibility of
sector-level financed emissions
targets for the Australian
agriculture sector, we have not
set any new goals or targets
this year.
For a summary of our goals and
targets, see pages 162–163.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

The energy sector continues to
be the biggest contributor to
Australia’s domestic emissions.
We believe that affordably
decarbonising Australia’s electricity
grid remains the most important
step for Australia.

Building community support for
Australia’s transition to net zero is
important. We welcome policies
that can help Australia deliver more
affordable and reliable energy for all
Australians, now and into the future.

We continue to provide lending
products and tools that can help our
customers understand the actions they
can take to reduce their emissions,
make their homes and businesses
more energy efficient and aim to ease
cost-of-living pressures.

Closing

As we prepare for new sustainability
reporting, including required
disclosures on climate targets, we
have taken the opportunity to clarify
our aspirations as targets or goals.
We considered factors like CBA’s
control or influence, as well as key
external dependencies, challenges
or assumptions. As a result, some
previously reported targets are
now described as goals. We believe
this change gives stakeholders
greater clarity on how we will
achieve our goals and targets, and
helps to enhance the accuracy and
transparency of our reporting.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Limiting global warming to 1.5°C above
pre-industrial levels appears increasingly
difficult and the pathway to net zero,
globally, is looking narrower. The
Australian Government is continuing
to develop a national Net Zero Plan
to guide our country’s transition to
meet our legislated target of net zero
greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.

Meeting Australia’s 2030 interim
emissions reduction target requires
significant investment. Estimates
suggest Australia’s transition to net
zero needs more than $600 billion of
investment by 2050 to decarbonise
the nation’s industry and energy
systems. CBA can support our
customers to fund their transition.

Given one in three Australians and one
in four Australian businesses call us
their main financial institution, we play
an important role in financing economic
activity. However, the main driver of
emissions reductions for our retail and
small business customers is Australia’s
electricity grid transitioning.

Physical climate risks to homes, national
infrastructure and communities pose
a growing challenge for affordability,
insurability and long-term economic
resilience. With only around 20%
of Australia’s $60 billion nationally
significant infrastructure initiatives
designed to mitigate climate risks,
there is more work to do on building
national climate resilience. We would
welcome reforms that build a shared
understanding of physical climate risks
to help inform decisions by households,
lenders, insurers and governments.

Since commencing our journey
we have continued to learn more
about sector-level pathways,
actions we can take and key
dependencies in achieving our
targets. Stakeholders have noted
that some of our sector-level
financed emissions targets are
dependent on external factors,
including decarbonisation
of Australia’s electricity grid.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Energy security is crucial to the
success of the Australian economy.
Australia is facing a critical moment
in the energy transition, needing to
deliver important national energy
infrastructure that lowers emissions
quickly and affordably.

All energy sources and grid stability
approaches have trade-offs such as
emissions intensity, cost, time-to build and
impacts on nature. We see an important
role for governments and industries
to balance and communicate those
trade-offs and develop sound policies.

Continuing to support
our customers

Looking ahead

We set our first operational
emissions targets in 2009 and our
first sector-level financed emissions
targets in 2022. We take meeting
our commitments seriously. We
are pleased to have achieved our
2025 Scope 1 and 2, and Scope 3
operational emissions reduction
targets, and to be on track for
12 of our sector-level financed
emissions goals and targets.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Australia’s transition to a net zero economy by 2050
is underway. Government policies, and new generation
and transmission infrastructure are assisting. As the
bank for all Australians, we remain committed to
helping our customers and supporting Australia’s
transition to net zero by 2050.

While progress on meeting Australia’s
renewable energy target is commendable,
over reliance on one energy source can
lead to vulnerabilities in our electricity
markets, blackouts and increased costs,
putting more pressure on energy bills.
We welcome the Australian Energy
Market Operator’s (AEMO) work on
approaches to grid stability.

This year we reviewed the settings in our
E&S Policy and Framework. Following our
review, we are expanding our Transition
Plan requirements to include thermal
coal clients who derive 25% or more of
their revenue from the sale of thermal
coal. Our policy settings continue to
reinforce our climate strategy.

We continue to transparently
outline our rationale for the
commitments we make in
supporting Australia’s transition
to net zero by 2050.

SUSTAINABILITY

The demand for electricity in Australia
is increasing due to economic
and population growth, increased
digitisation and electrification. Access
to affordable and reliable energy
sources is needed to support Australia’s
prosperity and future economy.

Grid reliability risks include extreme
weather, energy shortfalls and
operational issues with ageing coal-fired
power generators facing early closure.
Government and national energy bodies
have signalled gas will remain a key
part of Australia’s energy mix during
the transition. This is a pragmatic
trade-off, as gas currently remains
critical for key industrial processes, as
well as serving as a secure and reliable
back-up to renewables. We note the
Government is currently undertaking
the Gas Market Review to consider the
effectiveness of the current framework
to, amongst other things, ensure there
is sufficient domestic supply. Balancing
environmental and economic outcomes,
while increasing renewable energy and
maintaining grid stability, is needed.

Progress on our
commitments

CREATING VALUE

Historically, Australia has been a
reliable energy producer and exporter,
making affordable energy prices
a competitive advantage. As we
transition to a net zero economy, we
need to maintain this advantage to
support Australian living standards and
help build a brighter future for all.

The Bank’s exposure to the upstream
oil and gas extraction sectors is low at
<0.1% of total committed exposures
(TCE). Our lending portfolio will continue
to evolve alongside Australia’s energy
transition. Our Environmental and Social
(E&S) Policy and Framework has long
recognised Australia’s need for a secure
energy platform. We expect certain
upstream oil and gas extraction and
coal-fired power generation customers
to have Transition Plans, including Scope
3 emissions, before we provide corporate
or trade finance, or bond facilitation.

OVERVIEW

Australia’s future economy
needs more affordable
and reliable energy

Expanding renewables in Australia’s
electricity grid has been challenging
due to insufficient transmission
infrastructure and complex planning
processes across different levels of
government. AEMO has modelled
that Australia can meet its renewable
target provided planned investments
are delivered, making critical energy
infrastructure a national priority. Policy
coordination is also needed to better
integrate consumer energy resources
into the grid, benefitting the system
and all consumers.

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Message from our
Chair and CEO

104

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

105

Australians broadly support a focus on the nation’s climate change ambitions but are concerned about the impact on their cost
of living and quality of life. The transition requires government and industry to balance and communicate competing trade-offs
between energy sources. Effective system management and grid stability will continue to be important to maximise use of
renewables while preventing potential blackouts.

Australia’s Climate Change Authority (CCA) has published six sector pathways. 1 The CCA’s report identifies potential
technologies and actions for each sector that, taken together, could deliver net zero emissions for Australia by 2050.

The table below includes key sectors 2 informed by the Bank’s sector-level financed emissions. The table summarises each
sector’s emissions profile based on government sources, along with the potential technologies and actions needed to support
Australia’s transition to net zero by 2050, adapted from the CCA’s report.

Affordably decarbonising Australia’s
electricity grid is essential for
Australia’s economy.

Technologies and actions needed by 2050

Replacing coal-fired power stations with renewables is challenging. The nation needs
to address long planning timeframes, supply chain constraints and increase community
support for infrastructure. Installing rooftop solar remains popular with consumers. The
Government’s renewable energy target provided Australia with direction. Recent policies
including the Capacity Investment Scheme and Rewiring the Nation are helping to accelerate
the transition.

Achieving Australia’s emissions targets and producing reliable, affordable energy requires
effective community engagement and policies that seek to maintain our country’s
energy advantage.

storage technology

• Grid integration

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

With generation capacity and transmission infrastructure progressing, Australia is better
placed to meet its 2030 target of a 43% reduction in emissions below 2005 levels. It is
our understanding that Australia should be able to produce sufficient generation to meet
growing demand over the next decade, provided current State and Federal Government
projects to build renewable and dispatchable generation, transmission assets and policies to
coordinate consumer energy resources eventuate. Ongoing monitoring, policy adjustments
and managing new challenges are essential for helping Australia remain on track.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Electricity accounts for around 34% of Australia’s emissions. Decarbonising the electricity
grid is critical to the decarbonisation of other sectors, including housing – the largest
sector that CBA finances. For Australia, and the Bank, to achieve its emissions reduction
ambitions, the electricity grid needs to decarbonise.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Currently available and mature

As Australia’s economy and population grows, households and businesses electrify,
and digitisation increases, the demand for electricity will continue to grow. A reliable
and affordable electricity grid is integral to the economy and Australia’s future prosperity.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Key sectors 2

SUSTAINABILITY

The CCA recognises that emissions must fall across all sectors, however each sector is on a different pathway to net zero
by 2050. For some sectors, like power generation, with coordinated planning, the pathway may be steep and rapid as
technologies are mature and ready to scale. Sectors like agriculture, however, are expected to experience a slower and more
gradual path as technologies are in early development. Government policy support and investment are needed to accelerate
the deployment of early-stage technologies and actions.

CREATING VALUE

Reducing emissions in key sectors

OVERVIEW

Supporting
Australia’s
energy
transition

Renewable energy generation is increasing. However, high electricity costs remain a concern for consumers and businesses.
Australia needs to find affordable ways to achieve its 2030 renewable energy target and maintain system stability. We continue
to support AEMO’s view that flexible gas-powered generation is crucial in a high renewable electricity system. Along with batteries
and pumped hydro, gas can provide critical backup to variable renewable energy, reliability as coal-fired power stations retire, and
flexibility to adapt to demand changes. Gas plants, capable of rapid startup, help to provide continuous electricity supply and
support grid reliability and stability, especially during high-demand periods and extreme weather.

Financed emissions

We define operational emissions as our Scope 1, 2 and
selected Scope 3 emissions (excluding financed emissions)
resulting from the operations of our business for the Bank,
including ASB, CBA India and other overseas operations.

Financed emissions are our Scope 3, Category 15 emissions
resulting from the emissions we finance through our lending.
They are estimated based on an attributed proportion of our
customers’ emissions.

Australia is making progress in transitioning to net zero.

disproportionately impacted. This includes businesses
and households who may struggle to switch to loweremissions technologies and regions impacted by changes
in employment opportunities. These challenges can be
amplified by the physical impacts of climate change.

Australian housing

For important information on how we have assessed the resilience of our business model, see pages 149–158.

Financed emissions by sector
CREATING VALUE

While climate change is not currently estimated to be
financially material to the Bank in the short term, it is
important that our climate strategy aligns to our purpose.
This means supporting our customers as they take action to
adapt to and/or mitigate the physical or transition impacts
of climate change. Further, the risks we may be exposed to
or the opportunities that may be available to us need to be
assessed, quantified where possible, and have an appropriate
level of control and oversight by management and the Board.

Our in-scope drawn lending exposed to the Australian housing sector accounts for 63.9% of total in-scope drawn lending and
makes up 17.3% of our total financed emissions. The remaining portfolio consists mostly of business lending across a diverse
range of sectors and customers, ranging from small businesses to institutional customers. Lending to upstream oil and gas
extraction, and thermal coal mining accounts for <0.1% of total in-scope drawn lending and is 2.0% of our financed emissions.
Lending to the power generation sector represents 0.5% of our in-scope drawn lending and 1.9% of our financed emissions. CBA
has set sector-level financed emissions goals and targets on sectors that account for 71% of the Bank’s in-scope drawn lending. 1

Since 2009 we have progressively reduced the emissions
from our operations, for example the energy that powers our
branches and buildings. This now makes up less than 1%
of our total emissions. However, the emissions associated
with the lending we provide to our customers is more
than 99% of our total emissions. Importantly, much of the
emissions produced by our customers and suppliers come
from activities or actions that are not within the Bank’s direct
control or are more challenging to influence.

OVERVIEW

Climate change can have a significant impact on the
Australian economy and our communities. Given one in
three Australians and one in four Australian businesses call
us their main financial institution, our role is to help build
Australia’s future economy by supporting our customers
and communities as they navigate the choices they need to
make to transition to a lower-carbon economy.

Current state

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Supporting Australia’s transition to net zero by 2050

SUSTAINABILITY

We set our first operational emissions targets in 2009. To further support our climate strategy, in 2022 we set our first
sector-level financed emissions targets. We take meeting our commitments seriously and are pleased to have met our 2025
Scope 1 and 2, and Scope 3 operational emissions reductions targets. We are also below the reference scenario for 12 of our
sector-level financed emissions goals and targets.

CREATING VALUE

While the financial impacts of climate change for CBA may be less immediate and significant when compared to other material
issues, we have developed initiatives which aim to address climate-related risks and opportunities. Our climate strategy
includes mitigation and adaptation actions. Current opportunities include: developing specific banking products and services
to support customers to reduce their emissions and finance their transition or adaptation initiatives; supporting customers
with natural disaster recovery; helping customers to assess their climate risks and develop adaptation strategies; and
collaborating with stakeholders to develop tools, insights and research that can be shared.

OVERVIEW

Strategy

In helping build Australia’s future economy, our climate-related opportunities are centred on supporting our customers to
transition to a lower-carbon economy and in helping them make their homes, businesses or activities more resilient to the
impacts of climate change. We also aim to continue assessing, managing and monitoring our climate-related risks, and
investing in our direct operations to reduce our environmental impact.

OVERVIEW

Targets and goals are specific, measurable and time bound. They are set to manage and mitigate climaterelated risks or pursue opportunities, and are subject to assumptions and monitoring. A target is supported by
specific steps the Bank will take to change our exposures or reduce our emissions. Whereas a goal is a desired
outcome but its achievement is primarily shaped by external factors outside the Bank’s control or influence,
and the Bank has not made a current commitment to change our exposures or other activities to meet the goal.

Our focus is on providing banking services, predominantly lending, to retail and business banking customers
in Australia and New Zealand. A smaller portion of our lending is for large institutional banking customers.
We have set 2030 sector-level financed emissions goals and targets to help us support Australia’s transition
to net zero by 2050.

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Lending to support the transition

Targets

110

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

113

Baseline: reflects our portfolio’s starting point in terms
of absolute emissions or emissions intensity.

2

Actual: our portfolio’s financed absolute emissions or
emissions intensity for a given financial year.

3

Reference scenario: the science-based scenario we
currently use to inform our goal or target and what we
compare our portfolio’s financed absolute emissions or
emissions intensity to. We expect to review these over time.

4

CBA portfolio: an assessment of how the portfolio’s
financed absolute emissions or emissions intensity
estimate for the current reporting period compares to
the reference scenario at the same point in time.

5

Goal or target: our financed absolute emissions or
emissions intensity goal or target for the portfolio
to be achieved by 2030.

For some sectors the charts do not include the baseline or
actual to protect our customers’ confidentiality. This is to
support cases where, for example, only one customer has
drawn lending in the reporting period.
For some sectors, our baseline absolute emissions or emissions
intensity is not the same as the reference scenario base year.
For example, where our portfolio is not representative of the
relevant Australian or global sector, to account for the impacts
of COVID-19 on the sector, or due to data limitations.

to the sector

to reduce our overall exposures.

To better assist our customers with their sustainability
journeys, we support our bankers with further
learning, development and educational opportunities.
This year Business Banking (BB) partnered with the
Australian Graduate School of Management to deliver
a ‘Future focused banking’ program. The program was
completed by more than 150 of our approximately 800
frontline business bankers. It focused on improving
banker knowledge and skills to discuss the opportunities
and risks of the transition to a lower-carbon economy
with our customers.

Through risk appetite and in some
instances, pricing incentives, we
can actively steer our exposures
within a sector towards less
emissions-intensive customers.

4

1

To help simplify the car purchasing process for our customers
we developed the Buy and Own a Car platform, enabling
customers to purchase cars, including diesel, petrol and EVs.
The platform encourages EV uptake by offering customers
EV and charger discounts, an EV cost comparison tool,
discounted finance, and educational resources to buy
new and used cars. To support eligible essential workers
and those earning under $100,000 per year, we launched
the EV Access Program in partnership with the Clean
Energy Finance Corporation (CEFC). This program offers
lower-cost finance options for EVs under $55,000 enabling
more customers to experience fuel savings through EVs.

We offer tools which can help our customers identify
opportunities to make sustainable business changes.
Our Green Buildings Tool can help customers with
commercial property identify actions which may improve
energy efficiency and reduce emissions in their buildings.
The tool estimates the upfront costs of implementing
these actions, potential reduction in electricity costs upon
implementation and the potential impact on the building’s
National Australian Built Environment Rating System
(NABERS) energy rating. Our Sustainability Action Tool
offers insights, actions and resources to help small-tomedium businesses reduce environmental impacts and
business costs, focusing on energy, transport and waste.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Rebalancing our
portfolio towards less
emissions-intensive
customers

Our Home Energy Upgrades service is designed to support
customers through every step of their home energy upgrade
journey, to help inform their decision making and understand
potential energy bill savings. The service also connects
customers with accredited solar and battery installers
across Australia offering personalised quotes, along with
discounted finance options and special offers. Our Home
Energy Assessment Tool, developed in collaboration with
Home Efficiency Australia, helps educate our customers
by providing them an efficiency score and a cost-benefit
breakdown for different home energy upgrades.

Our approach varies by sector
but can include engaging with
customers, providing insights,
data and dedicated products,
services and tools, as well as
advocating for government
policies that can help them
reduce their emissions.

1

With one in four Australian businesses naming CBA
as their main financial institution, we aim to continue
supporting small-to-medium businesses. We have a
range of sustainable funding solutions to support our
business customers to reduce their emissions, help our
customers save on costs, build resilience and capture
new growth opportunities.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Taking steps that can help
our customers reduce
their emissions

There are five key concepts to understand our
sector-level financed emissions progress.

Australian consumers are increasingly investing in their own
energy transitions with the installation of rooftop solar panels,
battery storage and purchase of EVs. However, the costs to
implement these technologies continues to be a barrier for
many Australians who are seeking energy bill relief or fuel
savings. Our approach is to help guide our customers by
raising awareness of the technologies available, providing
education, connecting customers with installers and EV
manufacturers, and providing financing options and offers.

SUSTAINABILITY

There are three ways we
are working towards our
sector-level financed
emissions goals and targets:

How to read our sector-level charts

Support for our
business customers

CREATING VALUE

Helping our retail customers

Actions we are taking
Many factors can influence our financed
emissions, including the composition of
our lending portfolio, and our customers’
greenhouse gas emissions and activities.
As we work towards our 2030 goals and
targets, our reported figures may fluctuate
up or down. Our approach to setting and
achieving our goals and targets may also
evolve as new data, methodologies and
other factors emerge. Importantly, we
do not expect our customers to follow
the specific sector-level pathways we are
working towards.

There is a role we can play in helping our customers better understand their climate impact
and make more informed choices as part of the transition to a lower-carbon economy.

OVERVIEW

This year we have taken the opportunity to clarify how we classify our aspirations as goals or targets. This resulted in some
previously reported targets now being described as goals, supporting our continuous efforts to enhance the accuracy and
transparency of our reporting. This change is intended to better align to where our pathways for achieving outcomes are subject to
key external dependencies, for example the pace of Australia’s energy transition. We have set 10 goals and five targets covering 71%
of CBA’s in-scope drawn lending. For several of these sectors, including Australian housing, Australian commercial property and
aluminium, lower emissions are heavily reliant on grid decarbonisation. Should the electricity grid not decarbonise at the expected
rate, or if State or Federal Government policy settings do not support decarbonisation, then achieving our goals for these sectors will
be made more challenging. We continue to identify actions we can take to support our customers.

Supporting our retail and
business customers

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Lending to support the transition continued

Emissions metric

112

114

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

115

Lending in line with our E&S Framework
The E&S Framework outlines our minimum requirements that seek to manage the climate impacts of our financing activities,
including bond facilitation. This includes requirements for certain clients in the fossil fuel sectors, which can help us work towards
our 2030 financed emissions targets for thermal coal mining, upstream oil and gas extraction and power generation. As set out in
the E&S Framework, these requirements are subject to Australia having a secure energy platform.

Engaging our institutional customers on their transition readiness

Since we began this engagement in 2022, there has been a
reduction of 23 mining, oil and gas customers. Many of our
customers are currently reporting against the Task Force on

Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework
and undertaking additional climate stress testing. This is
expected to increase with the introduction of mandatory
climate reporting in Australia.
While our customers have continued to enhance the
information they can provide us, we acknowledge they may
be at different stages of transitioning their businesses. We
aim to continue working with our customers to understand
their strategies and plans.

Net zero GHG emissions by
2050 (or sooner) ambition

The client has a qualitative net zero GHG emissions ambition that covers
at least 95% of its Scope 1 and 2 emissions and covers the most material

Medium-term (2027–2035)
GHG reduction target(s)

The client has set medium-term targets that are aligned to a well-below 2°C
sectoral pathway.

Decarbonisation strategy

The client’s Transition Plan identifies the set of actions the client intends to take
to achieve its GHG reduction targets over the targeted time frame. If the client
chooses to employ offsetting in relation to its GHG reduction targets, they also
disclose the quantity and type of offset certification.

Retail trade

Capital allocation

The client discloses the stated value of its capital expenditure that it intends
to allocate towards climate solutions in the future. If relevant, the client also
discloses the value of its expected capital allocation they expect to deploy,
to transition away from carbon-intensive assets.

Climate governance

The client discloses evidence of board or board committee oversight
of the management of climate change risks.

1 Transition plan is based on the customer’s definition of what a transition plan is. This is not necessarily consistent with the Transition Plan criteria required for

Recognising that transition plan expectations are evolving, we also assess clients’ Transition Plans in relation to other matters.
These matters can include, just transition, where the client’s Transition Plan recognises the social and economic impacts of its
decarbonisation efforts, particularly the impact on communities with a heavy reliance on the fossil fuel industry; climate policy
engagement; or TCFD disclosures, where the client explicitly signposts TCFD-aligned disclosures in its annual reporting or
publishes them in a TCFD-aligned report. CBA will continue to monitor developments, including guidance being prepared by
the Australian Treasury.

From the publication of our 2025 E&S Framework, our Transition Plan expectations will also apply to clients who derive 25%

FINANCIAL REPORT

2022

28 26 26
24

Unless we had already determined we would cease to offer new corporate or trade finance, or bond facilitation to a client from
1 January 2025, we continued to monitor the Transition Plans of applicable clients in FY25. In some instances, we determined
the client would not have, or would be unlikely to have, a Transition Plan meeting our core criteria by 31 December 2024. After
making this determination, we did not provide new corporate or trade finance, or bond facilitation with a maturity beyond 31
December 2024, except for uncommitted exposures that could be cancelled by 31 December 2024. After 1 January 2025,
we did not provide new corporate or trade finance, or bond facilitation to clients whose Transition Plans did not meet the core
criteria, based on the third-party assessment. We took the same approach for refinancing. Otherwise, existing exposures for
these clients remain on our balance sheet beyond 31 December 2024 until their maturity.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Top 100 customers by industry

This year this expectation applied to existing oil and/or gas producing or metallurgical coal mining clients who derive 15% or
more of their revenue from the sale of oil, gas or metallurgical coal; or power generation clients who generate 25% or more of
their electricity from coal, and to who we offer corporate or trade finance, or bond facilitation. We continued to engage a third
party to independently assess applicable clients’ Transition Plans against the requirements set out in our E&S Framework,
using the core criteria set out below.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Many of our large, high-emitting institutional customers
operate in harder-to-abate industries or their business
activities are required to support Australia’s energy transition.
We have again engaged 100 of our most carbon-intensive
customers across Institutional Banking and Markets (IB&M)
to form a better understanding of the risks and opportunities
they face.

In 2023 we clarified our expectations for certain clients to have a Transition Plan from 2025.

SUSTAINABILITY

We offer a range of financing solutions to support our customers in achieving their climate strategies and goals. We have a full
range of financial markets capabilities, capital raising solutions, transaction banking services, and risk management products and
services. Our sustainable finance team is dedicated to supporting customers in taking actions that drive better E&S outcomes,
for example by linking their sustainability strategy to their financing arrangements. This includes structuring and facilitating use
of proceeds instruments. Our carbon team also provides bespoke solutions to support project developers with managing price
risk in the carbon market and supporting businesses to acquire or provide Australian Carbon Credit Units (ACCUs).

Client Transition Plan assessments and criteria

Banking products, tools and services

OVERVIEW

We intend to support our large corporate and institutional banking customers in line with our credit policies, E&S Framework
commitments, and sector-level financed emissions goals and targets. Our larger customers are generally more progressed
on transitioning or adapting their businesses to make them more climate resilient. Our approach is focused on working with
our customers to understand their long-term transition plans and funding needs. We provide financing solutions where it may
assist their plans.

Australian housing

In-scope drawn lending as a
proportion of Group TCE

Dependencies and challenges

41.7%

The reference scenario that informs our goal assumes
the decarbonisation of Australia’s electricity grid and a
coordinated transition to replace coal-fired power with
energy sources that produce lower emissions. Meeting our
goal assumes more Australian homeowners will adopt home
energy upgrades such as installing rooftop solar and batteries,
and electrifying gas appliances for hot water, cooking, heating
or cooling. Greater integration of full electrification and
thermal efficiency measures in the construction of new homes
or renovations is also assumed.

Sector overview and outlook
As at 30 June 2024, Australian housing accounted for 63.9% of our in-scope drawn lending. Residential buildings in Australia
use approximately 24% of overall electricity and contribute over 10% of total carbon emissions. 2 Home emissions are mainly
driven by electricity and gas for heating or cooling, cooking and hot water. While many households have made home energy
upgrades, further work by industry and government is required to support homeowners in apartments and townhouses where
energy efficiency measures are more difficult to adopt.

38.8

Despite strong solar uptake, with around one in three Australian
homes having systems installed, battery adoption remains
low due to the upfront costs homeowners incur. Recent state
and federal rebates designed to incentivise battery adoption
could increase installation rates. Should the electricity grid
not decarbonise at the expected rate, or if State or Federal
Government policy settings do not support decarbonisation,
then achieving our goal will be more challenging.
In 2023, we used scenario modelling to explore a pathway
to achieving our 2030 goal. We have drawn on insights from
AEMO and the International Energy Agency (IEA) to chart

SUSTAINABILITY

As at 30 June 2024, the emissions intensity of our portfolio was 35.0 kgCO2-e/m2, a reduction of 3.8 kgCO2-e/m2 compared
to our 2021 baseline, 0.1 kgCO2-e/m2 compared to June 2023 and is 17% above the reference scenario. The modest
reduction compared to 2023 partly reflects a change in the data provider we use to calculate a home’s floorspace and number
of bedrooms, and limitations in using publicly available data sets. Our financed emissions calculation draws on publicly
available household energy consumption benchmarks meaning, at times, it is unable to account for the impact of increased
solar and battery installations or changing energy usage by our customers.

SBTi – pathway (Australia) 5

Jun 30

While achieving our 2030 housing goal will be challenging, the Bank has no intention of reducing our lending to Australian
housing customers to achieve it. We remain committed to supporting our customers on their path to home ownership or property
investment, and where practicable, helping them reduce their energy bills and ease cost-of-living pressures. Our Home Energy
Upgrades service aims to help bridge knowledge gaps and simplify decision making for home energy upgrades. Eligible customers
purchasing solar and battery systems can access discounted finance offerings that are supported by the CEFC’s Household
Energy Upgrades Fund.

For more information on how we are supporting our retail customers, see page 113.

1 The analysis presented remains as originally released in our 2023 Climate Report, with no subsequent updates. While SBTi and CRREM do not provide an
analytical breakdown of drivers in changes in emissions intensity between 2021 and 2030, in 2023, we modelled an indicative pathway exploring:
– Direct or Scope 1 emissions. We modelled a potential reduction through reduced demand for natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) and wood, based
on AEMO and IEA projections.
– Indirect or Scope 2 emissions. We modelled the relative impact of rooftop solar, batteries and behind the meter activities; and decarbonisation of the
electricity grid, and other factors. This split was estimated based on data from AEMO and the Australian Energy Market Commission.

Australian
commercial

Jun 24

SBTi – pathway (Australia) 2

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

As at 30 June 2024, the emissions intensities of our office, retail and industrial portfolios were 58.1 kgCO2-e/m2,
48.6 kgCO2-e/m2 and 30.8 kgCO2-e/m2, respectively. This is below the reference scenario for all portfolios, reflecting
differences in our portfolio’s composition compared to the national average.

30.6
Goal

20

SUSTAINABILITY

As at 30 June 2024, our 2030 goals cover 77% 3 of our in-scope drawn lending to the Australian commercial property sector
and include the office, retail and industrial sub-sectors. We have set separate goals for these sub-sectors to recognise the
different baseline emissions intensities for different types of buildings. This year we have changed our goals to reflect a
material refresh of the Government’s Commercial Building Baseline Study (CBBS). Key changes in our goals for the retail
and industrial sub-sectors are largely driven by reallocation of energy demand across building types in the 2024 CBBS as
compared to the 2022 CBBS. Therefore our industrial sub-sector progress, which was above the reference scenario last year,
is now below the reference scenario. It also means, we are unable to report year-on-year progress for these sub-sectors as the
CBBS update does not provide comparative information.

CREATING VALUE

As at 30 June 2024, lending to the Australian commercial property sector accounted for 6.9% of our in-scope drawn lending and
3.3% of our financed emissions. In Australia, commercial buildings use approximately 25% of overall electricity and contribute 10%
of total carbon emissions. 2 Newer commercial properties tend to be more energy efficient and are expected to be better positioned
for the transition, while older buildings need significant capital expenditure and upgrades to improve their energy efficiency.

OVERVIEW

In addition to the pace of grid decarbonisation, sector disruption in the form of rising construction costs and low construction
labour availability may slow the transition and impact our ability to achieve our goals.

Australian road

proportion of Group TCE

Aviation

0.6%

passenger and light commercial vehicle finance

0.3%

As at 30 June 2024, our Australian road goal covers 23% of our in-scope drawn lending to the transport sector and 67% of
our in-scope drawn lending to the Australian motor vehicle finance sector. In 2023, Scope 1 emissions from the Australian
transport sector accounted for approximately 21% of Australia’s total emissions, 2 with passenger and light commercial
vehicles making up 60% of those emissions. Accelerating EV adoption and charging infrastructure investment is critical for
reducing emissions. Policies like the New Vehicle Efficiency Standard aim to help consumers save money on fuel costs and
provide more choice of new cars that are fuel-efficient, low or zero emissions, alongside environmental benefits.

As at 30 June 2024, the aviation goal covers 16% of our in-scope drawn lending to the transport sector and largely includes
lending to international customers. In 2023, aviation accounted for over 2% of global energy-related emissions.2 Australia is highly
dependent on the aviation sector to connect Australia to the world. Decarbonising this sector is challenging, given the reliance on
hydrocarbons and the lack of available low-carbon fuel alternatives.

Our goal and portfolio

Our 2030 aviation goal includes lending to operators of aircraft as well as lending to customers that own and lease these
assets to operators. As at 30 June 2024, aviation emissions intensity was 93 gCO2 /revenue passenger km, a reduction of
10 gCO2 /revenue passenger km compared to our 2023 baseline, and 6% below the reference scenario, reflecting new lending
to airlines who operate newer aircraft with higher passenger load factors.

As at 30 June 2024, Australian road emissions intensity was 234 gCO2 /vehicle km, a reduction of 11 gCO2 /vehicle km
compared to our 2023 baseline, and 15% below the reference scenario, reflecting increased lending to customers to purchase
low-emissions vehicles.

IEA NZE 2021 – Global pathway 5

Dependencies and challenges

While availability of EVs in Australia is growing, there continues to be barriers to retail and business customers switching to EVs,
such as the comparative price of EVs, availability of charging infrastructure and limitations on range. Should the uptake of EVs
not continue to increase as currently forecast by organisations such as the Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research
Organisation (CSIRO),6 or if State or Federal Government policy settings are not supportive, then achieving our goal may be unlikely.

The reference scenario that informs our goal assumes, amongst other things, that SAF begins to account for a larger
proportion of energy consumed by aviation by 2030. The reference scenario also assumes improvements in efficiency of
aircraft, including a mixture of those already available in addition to new technologies being developed.
Currently there are no zero emissions alternatives to aviation fuels that can be deployed at scale. Should the scaling of lowcarbon fuels not happen at the rate anticipated, or if policy settings are not supportive, then achieving our goal may be unlikely.

Our focus is to support our retail and business customers in their transition to EVs. We seek to encourage EV uptake with
specific discounts for EVs. We also support our business customers to purchase EVs, hybrid cars or charging infrastructure
through our Green Vehicle and Equipment Finance product.
For more information on how we are supporting our retail and business customers, see page 113.

Our intention and activities
We provide structured asset finance solutions which can be used by aviation customers to support the financing of newer
aircraft and more fuel-efficient planes.
For more information on how we are supporting our institutional and large corporate customers to transition, see pages 114–115.

Our heavy industry portfolio is concentrated to a small number of companies. To protect our customers’ confidentiality, we
have adopted a ‘traffic light’ disclosure of our portfolio against goals. This is to support cases where, for example, only one
customer has drawn lending in the reporting period.

1.8

Our goals and portfolio

CBA portfolio

2.0

CREATING VALUE

Steel is essential for many other sectors, such as construction and machinery, and is expected to continue playing an important
role in Australia’s energy transition, including the construction of wind turbines and solar farms. Cement is required for housing,
buildings and infrastructure. Alumina is an intermediate product in the production of aluminium, which is used in technologies that
are also critical for reducing emissions. Heavy industry is considered harder-to-abate due to its reliance on grid decarbonisation,
process efficiencies and low-emissions technologies that, in many instances, are not yet commercially viable at scale in Australia.

Alumina emissions intensity 1 tCO2-e/t-aluminium

Alumina emissions intensity (Heavy industries tab)

OVERVIEW

We define the heavy industry sector as the production of steel, alumina, aluminium and cement. As at 30 June 2024, lending to the
heavy industry sector represented <0.1% of our in-scope drawn lending and 13.3% of our financed emissions. In 2023, Scope 1
emissions from the production of alumina, aluminium, iron, steel, lime, cement and other metals accounted for approximately
7% of Australia’s total emissions.2 Greenhouse gas emissions from heavy industry production generally occur from the high heat
and chemical reactions required in manufacturing processes. These industries are also significant electricity consumers.

below the selected reference scenario.
Reference scenario assumptions: refiners switch to using
renewable electricity and the adoption of new technologies,
such as electric boilers.

100

FINANCIAL REPORT

Power generation emissions intensity 4 kgCO2/MWh

Our power generation portfolio includes customers ranging from lower emissions renewable energy generators to more
emissions-intensive customers, such as electricity gentailers. In Australia and New Zealand, we are supporting our gentailer
customers with financing as they transition to lower emissions technologies. We continue to focus on growing our lending
to renewable energy and electricity storage customers to support the firming of renewables.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

To maintain grid reliability and stability, and to reduce the sector’s emissions, investment in firming in the form of batteries,
pumped hydro and some gas-fired electricity generation is crucial. We see a role for government policy and public funding
to de-risk renewable energy and transmission projects. Should government policy settings change or not be implemented as
currently stated, it may impact our ability to achieve our target.

Scope of our sector target

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Our power generation portfolio is diversified by technology and geography with over 50% of our lending exposures outside
Australia. Our power generation portfolio is also 72% weighted to lending to solar and wind generation assets, 13% to
other renewable generation assets, along with smaller drawn exposures to electricity generators and gentailers that are
more emissions-intensive.

Jun 24

SUSTAINABILITY

Our interim 2030 target is 105 kgCO2 /MWh which is a 53% reduction compared to a 2020 baseline. As at 30 June 2024,
the emissions intensity of our portfolio was 91 kgCO2 /MWh, a reduction of 131 kgCO2 /MWh compared to our 2020 baseline
and 33 kgCO2 /MWh compared to 30 June 2023. Our portfolio was also 73% below the reference scenario. The reduction in
our portfolio emissions intensity was primarily driven by lower exposures to non-renewable power generation customers, new
drawn lending to the renewables sector and changes in customer emissions. While the emissions intensity of our portfolio has
continued to decrease since June 2020, drawdowns of unused lending limits could change our share of customers’ emissions
and generation resulting in potential fluctuations in emissions intensity.

Our target, portfolio and progress

343

+3

CREATING VALUE

In the 2024 Integrated System Plan (ISP), AEMO’s step change scenario projects that up to 90% of the National Energy
Market’s coal-fired power stations will retire by 2035 with the remaining 10% retiring by 2040. Renewable energy connected
with transmission and distribution, firmed with storage and backed up by gas-powered generation, is the lowest-cost way to
supply electricity to homes and businesses as Australia transitions to a net zero economy.2

0.8

OVERVIEW

As at 30 June 2024, lending to the power generation sector represented only 0.5% of our in-scope drawn lending and 1.9%
of our financed emissions. In 2023, Scope 1 emissions from the power generation sector accounted for approximately 34% of
Australia’s total emissions.1 Retiring coal-fired power stations and shifting to renewable sources of energy is critical to reducing
the emissions intensity of Australia’s electricity grid. Renewable electricity can provide a cost-effective way of reducing
emissions for consumers and is not only essential for the nation to meet its decarbonisation ambitions, but also to support
Australian households and other sectors in reducing their emissions.

IEA NZE 2021 – Global pathway 2

Assumptions and risks in achieving our targets
The reference scenarios that inform our targets assume a gradual decline in demand for oil and gas, noting the need for
dispatchable capacity, which could include gas-powered firming generation, in the future. This decline is complemented by
a small share of emissions being captured through carbon capture, use and storage. 4 In Australia, reductions in emissions in
the oil and gas sector are assumed to be supported by the Federal Government’s Safeguard Mechanism, which incentivises
solutions to minimise Scope 1 emissions, including through carbon capture, use and storage.

Actions we are taking
We support customers in these sectors by providing lending in line with our E&S Framework. The Framework outlines the
minimum requirements we have set for financing activities for certain customers in these sectors. We also monitor the impact
of any proposed new transactions in these sectors on our 2030 upstream oil and gas extraction targets. Certain customers in
these sectors are also expected to have published a Transition Plan.
For important information on our actions and the commitments we have made to customers operating in these sectors, see pages 114–115.
See our refreshed 2025 E&S Framework for more information at commbank.com.au/policies

Our targets, portfolio and progress
• Our upstream oil extraction target is 1.9 MtCO2, a 27% reduction compared to our 2020

Actual

Jun 22

Upstream gas extraction absolute emissions 3 MtCO2 per annum

In AEMO’s 2024 ISP, as coal-fired power stations retire, gas-powered generation is expected to continue playing a role in
supporting Australia’s economic activity through the transition. Gas-powered electricity generation can be used to provide
firming support to variable renewable energy when other dispatchable sources are unavailable. Gas is also used in a range of
industrial applications where lower carbon alternatives are not yet commercially available, particularly those requiring heat over
500°C. AEMO’s 2025 Gas Statement of Opportunities forecasts shortfall risks under peak conditions from 2028, three years
later than forecast in 2024. AEMO’s update reinforces the need to complete committed and anticipated production, storage
and infrastructure developments, amongst other measures.

Since 2023, our in-scope drawn lending exposures to customers in these sectors have decreased
by 25%, or $0.1 billion.

Jun 21

CREATING VALUE

As at 30 June 2024, lending to the upstream oil and gas extraction sectors represented <0.1% of our in-scope drawn
lending and 1.9% of our financed emissions. In 2023, Scope 1 emissions from upstream oil and gas extraction accounted
for approximately 10% of Australia’s total emissions.1 These industries also have significant downstream emissions due to
the combustion of the products they produce.

Sector overview and outlook

In 2023, Scope 1 emissions from coal mining, including both thermal and metallurgical coal, accounted for approximately
8% of Australia’s total emissions. 1 Some mining companies that we may lend to retain thermal coal interests as part of their
diversified operations.

As at 30 June 2024, our shipping target covers 2% of our in-scope drawn lending to the transport
sector and largely includes lending to international customers. In 2023, shipping accounted for over
2% of global energy-related emissions.1 Australia is expected to continue relying on international
shipping given we have no significant domestic shipping network. Decarbonising this sector is
challenging, due to the reliance on fossil fuels and the lack of available low-carbon fuel alternatives.

IEA NZE 2021 – Global pathway 4

Assumptions and risks in achieving our target

Actions we are taking

The reference scenario that informs our target assumes low-carbon fuels,
such as bioenergy and ammonia, begin to account for at least 15% of energy
consumed by shipping by 2030.

We expect to achieve our 2030 target
through a managed exit of our international
institutional banking exposures. Any new
origination in the shipping sector is intended
to be focused on Australian transport, trade
and infrastructure.

Decarbonising the shipping sector is largely dependent on efficiency
improvements including a mixture of those already available, in addition to new
technologies being developed and switching to low-carbon fuels. Should the
scaling of low-carbon fuels not happen or not happen at the rate anticipated, or
if policy settings are not supportive, then achieving our target may be unlikely.

For more information on how we are supporting
our institutional and large corporate customers
to transition, see pages 114–115.

Our
performance

Since 2020, we have reduced our Scope 1 and
2 emissions by 72% from our 2020 baseline,
exceeding our 2025 target of a 21% reduction.
This has been driven by the purchase of
renewable electricity for our New Zealand and
other overseas Scope 2 emissions.

Since 2020, we have reduced our Scope 3 emissions
within the target by 36% from our 2020 baseline,
meeting our 2025 target of a 16.3% reduction. This
has been driven by freight-related emissions reducing
a further 29% this year. We continue to see benefits
from optimising our freight demand and partnering
with a supplier with a growing EV delivery fleet.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

We set our current Scope 1 and 2, and Scope 3 operational emissions targets in 2021. In 2024, our Scope 3 operational
emissions target was updated to align with a pathway to limit global warming to 1.5°C for all Scope 3 operational emissions
categories included with our target, except for air travel which remained aligned to a well-below 2°C pathway. We also
updated our Scope 3 baseline due to material business structure changes, including divestments, that had taken place since
developing the initial baseline.

2025 targets

SUSTAINABILITY

Since 2014, we have achieved a 96% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 operational emissions. As at 30 June 2025, we have
achieved our operational emissions targets set for 2025 with a Scope 1 and 2 reduction of 72%, and a Scope 3 reduction
of 36%, since 2020.

Scope 3 operational emissions

CREATING VALUE

Progress on our operational emissions reduction targets

Scope 1 & 2 operational emissions

OVERVIEW

Building on our progress and using insights from reducing our operational
emissions, we are now focused on reducing our supply chain emissions. This year
we have achieved our operational emissions targets for 2025 and also completed
a comprehensive review of our operational emissions strategy covering targets,
boundaries and inventories.

Our operational emissions (‘000s tCO2-e)

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Managing our
operational emissions

Scope 1 and 2 and Scope 3 operational emissions reduction targets

132

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

133

This year we completed an evaluation of our operational emissions strategy as part of our five-year review cycle. The review
considered our Scope 1 and 2, and Scope 3 reduction targets, boundaries and inventories, and incoming new mandatory
climate reporting requirements.

Supporting action outside our operations

Actions to help us reduce our supply chain emissions

Challenges in setting sector goals and
targets for Australian agriculture
The agriculture sector faces specific challenges in goal and target setting,
including measuring emissions, multiple gas sources, different agricultural
activities, and the way emissions are generated and stored through natural
systems. We continued to explore the possibility of setting financed emissions
goals and targets for the Australian agriculture sector, which were deferred in 2024
due to data and methodology challenges. As these challenges remain unresolved,
we have decided not to set goals or targets this year. We remain focused on helping
customers measure their baseline emissions and have updated our methodology
for measuring and reporting our financed emissions for the sector.

financed emissions

Key:

Very high

We also continue to take steps to reduce the impact of our office and branch fit-outs by making them more resource efficient.
Our refurbishment projects incorporate sustainable design principles, and we look to reuse and repurpose existing building
materials and furniture, wherever possible.

We also measure the impact of our offices via major projects aiming to achieve lower embodied carbon compared to
previous comparable buildings in our portfolio. This year we completed the Bankwest Place refurbishment in Perth, achieving
a 42% reduction in embodied carbon. This is compared to the estimated emissions for a typical fitout with conventional
material selections.

1 Refer to page 429 for source.
2 Based on our 2023 Exploring Natural Capital Opportunities, Risks and Exposure (ENCORE) analysis as published in our 2023 Climate Report.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Our three-year ATM refresh project, covering approximately 1,700 ATMs, is implementing a modular construction system
using lower-carbon, natural and renewable materials. A circular design approach is being taken, facilitating efficient installation
and easy disassembly for repairs and reuse. In addition, our latest container branch, designed to support communities after
natural disasters, considers sustainable design principles. The container itself has been reused, and approximately 70% of its
interior is fitted with lower-carbon and circular materials, such as wall linings made from upcycled packaging, recycled plastic
acoustic panelling and furniture, as well as lower-carbon carpet and joinery panels.

High
FINANCIAL REPORT

Air travel emissions represent 70% of the emissions in our current Scope 3 operational emissions target. Our efforts to date
have been directed towards mitigating our own air travel impacts and supporting lower-carbon alternative fuels. Our Australian
business monitors business travel and tracks actual emissions against internal carbon budgets to balance the demand for
business critical travel while focusing on meeting our Scope 3 target.

The Bank has been supporting the Australian agriculture sector for over
100 years, and we intend to continue supporting our farmers through our
lending and other activities. We recognise that our customers are at different
stages of adopting decarbonisation activities and responding to the transition.
We partner with companies like Ruminati to provide our farming customers
with complimentary tools to estimate their baseline emissions and develop
tailored action plans to help them reduce their on-farm emissions. Our aim
is to continue investing in research partnerships and programs to build our
understanding and share insights with our customers.

in-scope drawn lending

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

We continue to monitor developments related to the
ACCU scheme and note the review Climate Active is
currently undertaking. We will reassess our position as
more information becomes available.

Given air travel emissions are particularly hard to reduce,
CBA has been part of the Qantas SAF coalition for the
past two years. The coalition aims to support the scaling of
the SAF industry to reduce aviation emissions. During the
year we increased our level of participation to become a
‘partner’, which contributes to the airline’s incremental cost
of purchasing SAF. The use of SAF purchased through CBA’s
partnership would be equivalent to avoiding 1,000 tCO₂-e of
CBA’s air travel emissions.

However, many are exploring options to reduce net emissions for improved
profitability, enhancements to natural capital for production, and incentives in
environmental markets and schemes. Availability and costs to implement new
technologies, and balancing competing land uses in the agriculture industry,
continue to slow the pace of progress on emissions reductions for the sector.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

This year CBA signed several multi-year agreements
supporting improved social, cultural and environmental
impacts through projects within the ACCU scheme.
Our portfolio focuses on projects supporting Australia’s
Indigenous communities and aligns with our Reconciliation
Action Plan. This year CBA supported Indigenous savanna
burning projects which also have benefits for nature.
Selected projects went through a due diligence assessment,
to assess the integrity and credibility of the carbon
credits generated. This due diligence assessment was based
on the Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market’s
Core Carbon Principles and transparency changes to the
ACCU scheme.

In 2024, ASB pre-purchased carbon credits for 2025
sourced from GHG removal projects under the Permanent
Forest Sink Initiative (PFSI). In 2024, Toitū Envirocare
announced its commitment to align their practices with
international standards. As a result, they will be moving
towards no longer accepting carbon credits issued under the
New Zealand PFSI for offsetting purposes. Toitū Envirocare
honours the use of pre-purchased, ‘reserved’ New Zealand
credits. As part of the transition phase, ASB will apply 2024
pre-purchased credits sourced from New Zealand-based
PFSI compliant projects to maintain its Toitū Envirocare Net
Carbon Zero certification.

The Australian agriculture sector is essential for our economy and food security,
and is central to regional communities and the financial health of their economies.
The sector accounts for 18% of Australia’s net emissions, or 16% excluding
land-use, land-use change and forestry. 1 Landowners face risks including acute
weather events, a changing climate and loss of ecosystem services.

SUSTAINABILITY

We do not use carbon credits to meet our operational
emissions targets. However, we continue to support climate
action beyond our value chain, such as purchasing carbon
credits and investing to help reduce our travel emissions.

Continuing to support Australian agriculture

CREATING VALUE

We have also made several changes to improve how we measure, manage and represent Scope 3 operational emission
sources, particularly those associated with our supply chain. Where we do not currently have activity data available, we are
developing spend-based estimates. We have conservatively estimated our expanded footprint as an increase of approximately
600% from our reported 2024 operational emissions. As our current Scope 3 target accounts for only 13% of our expanded
estimated emissions, the existing Scope 3 target is no longer meaningful in its present form. Accordingly, we will reassess
this target and focus on more accurately quantifying our Scope 3 operational emissions in line with new mandatory climate
reporting requirements in 2026.

Australian agriculture is exposed to both climate- and nature-related risks and
opportunities given the emissions created, carbon sequestered into land, and the
impacts and dependencies to produce food and fibre for our economy.

OVERVIEW

Given the significant progress in executing our reduction strategies for Scope 1 and 2, we are increasing our reduction target
from 42% to 75% by 2030 against our 2020 baseline. We are also able to expand our Scope 1 and 2 target coverage from
2026 onwards as we now have better data for our other overseas offices, including India.

Nature and biodiversity
We continue to recognise the interconnection between nature and climate issues, and the
importance of Australia taking action to maintain, restore and enhance nature and biodiversity.

OVERVIEW

Progressing our approach to nature

Understanding our customers’ impacts and dependencies on nature
can help us manage the risks and opportunities these create. Our
2023 ENCORE analysis identified agriculture and mining as priority
sectors, as well as the built environment in relation to our operations.

We continued to build analytical capabilities to assess nature
impacts and dependencies for parts of our lending portfolio.
This includes taking opportunities to partner with organisations
who can support better measurement of nature-related metrics and
support customers in working towards improved nature outcomes.

The Board continues to oversee the
assessment of risks and opportunities
for the Bank arising from climate change.
The Board’s oversight of climate issues
The Board’s responsibilities include considering the material E&S impacts of the Bank’s activities.
Each of the Board’s four standing Committees assists the Board to carry out its responsibilities,
with the Audit and Risk & Compliance Committees primarily assisting the Board with its oversight
of climate issues.
The Board approves the Group E&S Policy and Framework, and oversees the management of
climate risk within the Bank’s overall business strategy and risk appetite. The Board also monitors
the E&S work program, which includes the development and delivery of CBA’s climate strategy and
progress against goals and targets. The Board and the Audit and Risk & Compliance Committees
regularly receive reports from management on climate-related matters. The contents, including
management’s recommendations, are noted, discussed and/or approved.
For important accompanying information on the role of the Board, its Committees and how information
flows to and from the Board, see page 38.
See our Board and Board Committee Charters at commbank.com.au/corporategovernance

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

We also participated in an Early Adopter Program with CSIRO to test
their new NatureIQ tool that combines multiple CSIRO environmental
indicators, models and datasets. The tool supports assessment and
monitoring of indicators such as ecosystem condition and species
extinction risk.

As a continuation of our embodied carbon
assessments, we engaged a third-party
consultant to conduct an assessment into
the impacts on nature from our retail fitout.
The assessment identified the highest
impact materials on nature as aluminium,
steel and timber and further assessment
of the supply chains of these materials
were carried out. We continue to explore
how these findings can be integrated into
design decisions.

FINANCIAL REPORT

This year we built on previous geospatial analysis and continued
to explore publicly available datasets and methodologies to assess
where our operations and lending activities intersect with nature.
This exercise identified challenges in timeliness and accuracy of
publicly available information on the location of relevant assets.
Through our climate stress test, we also explored exposure to water
stress for select regions in our agriculture portfolio.

We continued to monitor our water and
waste use which were identified as the
Taskforce on Nature-related Financial
Disclosures (TNFD) metrics most material
to our operations. As part of our ongoing
efforts to reduce waste to landfill, we trialled
reusable coffee cups, expanded the roll out
of coffee cup sorting stations, upgraded
our waste infrastructure and signage, and
introduced a dry waste stream into our
Sydney commercial offices this year.

nationally consistent definitions, metrics and datasets tailored to
the Australian context.

The Board and its Committees consider a range of climate-related topics where they are responsible
for oversight, including the E&S impact of the Bank’s activities. Board and Committee papers are prepared
by management. The Board and Committees consider the views of a range of stakeholders when reviewing
the papers. Agenda items may be considered in detail first by management at the E&S Committee or
by another management committee before the report is provided to the Board or Board Committee.
The table below is a non-exhaustive list of key climate matters discussed by the Board, and Audit and
Risk & Compliance Committees in the 2025 financial year.

The Board regularly reflects on our strategy, operating context, key priorities, and oversees progress and sets targets. In recent
years the Board has spent time discussing climate issues, including understanding CBA’s role and plans to support Australia’s
transition to net zero by 2050. The transition is an important issue to consider in the context of our strategy. We have used the
information currently available to us to understand the potential impacts, and acknowledge long-term outcomes on the Bank,
our customers and the economy.

Executive remuneration
Our Executive performance and remuneration frameworks
consider a variety of matters including climate. Executives’
remuneration outcomes are determined through the
assessment of collective and individual measures, which
include climate-related objectives where relevant as part of
the ‘Strategy Execution’ key performance indicator (KPI) for
short-term variable remuneration awards.
For 2025, the ‘Strategy Execution’ KPI has a minimum
weighting of 25%, for relevant Executives’ scorecards.
While there is no set weighting for specific climate-related
measures, execution and delivery of those climate-related
measures will impact the overall outcome of the ‘Strategy

Execution’ KPI. The Board can also exercise discretion to
adjust individual and/or collective outcomes, including
discretion to apply malus clawback provisions in the event
of underperformance.
As part of determining performance and remuneration
outcomes, the four Board Committees met concurrently
to review each Executive’s individual and collective
accountability. For 2025, continued adherence
to the E&S Framework has been incorporated into
our risk and reputation modifier to maintain a strong
link to remuneration outcomes.

For further information on executive remuneration, see pages 182–218 of the Remuneration report.
