The Board has delegated the management of the Bank to the CEO, except for certain powers retained by the Board or
delegated to others including Board and Management Committees. Once the Board has approved the strategic objectives and
risk appetite of the Bank, which includes our climate strategy, the CEO is accountable for execution. The CEO then delegates
authority to the Group Executives for the effective execution of the Bank’s operational activities.

Chief Executive Officer
Management of the Bank, including executing and delivering our strategy; and Chair of the Non-Financial Risk Committee
(NFRC) and E&S Committee.

Governance committees within the Bank, such as risk management committees, product governance forums and
transaction-level committees, support the Board’s oversight and the Executive Leadership Team’s (ELT’s) management
of climate-related risks and opportunities. Each of these committees and forums have charters that govern and outline roles
and responsibilities. Climate-related financial risks and opportunities can be escalated through these reporting structures.
Governance processes are supported by the application of a range of internal policies, standards and procedures that
determine the way we deliver our products and services. For example, our Product Development and Distribution Standard
embeds consideration of E&S product features into the product governance process, which includes oversight of changes
to product features and periodic reviews through an E&S lens.

CREATING VALUE

Chief Financial Officer
Reporting obligations, including our Annual Report; environmental policy settings captured in our E&S Policy; overseeing and
reporting on our performance and progress towards goals and targets; executing our property strategy and managing the
facilities in our Australia-based buildings; and management of our supplier framework, including relevant policies.

SUSTAINABILITY

Chief Risk Officer
Creating and maintaining our risk appetite statement (RAS) and preparing the Board-approved RAS, including RAS indicators;
ensuring an appropriate Risk Management Framework (RMF) is in place to manage our risks; ensuring appropriate processes
and tools are in place to strengthen our risk culture; shares responsibility with business units for certain credit decisions 1; and
Chair of the Financial Risk Committee (FRC).
Group Executives for Retail Banking Services (RBS), Business Banking (BB) and Institutional Banking and Markets (IB&M)
Products and services offered to our customers, which can include designing and distributing products or services with E&S
features; shares responsibility with the Chief Risk Officer for certain credit decisions 2; creating and maintaining the RAS for
their business unit; and ensuring the E&S Policy is adhered to in their business unit, including delivering actions and allocating
resources in line with our commitments.

Key areas of Executive Leadership Team discussion during 2025

The E&S Committee oversees the E&S strategy, governance and priorities for implementation
which includes climate-related matters. In preparation for Board approval, the Committee
discussed and endorsed the E&S Policy and updated Framework following input from
impacted business and support units as part of the biennial review.

For important accompanying information on our management committees, see page 63.
1 Shared responsibility for credit decisions on transactions with Group Executives of BB, IB&M and RBS (as it relates to Commonwealth Private customers) in
accordance with applicable Group policies and standards.
2 The Group Executive RBS shares responsibility for credit decisions with the Chief Risk Officer as it relates to Commonwealth Private customers. All other credit
decisions within RBS are retained by the Group Executive.

Transaction oversight for Institutional
Banking and Markets customers

In RBS, we take a portfolio managed approach to E&S
governance, implementing this for products, tools or features
that we provide to customers, rather than through individual
customer ESG risk assessments. For new CBA product
offerings with E&S features, including the Home Energy
Upgrades service, the Bank carefully considered the potential
impacts to our conduct, strategic and credit risks. Before
being sent, customer communications are reviewed to check
environmental claims are accurate and can be supported.

In IB&M, lending customers are relationship managed and
supported directly by our bankers. As part of the corporate
lending process, ESG risk assessments are completed for
institutional customers, and are reviewed and refreshed
annually. IB&M operates two key forums that support
consideration of E&S risks and other reputational risks.
These review forums, which involve senior management
from within and outside IB&M, include an E&S Deal
Review (EDR) and the IB&M Commitments Committee.

Our approach to transaction
governance in Business Banking

Before credit approval, a proposed transaction is escalated to
EDR if it meets certain conditions, including if the customer’s
activities are captured in the relevant fossil fuel financingrelated commitments in our E&S Framework; the transaction
may impact a sector-level target and/or exposure reporting
for certain sectors; or the ESG risk assessment results
in a heightened ESG risk rating. Where appropriate, the
EDR can also escalate a proposed transaction to the IB&M
Commitments Committee, particularly where it may impact
the Bank’s non-financial risks, reputational risk, strategy or
values. If the transaction is supported for approval, it then
proceeds through the credit approval pathway.

In BB, our approach to climate-related governance is determined
by the size of a customer’s current or proposed commercial
or corporate lending. Customers with greater than or equal to
$1.5 million in lending require an ESG risk assessment. The
majority of these customers are relationship managed by our
bankers. Depending on the customer’s ESG risk profile, or where
additional oversight is required, ESG risk assessments may
be escalated to senior management or the BB Commitments
Committee. Matters escalated to the BB Commitments
Committee are noted at the E&S Committee and may include
those subject to commitments in our E&S Framework.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Environmental &
Social Committee

The NFRC reviews and monitors the effectiveness of non-financial risk aspects of
the RAS. It also assesses emerging risks and trends, oversees the Bank’s regulatory
relationships, and reports significant risk matters to the Board or its Committees,
including those related to climate. In June 2025, the Committee noted an update on
the implementation of Australian mandatory climate reporting, which included key
actions addressed in 2025 and plans for 2026. Following this discussion, an update
was provided to the Audit Committee for oversight.

Product governance forums to support
Retail Banking Services

FINANCIAL REPORT

Non–Financial
Risk Committee

The FRC reviews and monitors our financial risk frameworks and key financial risk policies.
It assesses new financial risks and trends, oversees stress testing outcomes, and reports
significant financial risk issues to the Board, including those related to climate. Before
reporting to the Board, the Committee reviewed the results of the annual climate risk
materiality assessment which assessed the current and anticipated climate-related
effects on the Bank’s material risk types under three severe, but plausible,
climate scenarios.

Our customer-facing business units have controls and risk mitigation strategies in place. Examples include sector-level
financed emissions goals and targets for lending to certain sectors and ESG risk assessments; an ESG Credit Standard to
consider climate risk in credit risk assessments and the management of credit portfolios; scenario analysis to assess the
physical and transition risks of climate change on the Bank; supplier E&S due diligence processes; and a Supplier Code of
Conduct to support adherence to CBA’s E&S Framework.

Considering climate-related opportunities for the Bank is a responsibility for the Board and all employees, with individual
opportunities predominantly identified by our customer-facing business units – RBS, BB and IB&M. Where appropriate,
climate-related opportunities may be escalated to an appropriate management committee, or the Board, for consideration
or approval.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Reporting against our fossil fuel financing-related commitments
We have a commitment to report annually on our compliance with the fossil fuel financing-related commitments outlined in
our E&S Framework. This applies in respect of certain oil and/or gas producing, thermal coal, metallurgical coal mining and
power generation clients. For these commitments, a power generation client is identified as an entity with an ANZSIC code for
electricity generation or electricity supply which also has power generation activities. IB&M and BB have used governance
processes similar to, or the same as, those described above to support compliance with these commitments. Based on those
processes and other checks we have completed, we have complied with the fossil fuel financing-related commitments in our
E&S Framework.

For more information on our approach to managing risks and risk governance, see pages 60–63.

• Obstacles in financing new industries could result
in a competitive disadvantage and revenue decline
in the long term.

Non-financial risk drivers
• Our branches, ATM network, payments processing
infrastructure and the data centres we use could
be impacted by extended outages from severe
weather events.
• Changes in our policies and practices to limit the Bank’s
exposure to climate-related risks could result in the
perception of unfair treatment to segments of customers.
• Rapidly changing climate regulations could lead to
compliance failures by the Bank or our suppliers,
leading to penalties or legal action.

Financial risk drivers

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

• Climate transition – New climate change or energy
policies, technological advancements, or changing
community expectations could reduce the business model
viability of certain business or institutional customers. This
could impact their ability to repay loans, with the severity
of potential credit losses to the Bank increasing due to
declining asset values held as collateral. Unemployment
could also increase and home values could decline in
regions dependent on these businesses, resulting in
increased losses to the Bank from retail customers.

FINANCIAL REPORT

• Physical climate changes – More frequent, severe
weather events and long-term changes in climate
patterns could cause damage or decline in asset values,
impacting the ability of households and businesses
to repay their loans. The severity of credit losses to
the Bank may increase due to declining asset values
held as collateral, especially for customers in high-risk
areas without adequate insurance, or who are facing
prolonged unemployment or business disruption.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

We also perform transition and physical risk scenario
analyses to evaluate the resilience of the Bank’s strategy
and business model to climate-related changes,
developments and uncertainties. Our climate scenario
analysis, combined with our climate risk materiality
assessment (CRMA), helps us understand our exposure
to strategic, financial and non-financial risks from climate
change, as well as our risks and opportunities over the short,
medium and long term. We continue to enhance our climate
risk management tools and practices in line with evolving
industry practices, and regulatory expectations.

• Inadequate climate commitments or lending to
companies facing reputational challenges could damage
the Bank’s reputation and exclude us from opportunities
in the global and domestic transition economy.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

We use a range of tools and techniques to help us identify,
assess, manage and monitor climate-related risks holistically
at the Group level, and on a portfolio basis or for individual
customers in certain circumstances. This approach helps us
explore the physical and transition risks from climate change
that may impact our business and customers over different time
horizons. It also helps us consider how our business activities
can impact the environment, our customers and the community.

• Our commitment to support Australia’s transition
to net zero by 2050 could be impacted by global
economic and geopolitical changes.

SUSTAINABILITY

The Bank manages risks, including climate-related risks,
through our RMF. The RMF is reviewed by management,
the Risk & Compliance Committee and the Board to
accommodate changes in our operating environment,
better practices, and regulatory and community
expectations. Climate-related risks are embedded in the
Board-approved risk taxonomy under our E&S risk type.
E&S risk is integrated into the Bank’s governance structures,
policies, risk appetite and strategic planning processes.
E&S risk includes climate- and nature-related impacts, and

represents drivers of potentially material strategic,
financial and non-financial risks to the Bank.

Strategic and reputational risk drivers

CREATING VALUE

Climate change has been identified as a risk that could have
a material impact on stakeholders within our value chain and
expose the Bank to both physical and transition risks. While
our current analysis shows that climate change is unlikely to
have material financial impacts on the Bank in the short term,
failure to respond adequately to the potential and expected
impacts of climate change could affect our long-term
strategy, performance and franchise value. We acknowledge
some of our customers, communities and regions will face
greater climate impacts and risks than others, and we remain
well positioned to support them.

As a bank that provides deposit and lending services to Australian retail, business
and institutional customers, our business and value chain may be exposed to
climate-related risks and opportunities from a number of sources, including the
customers and communities we support and serve.

OVERVIEW

Delivering our climate strategy requires an understanding of the
different ways climate-related risks could impact our business, and
how our business activities could impact the climate, our customers,
people and communities. The Bank’s embedded RMF, culture and
Code of Conduct, guide our people to manage climate-related risks
and opportunities.

opportunities are concentrated

Our home loans portfolio is geographically spread across Australia. Homeowners in some areas are highly exposed to acute
physical risk and severe weather events such as floods, cyclones and bushfires. This year we sought to more accurately
quantify our potential exposure to extreme weather events using a case study of severe cyclones impacting South-East
Queensland (QLD).
We continue to develop and enhance our view of acute physical climate risk by exploring new datasets and improving our
understanding of the pathways for credit losses as well as the implications of underinsurance in the home loans portfolio.
In the event our customers are affected by natural disasters, such as bushfires, floods or storms, they may be able to access
our emergency assistance support options.
To read about our South-East QLD severe cyclone scenario analysis, see page 152.

To help us effectively manage our climate-related risks, we monitor the impact of severe weather events and natural disasters
on our business and customers, including in our home lending portfolio. We use third-party datasets and model providers to
help enable a more complete view of the potential risk exposures across our portfolios. This year our analysis indicated CBA
exposures of:

OVERVIEW

Our assessment of the Bank’s financial exposure to climate-related risk considers the extent to which regions or industries we
lend to are currently exposed to elevated physical and/or transition risks. The table below identifies sub-sectors, primarily based
on ANZSIC classifications within our portfolio, which are currently exposed to elevated levels of climate-related physical or
transition risk. This year we have assessed the resilience of 47% and 89% of our lending portfolios to potential climate-related
physical and transition risks, respectively. The table also shows where the Bank’s climate-related physical and transition risks are
concentrated within our lending portfolio, and provides context for senior management when assessing the impact of climate
change through our CRMA.

Climate-related risk can amplify other risk types. Our approach aims to integrate the
identification, assessment and management of climate-related risks into existing
business activities to help us understand and manage any changes to our strategic,
financial and non-financial risks.

E&S Policy and Framework

SUSTAINABILITY

Purpose

CREATING VALUE

We use a range of tools and techniques to help us identify, assess, manage and monitor climate-related risks and opportunities
to our strategy, our portfolios, and at an individual supplier and customer level where certain criteria are met. This year we
continued to enhance our climate risk management tools and practices in line with evolving industry practices and regulatory
expectations. The table below outlines the key policies, processes and tools in place to help us identify, assess, manage and
monitor our risks and opportunities.

More frequent, severe weather events and long-term changes in climate patterns can cause damage or decline in asset values,
impacting the ability of households and businesses to repay their loans. The ability of some businesses to repay loans could
also be impacted by a decline in the viability of their business models and profitability. This could be due to new climate change
and energy policies, technological advancements, or changing community expectations that impact demand for their products
or services. Unemployment could also increase in regions dependent on these businesses, resulting in increased losses to the
Bank from retail customers. Physical and transition factors could also decrease the value of the assets held as collateral by the
Bank, resulting in more severe credit losses when a default occurs. It is important we assess and understand the impacts to
help us manage our risks effectively.

OVERVIEW

ESG risk assessment tool
Our ESG risk assessment tool plays an important role in our
business and institutional lending processes by assisting our
bankers to:

• assess the mitigating actions that our customers take
to manage their ESG risks
• assess how lending to our customers aligns to the
commitments made in our E&S Framework.

We aim to use scenarios that:
• are considered credible global climate scenarios
• have plausible and appropriately severe outcomes

Our long-term climate scenarios are based on the Network
for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios. This
is a set of scenarios, developed by an international network
of central banks and supervisors, to provide a common and

The Board reviews and approves the RAS indicators annually.
In the latest review, the Board increased the ‘early warning’
and ‘intervention’ levels for the institutional RAS indicator
due to a methodology change, not an increased risk appetite.

The NGFS scenarios incorporate the latest climate science
findings, recent economic and climate data, country-level
policy commitments to reach net zero emissions, and the
latest trends in renewable energy and mitigation technologies.
We prioritise our scenario analysis work based on the size of
our lending portfolios, their exposure to climate-related risks
and our ability to conduct analyses based on the availability
of data. We recognise that climate scenario techniques and
data are constantly evolving and maturing, and we may
change our selection over time.
For more detail on our scenario analysis approach including
assumptions and limitations, see pages 406–414 in the Climate
disclosures appendix.

FINANCIAL REPORT

For home loans, our indicator measures the percentage of
loans with high loan-to-value ratios and exposure to climate
physical risks like floods, fires and cyclones. This helps monitor
the likelihood of higher credit losses if property values fall in
areas experiencing extreme weather. For institutional lending,
the indicator tracks the percentage of loans to customers with
high climate transition risk. This assesses the potential for

We use a combination of scenarios to explore the impact of
individual severe physical and transition events that could
impact the Bank in any given year, as well as long-term
climate scenarios to understand impacts to the Bank under
different futures exposed to high transition or physical risks.

• are consistent with market practices

Risk appetite statement
The Board-approved RAS defines the maximum risk allowable
to deliver the Bank’s strategic priorities. Management is
required to operate within risk appetite. We have integrated
two quantitative RAS indicators to help minimise potential
credit losses from portfolios with higher exposure to
climate-related risks. These focus on physical risk in the
retail portfolio and transition risk in the institutional portfolio.

How we prioritise the scenarios we run

Number of lending assessments
completed 2

Business Banking 1

Climate-related risk is highly uncertain. We perform scenario analysis, as needed, to understand the potential future impacts
of climate-related risks on our financial position, performance, cash flows and business model. It may also help us make
decisions to manage our risks which may include updates to policies, changes to risk appetite settings, portfolio limits or
exclusions and consideration in capital planning. We acknowledge some of our customers, communities and regions will
face greater climate impacts and risks than others, and we remain well positioned to support them.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

The tool is supported by a set of inherent risk ratings across
industry ANZSIC codes for nine key focus areas: climate
and energy; climate physical risk; nature impact; nature
dependency; human rights; labour rights and modern
slavery; Indigenous rights; workplace health and safety;

Climate scenario analysis

• identify and assess the ESG risks that our customers
are exposed to

and anti-corruption and governance. The tool also includes
specific questions aimed at assessing whether lending
to a customer is aligned to the commitments in our E&S
Framework. The tool directs bankers to obtain appropriate
business and credit risk approvals. Decisions on lending
transactions may be escalated to senior management or
business unit governance committees if the assessment
requires further review against the E&S Framework or other
ESG risk indicators. These escalation pathways support
our bankers to make risk-based decisions that consider
potential ESG issues. For relevant project-related finance
transactions we follow the Equator Principles process
requirements. In 2025, we completed more than 14,0001
ESG risk assessments, with 46 escalated to business unit
governance committees and noted at the E&S Committee.

Our climate scenario analysis, combined with our climate risk
materiality assessment, helps us assess the resilience of the
Bank’s business model and strategy to climate-related changes,
developments and uncertainties.

CREATING VALUE

We have not currently identified a globally accepted, standardised methodology for quantifying climate-related impacts on credit
risk. Therefore, our ESG credit assessments continue to be driven by customer due diligence and qualitative assessments in
processes tailored to the risk of specific portfolios. We expect our approach to integrating ESG into our credit risk framework
will continue to evolve and adapt as industry practices mature, and as regulatory and stakeholder expectations change.

Resilience of our business
to climate change

OVERVIEW

We have explicitly incorporated the consideration of ESG risk assessments into our credit risk framework through our ESG
Credit Standard. The standard provides a structured framework for the way in which E&S risks are to be considered in
credit risk assessment processes for our customers. Along with all our other group-wide credit standards, it establishes the
minimum credit expectations and requirements for identifying, managing and monitoring a borrower or counterparty’s E&S
risks. The standard, which is applicable across the Bank, requires that existing or new non-retail borrowers or counterparties
that meet certain criteria be considered against the commitments in the Bank’s E&S Framework when performing a credit
assessment. Throughout the credit process, E&S risks identified are assessed for their impact on our customers’ ability to
repay. In some cases, the standard also requires reviewing the borrower’s collateral they pledge to the Bank for E&S risks.

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

ESG Credit Standard

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

151

Outcomes of our long-term scenarios

Long-term scenarios

Outcomes under all three long-term scenarios indicate the Bank’s business model remains resilient, and reinforces our
approach to supporting Australia’s transition to net zero by 2050 and building climate resilience. While severe weather
may cause substantial losses in some years in the long term, the financial impacts of climate-related risks are not currently
considered material to the Bank. This is based on modelling which shows minimal impact on loan impairment expenses and
average operating profit over time.

OVERVIEW

This year we performed three long-term climate scenarios based on the NGFS scenarios. They explore futures with high
transition risk (net zero 2050), higher transition risk (delayed transition) and higher physical risk (current policies). These three
NGFS scenarios were selected to enable the assessment of a range of plausible climate physical and transition risk outcomes.
To date, global progress against the goals of the Paris Agreement has lagged, increasing risk of the net zero 2050 scenario
being out of reach. The delayed transition and current policies scenarios were selected as the Bank believes slower global
progress in climate policy increases the probability of either scenario occurring. The NGFS scenario macroeconomic forecasts
were tailored to the Australian context by an external economic advisory firm (Oxford Economics), and then modelled using
the Bank’s suite of stress testing models to determine balance sheet and credit loss results extending out to 2050.

Net zero 2050 and delayed transition

Delayed transition

Current policies

Net zero 2050 limits global warming
to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels by
2100 through immediate introduction
of stringent climate policies and
innovation, reaching global net zero
CO2 emissions around 2050. This
leads to high transition risks.

Assumes global annual emissions
do not decrease until 2030. Strong
policies are introduced globally after
2030 but differ across countries and
regions. Warming is limited to ~1.7°C
above pre-industrial levels by 2100
but leads to higher transition risks.

Assumes climate action is limited to
currently implemented government
policies. Global emissions continue to
rise until 2080, and the world warms
to ~3°C above pre-industrial levels by
2100. This results in more frequent
and extreme climate events.

impact to Australia is minimised by high levels of renewables
penetration in the Australian electricity grid at that stage.
The cash rate is assumed to remain low under these scenarios,
supporting sustained growth in house prices. This limits
average credit losses for the Bank due to the continued
strong average loan-to-value ratio of borrowers. However,
some industries and regions are more impacted than others
under these higher transition risk scenarios, such as mining,
utilities, manufacturing and agriculture, and regional areas
exposed to coal and gas extraction industries.
The net zero 2050 scenario has the better outcome for the
Bank in the long term as the orderly nature of the transition
results in higher growth and lower credit losses. The delayed
transition scenario has slower growth between 2035 to 2045
due to the disorderly nature of the transition before recovering.

Air and road transport (0.8%)

Under the current policies future, the economy improves in
the short term as Australia continues to export fossil fuel
products. However, in the long term, inflation increases and
economic productivity declines as capital is used to rebuild
after disasters, rather than being used to grow the economy.
Capital intensive industries such as agriculture, transport and
manufacturing are impacted most due to continuous and
compounding damages. House prices remain high due to
continued housing supply shortages which are exacerbated by
more frequent severe weather events that damage property
at a time when construction is in high demand. While the Bank

Current policies

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

The economic impacts are relatively mild under both scenarios
because declines in coal and gas exports are assumed to be
partially offset by increased demand for Australia’s critical
minerals. While the delayed transition scenario assumes
sharp global increases in emissions pricing from 2030, the

Transition risk
Changes relative to other scenarios:

The introduction of strong climate policies, immediately
under net zero 2050, and after 2030 under a delayed
transition, are assumed to increase production costs and
reduce demand for emissions-intensive industries such as
coal and gas mining, manufacturing and livestock farming.
This results in a drag on the economy and leads to moderate
increases in unemployment, which are felt more immediately
under the net zero 2050 scenario. However, the net zero
2050 future requires less aggressive government policies
allowing the economy to more gradually transition away
from emissions-intensive industries over a longer timeframe.

The scenarios we performed this year

Coal mining (0.1%)

0 to -5%

-5% to -10%

<-10%

The purpose of these NGFS-aligned scenarios is to identify impact trends that climate change could have over time on the broader
economy, certain industry sectors and ultimately the Bank. The scenarios do not attempt to predict the timing and severity of
individual acute physical or abrupt transition events that could impact the Bank’s financial performance in any given year. Rather,
the cumulative effects of such events are factored into macroeconomic variables used to model these trends. Although this
approach enables a clearer view of any trends over time, it likely underestimates potential losses to the Bank because it does not
account for localised declines, or direct impacts on, asset values from severe climate physical or transition events that could occur
in any given year. Therefore, three acute, event-driven scenarios were conducted to supplement this view (see pages 152–153).

Long term
5 year loss rate <0.6%, <0.2% increase to 5 year historical loss rate 2
5 year loss rate 0.6% to 0.8%, 0.2% to 0.4% increase to 5 year historical loss rate 2
5 year loss rate >0.8%, >0.4% increase to 5 year historical loss rate 2
1 Although average credit losses remain limited, substantial credit losses may arise in any given year from more extreme weather events.
2 Historical loss rate refers to the 5 year loss rate over the period 2020–2024 of 0.4%.

These three acute scenarios were selected as they are considered severe, but plausible climate-related
events that could impact the Bank’s profitability and cash flows in any given year.

Assumes a severe drought across
large areas of Australia lasting for
10 years. There is a simultaneous rise
in interest rates due to unrelated

Assumes two ageing coal-fired
power stations in NSW become
inoperable for up to six months.
This results in rolling blackouts
which impact productivity of high
energy usage industries.

Although not modelled in this scenario, losses would likely
be further exacerbated if impacts to nature are considered.
Prolonged drought conditions degrade nature and critical
ecosystem services required by farming systems. For example,
drought can cause a loss of soil and sediment retention and
loss of vegetation that reduces soil quality and increases risk of
soil erosion, desertification and soil salinity, further impacting
agricultural productivity and resilience.

Extended electricity outage in New South Wales

Cyclone over Brisbane
Why we ran this scenario
AEMO noted in the 2024 ISP that 90% of Australia’s coalfired power generators are forecast to retire by 2035, and
are becoming less reliable and more difficult to maintain.
Investment is needed urgently and new generation, storage
and firming must be in place before coal-fired power stations
retire. We ran this scenario to enhance our understanding of
the potential losses to the Bank from a long-term electricity
outage caused by two coal-fired power stations becoming
simultaneously inoperable.

Key scenario assumptions
The scenario assumes two ageing NSW coal-fired power
stations, responsible for approximately 40% of NSW
electricity supply, become simultaneously inoperable for
six months, leading to energy price increases and rolling
blackouts. Wholesale electricity prices are assumed to
increase fourfold, with retail prices increasing from year two
by 30%. Electricity supply is assumed to be prioritised for

residential and commercial businesses, however high energy
consuming businesses, particularly in manufacturing, are
required to reduce production. The scope of this exercise
was limited to manufacturing industries and the economic
impacts on retail customers. This is based on the assumption
that electricity would be prioritised to other high electricity
usage sectors such as retail, commercial and services,
electricity, gas, water and waste services.

Insights from our analysis
This combination of increased wholesale electricity prices
and reduced production leads to estimated credit losses
to the Bank from the manufacturing sector of below $200
million over five years. The greatest losses arise from the
manufacturing sub-industries of transport equipment,
machinery and equipment, food and chemical manufacturing.
Energy price increases of 30% in retail do not drive material
retail credit losses for the Bank, with losses estimated to be
below $100 million driven by a slight economic downturn.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Total losses to the Bank modelled in this scenario are
approximately $1.5 billion over a five-year period, with more
than 75% of the losses occurring in the first two years. These
losses arise from home loan exposures in impacted areas of
approximately $91 billion and non-retail exposures of $16 billion.
Losses are approximately double in the retail portfolio compared
to non-retail. Mortgage losses are driven by borrower repayment
stress after accounting for insurance, coupled with property
price declines in impacted areas. Non-retail losses are driven by
downtime, supply chain disruptions and economic decline that
culminates after the second event. The largest non-retail losses

Out of scope

FINANCIAL REPORT

Insights from our analysis

Total losses to the Bank over the 10 years are assessed to be below
$500 million, split relatively evenly between farming – mainly grain
growing, mixed farming and beef cattle farming – and agriculture
adjacent industries, mainly from food manufacturing and farm
produce wholesaling. The maximum loss in any individual year under
this scenario does not exceed $100 million.

Low water risk areas

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

The scenario assumes two severe cyclones (Category 4) make
landfall over South-East QLD a year apart. First over the
Sunshine Coast and then Brisbane. Each of these Category 4
cyclones are currently individually assessed as 1-in-200-year
events, however the probability of two severe cyclone events
in successive years in this location is currently extremely low.
This scenario results in significant property damage from
wind, storm surges and inland cyclone-induced flooding. This
is exacerbated where properties are not built to withstand
Category 4 cyclone winds. Limited government support is
assumed. Supply chain disruptions and downtime impact
business productivity. Following the back-to-back cyclones,
property prices decline in impacted areas and the events lead
to an economic decline in QLD with unemployment rates
increasing materially, particularly in regional areas.

arise from commercial property and wholesale trade. Although
not modelled under this scenario, losses could also be expected
from personal loans and credit cards, at similar rates to those
modelled under the economic downturn stress tests. Although
this represents an increase in single year credit losses, it is
currently not considered to be financially material to the Bank
due to its low probability of occurring and is mitigated by our
existing credit provisions. An event of this magnitude would also
not impact our current capital or liquidity RAS indicators.

Insights from our analysis

High water risk areas

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Key scenario assumptions

Key scenario assumptions

SUSTAINABILITY

Series of severe cyclones over South-East Queensland

Research indicates that large, populated areas across
South-East QLD and Northern NSW may experience more
severe cyclones in the future as cyclones begin to track further
south. We ran this scenario to enhance our understanding
of the potential losses to the Bank from an extreme weather
event that may become increasingly plausible over time.

Water risk score for in-scope postcodes

Droughts have been a feature of the Australian landscape throughout
history and can have severe impacts on the environment, industries,
economy and people. Climate change is increasing temperatures and
affecting rainfall, evaporation and climate systems in Australia, with
climate projections indicating that droughts may be more severe and
last longer. We ran this scenario to enhance our understanding of
the potential losses to the Bank should a long-term drought lead to a
substantial decline in farm values.

The scenario assumes a severe drought lasting for 10 years across
parts of the Murray-Darling Basin and Western Australia. The
scenario assumes farm values decline in the high-drought impact
areas, the drought occurs during a high interest rate environment
for reasons unrelated to the drought and the effectiveness of
government support declines over the scenario.

• Current term (within the next 12 months).
• Short term (~3 years) to align with the Bank’s strategic
planning cycle.
• Medium term (~10 years) to align with the timeframe of
when physical impacts begin to diverge for the low versus
high warming futures.

The risk ratings indicate the impact of climate change on the
relevant risk type under each scenario. For financial risks and
capital adequacy risk, ratings were determined with reference
to the relevant Board approved RAS. For non-financial, E&S
and AI risks our 5x5 risk matrix was applied.

We acknowledge that available data and techniques to
perform this type of assessment are still maturing across
the industry. As a result, our assessment of the impacts
of climate-related risks involves a greater reliance on
assumptions than is typically required for more mature risk
types, where historical data is a better predictor of future
events. As data and assessment techniques improve over
time, we anticipate that our future CRMAs will incorporate
more advanced assessment techniques.

For information on our long-term and acute scenarios, see pages 150–151 and 152–153, respectively.

To read about the climate scenarios we used and the inputs, uncertainty and limitations of climate scenario analysis, see pages 406–414 in the
Climate disclosures appendix.
1 This assessment is informed by scenario analysis, which considers hypothetical future events with highly uncertain outcomes. It does not represent a forecast of
future events. Scenario analysis has inherent limitations due to the number of judgements, assumptions and estimations used. While best efforts have been made
to use reasonable estimates and provide readers with the assumptions in our calculations, a high degree of uncertainty remains in the results of our analysis.
This assessment also considered the frameworks, processes and control environment currently in place without any material changes to the business model or
strategies in response to the future risk drivers captured in these scenarios.
2 The environmental strategic risk type (sub-risk of E&S strategic risk type) is defined as the risk that setting and executing the Bank’s environmental strategy,
policy and program does not adequately: consider and address the impacts that our business activities may have on the climate and natural environment; or how
the changing climate or natural environment may impact our franchise value, business model or profitability.

7

FINANCIAL REPORT

Risk appetite statement – Risk ratings were set based on the
expected position relative to the relevant Board approved RAS
thresholds. For example, a financial impact resulting in the Bank
being outside risk appetite would be rated as ‘very high risk’.

Each risk was assessed based on its impact to the Bank
in the time horizon in which the relevant scenario occurs,
rather than cumulative impacts over all time horizons. When
assessing impacts for future time horizons under these
scenarios, no additional material changes to the business
model or control environment were assumed beyond those
already in place. For example, while a potential strategic shift
could mitigate some risks in the future, such changes were
not factored into the future risk ratings.

2

portfolio where home loans can be contractually agreed
to for up to 30 years.

For example, a medium risk rating could arise from an event
expected to occur once every 10 years, with a significant
financial impact of $25 million to $100 million. Alternatively,
a medium risk rating could also arise from multiple events
expected to occur in the next year, but with a minor impact
of $1 million to $5 million.

The anticipated effects on the Bank under these scenarios
were considered across four time horizons:

• Impacts uses a combination of qualitative factors and
financial measures. Qualitative factors consider impacts
on customers, our operations and people, shareholders,
communities, and regulatory and legal. The quantitative
financial impacts range from negligible ≤$1 million; minor
>$1 million; major >$5 million; significant >$25 million; or
critical >$100 million.

SUSTAINABILITY

• The long-term scenarios provided a perspective on

5x5 risk matrix – Our risk matrix, updated in 2025,
considers the impact and expected frequency of a risk
eventuating, resulting in risks being rated as either low,
moderate, medium, high or very high risk. Impact and

Anticipated effects under these scenarios 1

CREATING VALUE

The CRMA assesses how current and anticipated
climate-related risks and opportunities arising from each
of the futures outlined in our long-term scenarios, may
impact the Bank’s material risk types, strategy, business
model and value chain across the current, short, medium
and long term. The assessment of impacts to our material
non-financial and strategic risk types was performed through
workshops with senior management and risk type owners,
including ASB. The assessment of financial risk impacts
incorporated outcomes from both the long-term and acute
scenarios performed this year:

The CRMA indicates that our business model and strategy is expected to remain resilient under all three long-term scenarios,
reinforcing the Bank’s commitment to supporting Australia’s transition to net zero by 2050 and building climate resilience.
In some years, due to extreme weather events, the Bank may experience moderate increases in credit losses or be exposed
to some high-rated strategic and non-financial risks. However, the Bank’s business model and strategy are not expected to
require material changes as a result of long-term changes in weather patterns or transition-related factors.

OVERVIEW

The CRMA describes the current and anticipated effects of climate-related risks and
opportunities on the resilience of the Bank’s strategy, business model and value chain.

Conduct risks may progressively heighten due to decisions
the Bank may need to make in relation to lending practices in
high physical risk zones that could be perceived to be unfair
to certain groups of customers.

The Bank may need to manage through uncertainty arising
from industry, government and regulatory responses
to possible challenges such as declining levels of home
insurance and continued construction in high physical risk
zones. The Bank may also be called on to provide greater
support to business and institutional customers seeking

The increased frequency of more severe weather events
could also lead to growing expectations in relation to
the Bank’s approach to identification and assessment
of physical risks, which may require the Bank to develop
new data, climate modelling capabilities, products and/or
services for customers. Severe weather events could also
impact the Bank’s operations, the operations of third parties
or the electricity grid, leading to an extended disruption of
our operations.

Delayed transition scenario
Under this scenario, the Bank’s business model and strategy
is expected to remain resilient.
The long-term macroeconomic scenario analysis we
performed this year indicates the business model of the
Bank and our long-term operating profit margins will not
be materially impacted under this scenario. Our exposure to
fossil fuel extraction continues to remain low at 0.1% of TCE.
A decline in revenue from these sectors under this future is
not expected to result in a material financial impact to the
Bank. However, rapidly introduced and possibly unpredictable
new regulation around carbon and energy in the medium
term, along with shifts in market sentiment, may require the
Bank to adjust its climate strategy, policy settings and/or
goals and targets. This elevates our E&S strategic risk and
legal risk exposure under this scenario.

The pace and possible challenges of regulatory change
applicable to the Bank, our customers and third parties after
2030 elevates the inherent risk of regulatory non-compliance
and legal risks for the Bank in the medium term and long
term under this scenario. The Bank could also engage with
third parties who fail to meet changing standards, potentially
resulting in operational, reputational or litigation issues.
Conduct risk is elevated as the need to rapidly deploy new
sustainable finance products increases the risk of potentially
misleading product design, distribution and disclosures.
Although the viability of some high-emitting businesses is
threatened under this scenario, due to our low exposure to
these sectors, only moderate-rated transition-driven credit
losses are anticipated. This scenario would also increase the
need for greater skills and sophistication in how we identify,
assess and disclose climate-related risk information.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Current policies

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Over the medium and long term, this scenario sees increasing
impacts of climate change, however our macroeconomic
analysis performed this year indicates the long-term
profitability and business model of the Bank continues to be
resilient. The Bank’s Scope 3 emissions goals and targets
for financed and operational emissions, could also be more
difficult to achieve due to the reliance on government policies
to support grid decarbonisation, and a lack of industry
investment in new lower emissions technologies.

The increased frequency and severity of weather events
under this scenario could impact the ability of households to
repay their loans, particularly if availability and affordability of
insurance declines, and asset values are impacted in high-risk
areas. Our acute stress test of a series of cyclones over
South-East QLD indicates that higher severity, but less likely
physical climate events, could produce estimated losses of up
to $1 billion for the Bank in any given year. These losses are
assessed as medium risk to the Bank as they would not result
in a breach of capital or liquidity risk appetites and the Bank
is well provisioned for this degree of loss.

SUSTAINABILITY

The current and short-term effects of climate change
on our risk profile are largely consistent with each
other. Medium-rated strategic risks are driven by
potential increased expectations to provide support
to customers experiencing escalating levels of climate
physical impacts, and decreasing energy affordability
and security. Legal risk remains medium rated due to
potential for misunderstandings of how divergent climate
laws, regulations and contractual obligations apply in the
different locations in which the Bank operates.

CREATING VALUE

changes seen in this scenario.

expectations under this scenario leads to medium levels of E&S
strategic, conduct, regulatory and legal risks. A moderate risk
of credit losses is assessed under this scenario, arising from
industries more exposed to transition risk and the communities
dependent upon those industries. This scenario could rapidly
increase the demand for skills, data and modelling capabilities
required to assess and disclose climate transition risks, and
develop new decarbonisation and climate mitigation-related
products across industries and customer segments.

OVERVIEW

Under this scenario, the Bank’s business model and strategy
is expected to remain resilient.

Under this scenario, transition risks are experienced from the
short term as policy changes are introduced immediately.
This allows the economy to transition away from emissionsintensive industries more gradually over a longer timeframe.
The macroeconomic analysis we performed this year indicates
long-term profitability would not be materially impacted
and the Bank’s business model and strategy is expected to
remain resilient. There are no high-rated risks for the Bank
under this scenario. Heightened regulator and stakeholder

158

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Current year impact on our financial statements

Anticipated impacts on our financial statements

The Bank is well placed to identify and pursue available opportunities arising from Australia’s transition to net zero by 2050.
For example, the development of sustainable finance products, tools and services to support customers to transition and
adapt to the impacts of climate change.

For further details on our credit risk, see Note 9.2 on page 311 in the Financial report.

In May 2024, we launched the climate stress index (CSI)
tool for our bankers, developed in collaboration with
CSIRO. The CSI tool forecasts climate stress through to
2030 by postcode, based on key agri-climatic productivity
variables across grain, livestock and dairy sectors. It
provides a composite score for each postcode-commodity
combination indicating whether climate stress is expected
to decrease, remain stable, or increase moderately or at
a high rate. The tool enhances our understanding of the
degree, nature and location of chronic climate risks to our
lending securities. Currently, 19% of the Bank’s relevant
agricultural exposures are secured by properties located
in postcodes with forecasted increasing rates of climate

stress. The tool enables our bankers to have conversations
with customers on their long-term climate adaptation plans
relevant for credit risk assessment. CSI insights are also
used for portfolio monitoring in livestock, grain and dairy
commodities, helping us track exposure trends and inform
strategic planning. Insights from the tool can be shared with
customers through a Climate Insight Report, tailored to their
region and commodities, to help them consider the data and
plan for future climate-related resilience. We will retire our
productivity mapping tool given the launch of the CSI which
provides a forward view of climate risk. We will continue to
assess tools and capabilities for our agriculture customers’
climate risk assessments.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Based on the 71 suppliers assessed in 2025, we have been able to gain the following insights:

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

In June 2023 we introduced the environmental risk assessment in the supplier risk governance (SRG) tool. As part of CBA’s
annual review, industry environmental risk ratings are updated to support the inherent environmental risk assessment of our
suppliers. For suppliers in industries with high risk ratings, assessments are undertaken when onboarding for new services,
or variation or renewal of an existing service, and form part of supplier risk screening before contracts are executed. In 2024,
in addition to the assessments that were completed when onboarding suppliers in high-risk industries, we triggered the
environmental risk assessment for our leasing agents and landlords, and our top 100 suppliers by spend. As a result, the
number of suppliers assessed decreased from last year and now totals 71.

SUSTAINABILITY

While climate change presents challenges to the Australian economy and our communities, our core business remains
resilient. A relatively small proportion of the Group’s TCE is currently exposed to high or very high physical or transition risk.
Our customers have historically demonstrated strong repayment performance and we have established support mechanisms
to assist them in times of financial stress, including those potentially arising from climate-related events. Although severe
climate-related events could result in an increase in single year credit losses, these losses are assessed as a medium risk to the
Bank as they would not result in a breach of our current capital, liquidity or profitability RAS indicators. This reflects our strong
provisioning levels and capital position.

Climate stress index for agriculture

CREATING VALUE

In some years, the Bank may experience increases in credit losses due to extreme weather events, however the scenario
results indicate that financial impacts to the Bank from climate-related impacts are not expected to be material. This reflects
the nature of our operations, the sectors in which we operate, and our existing risk management frameworks and portfolio
diversification, which together provide a degree of resilience against potential climate-related impacts.

Other climate-related risk
assessment tools

OVERVIEW

The Bank monitors its financial performance, financial position and cashflow on an ongoing basis through its governance
structure. Climate-related effects can be widespread and interrelated, and may influence our operations, strategy and financial
outcomes over the short, medium and long term. As per the result of our CRMA, no climate-related risks or opportunities were
noted this year that have had a material impact on the Bank’s financial performance, financial position or cash flows.

In-scope portfolio excludes exposures in the finance and insurance, and government administration and defence ANZSICs. Portfolios not assessed include
consumer finance (excluding Australian motor vehicle finance) and commercial property outside of Australia and New Zealand. Financed emissions are
calculated using in-scope drawn lending.

Where New Zealand sectors are disclosed separately they include our CBA New Zealand Branch and our subsidiary ASB. ASB is subject to a separate New
Zealand mandatory disclosure regime and expects to publish a standalone climate report later this year. As such equivalent numbers in ASB’s own disclosures
may change between the publication of CBA’s 2025 Annual Report and ASB’s 2025 disclosures.

8

3

For customers captured in sector-level financed emissions goal or target reporting, scope of sector is aligned to sector-level goal or target inclusion criteria.
For power generation, thermal coal mining, upstream oil and gas extraction and heavy industry if a customer is excluded based on the sector inclusion criteria
they are reallocated to the respective parent category. For all other customers, exposures are reported based on ANZSIC classification.

Absolute emissions for thermal coal mining, upstream oil and gas extraction, power generation and Australian motor vehicle finance are CO 2 only (or CO 2 -e
subject to data limitations).

5

In this table we present financed emissions rounded to one decimal place for ease of interpretation. These numbers may not cast due to rounding. In other
parts of this report other numbers are presented that are calculated using financed emissions data. In those instances, our underlying calculations rely on exact
financed emissions data. As such, recalculating those numbers using the financed emissions presented in this table may not lead to the same result.

For more information on our financed emissions methodology and scoping, see pages 392–399.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Our financed emissions estimates are based on our drawn
lending only, in line with the PCAF Standard’s use of
outstanding amounts. Undrawn loan commitments are not
included. As at 30 June 2024, we have estimated financed

Greenhouse gas emissions by geography

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

In-scope drawn lending refers to the on-balance sheet
exposure to the sectors included within the scope of our
financed emissions calculations. Some asset classes and
sectors we lend to are currently excluded due to lack of
available data, methodologies and/or relevance. The inscope TCE lending portfolio excludes exposures to finance
and insurance, government administration and defence
ANZSICs, and non-lending products.

The PwC Assurance Report is provided on pages 430–437.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Changes to our portfolio can include changes in our
drawn lending exposure to new and existing customers,
as well as changes in existing customers’ enterprise value,
which result in changes to the share of these customers’
attributable emissions.

See a more complete set of metrics available for download at commbank.com.au/sustainabilityreporting

SUSTAINABILITY

Portfolio changes have contributed to an increase in
financed emissions. This was largely driven by portfolio
changes in the transport and storage, manufacturing, and
agriculture, forestry and fishing sectors. This was partly
offset by portfolio changes to the mining sector.

For methodology and definitions of metrics in this section, see the Climate disclosures appendix and Glossary on pages 389–429
and 438–451, respectively.

CREATING VALUE

Portfolio changes

We report our operational greenhouse gas emissions at the group level, as well as
breakdowns for key geographies. From 2024 we have reported India separately
from other overseas. We include both market-based and location-based reporting,
covering Scope 1, Scope 2 and selected Scope 3 emissions in metric tonnes of
CO2 equivalent (tCO2-e).

OVERVIEW

Our calculations cover 96% of our in-scope drawn lending exposure. Movements in the overall absolute financed emissions
and emissions intensity of our in-scope drawn lending portfolio are generally dependent on changes in our portfolio mix over time
and differences in emissions profiles at the sector or customer level. We estimate our 2024 absolute financed emissions for our
in-scope lending portfolio at 22.6 MtCO2-e, an increase of 9% (+1.8 MtCO2-e), compared to the restated 2023 absolute financed
emissions estimate (20.8 MtCO2-e). Most notable increases were observed in the transport and storage, and manufacturing
sectors. These movements were partly offset by reductions in estimated absolute emissions in the mining sector. Decarbonisation
of the grid, reflected in changes in grid intensity factors in our estimates, continued to contribute to emissions decreases in
Australian housing and Australian commercial property. 1

MtCO2-e

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

In line with the PCAF Standard, when we refer to our financed emissions this is absolute gross emissions where data allows.
We do not currently have plans to purchase carbon credits to achieve our sector-level financed emissions goals and targets.
However, we acknowledge that some of our customers may be subject to regulatory regimes that require them to use carbon
credits, while others may be voluntarily using credits to offset their emissions as part of their decarbonisation strategies.

Renewable electricity procurement 1

CREATING VALUE

– Selected Scope 3 emissions

2

We are committed to purchasing the equivalent of 100% renewable electricity for our operations by 2030. In 2024, we
achieved RE100 certification at 99.6% for the Group, with ASB metering limitations accounting for the remaining 0.4%.
Our Australian operations have sourced 100% renewable electricity since January 2020, extending to other overseas
operations in 2021 and ASB in New Zealand in 2022. We have also achieved our target of 2 MW of on-site solar capacity
across our Australian properties by 2025.

OVERVIEW

We set our organisational boundary and measure our operational emissions with reference to the GHG Protocol Corporate
Standard, accounting for the operations, facilities and subsidiaries owned or controlled by the Group and take an operational
control approach to establish our reporting boundary. We have extended this boundary to include selected Scope 3 emission
sources (Category 1-14) beyond our operational control that occur as part of the Group’s value chain activities and where
reliable data is available. We continue to focus on more accurately quantifying our Scope 3 emissions in line with new
mandatory climate reporting requirements that come into effect for the Bank in FY26.

172

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

173

Tracking towards our Sustainability Funding Target

Capital deployment

As at 30 June 2025, we have provided $64.4 billion in cumulative funding towards our Sustainability Funding Target (SFT)
of $70 billion by 2030. Given the recent release of the Australian Sustainable Finance Taxonomy and in light of reaching more
than 92% of our 2030 target this year, we will review our target and eligibility criteria next year. ASB separately tracks and
measures the funding they provide towards their SFT. Since 2022 ASB has provided a cumulative NZ $2.1 billion in funding
towards their target of NZ $6.5 billion by 2030.

Capital deployment relating to climate-related risks and opportunities may be categorised into the following:

$70bn

Expenditure: Climate-related expenditure refers to capital or operational spending incurred in managing climate-related
risks or pursuing climate-related opportunities. This includes direct costs related to internal systems, infrastructure, tools or
processes that are focused on climate risk mitigation, adaptation or resilience. No material direct climate-related expenditure
was noted this year. Indirect costs include ongoing expenditure which would be incurred ordinarily, making it impractical to
allocate costs as specifically climate-related. These indirect costs include activities such as branch and office refurbishments
where we are actively incorporating sustainable practices through branch design and energy efficient retrofits; the ATM
refresh project which incorporates lower carbon, natural and renewable materials; and the resourcing costs associated with
ESG teams who work to deliver our climate strategy.

Investment: Climate-related investment refers to capital deployed through financial instruments or equity stakes in public
or private markets that relate to the Bank’s climate-related risks or opportunities and where climate considerations form
a material part of the investment rationale or potential outcomes. This year CBA invested $46 million in climate-related
investments.

that support customers in addressing their climate-related risks or pursuing their climate-related opportunities. Our SFT
monitors the level of cumulative funding within the scope of our SFT eligibility criteria.

The Directors of the Commonwealth Bank of Australia present their report, together
with the Financial report of the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) and of the
Group, being CBA and its controlled entities, for the year ended 30 June 2025.

Assets and liabilities
Home loans increased $43 billion or 6%, reflecting our continued focus on retaining existing customers in a highly competitive
market as well as ongoing investment in our proprietary channel.
Business and corporate loans increased $22 billion or 8%, driven by growth in business banking reflecting diversified lending
across a number of industries as well as institutional and structured lending portfolios.

Debt issues increased $26 billion or 18% reflecting funding requirements for asset growth.

Principal activities
We serve more than 18 million customers with a focus on providing retail and commercial banking services predominantly
in Australia, and in New Zealand through our subsidiary ASB. Our products and services are provided through our divisions,
Retail Banking Services, Business Banking, Institutional Banking and Markets, and ASB New Zealand.

The Group’s statutory net profit after tax for the financial year ended 30 June 2025 was $10,116 million, an increase of
$722 million or 8% on the prior year. Statutory net profit after tax from continuing operations for the financial year ended
30 June 2025 was $10,133 million, an increase of $652 million or 7% on the prior year. The increase was driven by a 5% growth
in total operating income and a 9% reduction in loan impairment expense, partly offset by a 5% increase in operating expenses.

The Group’s cash net profit after tax including discontinued operations for the year ended 30 June 2025 was $10,253 million,
an increase of $406 million or 4% on the prior year. Excluding discontinued operations, cash net profit after tax for the year
ended 30 June 2025 was $10,252 million, an increase of $416 million or 4% on the prior year. For further detail on the drivers
of cash net profit after tax, refer to the Financial performance section on pages 26–33.

Non-cash items include:
• Gain/(loss) on acquisition, disposal, closure and demerger of businesses and associates (classified as discontinued
operations): Gains and losses on these transactions are inclusive of foreign exchange impacts, impairments, restructuring,
separation and transaction costs and cover both controlled businesses and associates classified as discontinued operations.

Litigation and regulatory matters
A number of litigation proceedings against the Group are continuing. The proceedings include the defence of seven class
actions. These include two separate shareholder class action proceedings (the appeal judgment was delivered in May 2025),
two class actions in relation to superannuation products, two class actions related to financial advice (one of which was
dismissed in May 2025, but has been appealed), as well as a class action commenced in New Zealand against ASB Bank
regarding disclosure of loan variations.
There are a range of ongoing matters where domestic or foreign regulators or other bodies are investigating whether CBA,
ASB Bank or another Group entity has breached laws or regulatory or other obligations. Where a breach has occurred, or
obligations have not been met, regulators or other bodies may impose, or apply to a Court for, fines and/or other sanctions, or
may require remediation. The subject matter of these investigations is wide-ranging, and include a number of historical issues,
which were notified to, or identified by, regulators or other bodies. The Group is also party to one enforceable undertaking
and one compliance program with a regulator and continues to receive various notices and requests for information from
its regulators, particularly in Australia and New Zealand, as part of both industry-wide and Group-specific reviews.
In addition to regulatory investigations, enforcement actions, fines and other financial penalties, there may also be financial
exposure to claims by customers, third parties and shareholders and this could include further class actions, customer
remediation or claims for compensation or other remedies. The outcomes and total costs associated with such matters,
and possible claims, individually or collectively, remain uncertain.
The Board continues to monitor significant proceedings and investigations. The Group also continues to engage with
its regulators and other bodies in relation to the matters under investigation and review.
For further information about some of the more significant litigation and regulatory matters referred to above, refer to Note 7.1 in the Financial
report on pages 289–292.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

• Hedging and IFRS volatility: Hedging and IFRS volatility represents timing differences between fair value movements
on qualifying economic hedges and the underlying exposure. To qualify as an economic hedge, the terms and/or risk profile
must match or be substantially the same as the underlying exposure.

Details of dividends paid and dividends determined are outlined in Note 8.4 in the Financial report on pages 301–302.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Statutory net profit after tax complies with the requirements of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth), Australian Accounting
Standards and International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). Cash net profit after tax is management’s preferred
measure of the Group’s financial performance. It excludes items that are non-recurring in nature and are not considered
representative of the Group’s ongoing financial performance (non-cash items). We use the cash net profit after tax to
present a clear and consistent view of our financial performance from period to period.

Options and share rights outstanding

Company secretaries

There are no options over CBA shares on issue as at the date of this report. As at the date of this report, there are

Karen O’Flynn was appointed Company Secretary of CBA on 1 September 2023. Karen is also Group General Counsel
and Group Executive, Legal & Group Secretariat and her experience is set out on page 56.
Qualifications: BA, LLB.

A number of Directors have given written notices stating that they hold office in specified companies and accordingly are
to be regarded as having an interest in any contract or proposed contract that may be made between the Group and any
of those companies.

Constitution
The Constitution of the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (Constitution) requires CBA to indemnify current and former
Directors, Company Secretaries and other executive officers (as defined in the Constitution) of CBA.

CBA must indemnify each Officer on a full indemnity basis and to the full extent permitted by law against all losses, liabilities,
costs, charges and expenses incurred by the Officer as an officer of CBA or of a related body corporate.

Deeds of indemnity

For eligible personnel that do not have an individual deed of indemnity, CBA has also executed an indemnity deed poll, under
which it must indemnify current and former directors, officers, company secretaries and certain other employees of the
Group, on substantially the same terms as the indemnity provided in the Constitution and the individual deeds. An individual
is required to provide written confirmation of their acceptance of the terms of the indemnity deed poll prior to being able
to rely on the rights and benefits under the deed poll.

Insurance

Proceedings on behalf of CBA
No application has been made under section 237 of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) in respect of CBA, and there are
no proceedings that a person has brought or intervened in on behalf of CBA under that section.

Commonwealth Bank is an entity to which ASIC Corporations Instrument 2016/191 (Instrument) dated 24 March 2016,
relating to the rounding of amounts in Directors’ reports and Financial reports, applies. Pursuant to this Instrument,
amounts in this Directors’ report and the accompanying financial report have been rounded to the nearest million dollars,
unless indicated otherwise.

CBA has, during the financial year, paid an insurance premium in respect of a directors’ and officers’ liability and company
reimbursement insurance policy (Policy) for the benefit of CBA and persons defined in the Policy who include directors,
company secretaries, officers and certain employees of the Group. The Policy prohibits disclosure of any further details
about its terms, including the nature of the liability indemnified and the amount of the premium.

Financial performance
The Group cash net profit after tax
(NPAT) performance was above target
at $10,253m and our profit after capital
charge (PACC), a risk-adjusted measure,
outcome was above target at $5,840m.
Total shareholder return (TSR) over the
period 1 July 2021 to 30 June 2025
was 110.38%.
The Bank’s capital position remains
strong and well above APRA’s
requirements. Our disciplined,
long-term balance sheet settings
and capital management, have
enabled us to support our customers
and the economy through a range of
macroeconomic conditions.

We recognise the role of non-financial
drivers in longer term value creation
and our executive scorecards consider
performance across customer,
people and leadership and strategy
execution outcomes, which include
climate-related objectives, as relevant.

The Bank has further improved
employee engagement outcomes and
is building skills for the future to benefit
our people and communities. The Bank
has been recognised in the top five
banks globally for AI maturity and
maintained #1 position in Asia-Pacific
in the 2024 Evident AI Index.

Executive Talent
During the 2025 financial year,
there were changes to our Executive
Leadership Team (ELT) with two
retirements, two new executives
joining the Bank from offshore,
and two incumbent executives
appointed to different portfolios.
We were pleased to retain exceptional
executive talent while bringing in
valuable experience from offshore to

One of the Board’s responsibilities
is to establish and assess executive
performance and remuneration targets
and outcomes.

• Vesting of the FY22 LTAR award
with no reduction (50% for the CEO
and 100% for GEs), following Board
review of multi-year leadership
and strategic execution, and risk
outcomes for individual executives.
No material matters were identified
that would warrant reduced vesting
outcomes, other than those risk
outcomes where adjustments were
made to another element of the
remuneration framework.

When determining annual performance
scorecards, the Board considers past
performance, internal forecasts, and
external expectations, to set targets
that are stretching and aligned to
strategic areas of focus.
The Board Committees meet
concurrently to review executive
performance against shareholder,
customer, people and leadership, and
strategic outcomes. These meetings
include consideration of audit outcomes
and risk assessments to embed a strong
risk culture, and in June also consider
remuneration outcomes so they are
balanced and reflective of overall
performance, with opportunity to adjust
remuneration for material risk and
reputational matters, as appropriate.
The Board considered a number
of matters in the 2025 financial
year which for the ELT resulted
in two downward and one
upward adjustments for risk and
reputation matters.

Remuneration decisions
In determining executive remuneration,
the Board considered financial and
non-financial performance outcomes
in the context of the 2025 financial
year and over a multi-year period.

• FY25 LTAR awarded with no reduction
following the pre-grant assessment.

• The FY22 long-term variable
remuneration (LTVR) performance
outcome of 100%, which reflects
long-term (4-year) TSR performance
at the 90th percentile relative to the
20 largest ASX companies by market
capitalisation and 87.5th percentile
relative to financial sector peers.
Performance rights vest for the
CEO and GEs (including CEO ASB)
following the end of the relevant
holding period.

Looking forward
During the year, the Board
undertook a review of the LTVR
TSR peer groups. As a result,
Suncorp has been removed from
the peer group for the 2026
financial year. This ensures our
TSR peer groups are appropriate
to reflect outperformance against
our banking peers for capital,
customers and talent.
The Board will continue to monitor
the Bank’s remuneration framework
enabling us to attract and retain
the best talent, meet regulatory
requirements and to create
shareholder value. As always,
we welcome your feedback.

considered by the Board

meeting the requirements of CPS

effectiveness review of the
remuneration framework was

risk. This focus on greater transparency
has resulted in a new section on key
remuneration matters to supplement
this letter.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

I am pleased to present the 2025
remuneration report. As a Board we
oversee the Bank’s remuneration
framework, regularly reviewing its
policies and practices for effectiveness
and alignment with our principles.
It is pleasing that during the year, an
independent review was completed to
fulfil the requirements of the Australian
Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA)
Prudential Standard CPS 511 (CPS
511). The review found our framework
is effective, supports the Bank’s culture
and strategy and is aligned to positive
risk outcomes, long-term soundness,
and sustainable performance.

Details on comparative benchmarking can
be found on pages 188–189.

The Board continues to monitor
year-on-year progress to address the
gender pay gap. We are committed
to reducing our gender pay gap
through recruitment, development,
retention, greater access to flexible
work arrangements, challenging gender
role stereotypes, and encouraging and
supporting more women to pursue
careers in technology and other
specialist roles.

SUSTAINABILITY

Our 2025 financial year remuneration
outcomes reflect consistent, disciplined
execution of our strategic priorities,
strong progress on initiatives to safeguard
our customers and community, and
sustainable returns for our shareholders.

Given our scale and the need to attract
and retain exceptional executive talent,
the Bank’s remuneration offering for
executives needs to compete not only
against Australian peer banks and the
ASX20, but also with offshore markets.

CREATING VALUE

Dear Shareholder

The FY25 realised remuneration for the
CEO was $7.02m, a decrease of 22%
from FY24. FY25 realised remuneration
for the CEO and Group Executives (GE)
reflects the longer deferral periods of
the executive remuneration framework
implemented in the 2021 financial year
and includes 2022 and 2023 deferred
short-term variable remuneration
(STVR), and FY21 long-term alignment
remuneration (LTAR).

assessed as overall effective
with three areas identified
for continuous improvement,
relating to ongoing simplification
of remuneration processes and

framework to provide higher
