Financed emissions

This methodology is used for the financed emissions table on pages 164–165 of the Metrics and targets section.

The table below summarises the key methodological decisions that underpin our financed emissions methodology.

Scope – financial products
and services

The in-scope portfolio excludes finance and insurance, and government administration and
defence ANZSICs. Portfolios not assessed include consumer finance (excluding Australian
motor vehicle finance) and commercial property outside of Australia and New Zealand.

Scope – PCAF asset classes

Business loans and unlisted equity, residential mortgages, commercial real estate, project
finance and motor vehicle loans are in scope.

Scope – customer emissions

Customer Scope 1 and 2 emissions for all sectors in scope except for Australian agriculture
and power generation, which include Scope 1 emissions only. Customer Scope 3 emissions
for upstream oil and gas extraction, thermal coal mining or heavy industry. In power
generation, upstream oil and gas extraction, thermal coal mining and heavy industry, where
we have identified that a customer owns but does not operate certain assets, we include a
proportion of the emissions related to the operation of those assets within these customers’
Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions, following the equity share approach. In aviation and shipping,
where we have identified that a customer leases aircraft or vessels, we include the emissions
associated with the aircraft or vessel in the customer’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions.

Measurement – attribution

Financed emissions lag behind our Annual Report by one year given customer reporting
cadences. Our 2025 climate disclosures presents financed emissions as at 30 June 2024
and 30 June 2023 (our prior comparative period). When collecting customer emissions,
financial or activity data, we use the reported information closest to but prior to the
reporting period date.

In aviation and shipping, it is common for customers to lease aircraft and vessels. The emissions associated with these aircraft
and vessels would represent Scope 3 emissions for the customer. Where we identify such leases, for clarity of presentation
and consistency with our sector-level financed emissions goal and target, we include the emissions associated with the aircraft
or vessel in the customer’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions. For more information on Scope 3 measurement, see pages 402–403.
In line with the PCAF Standard, when we refer to our financed emissions this is absolute gross emissions. Where data allows,
we aim not to reflect our customers’ use of carbon credits, or customers’ avoided emissions or emissions removal. Where
we use customer-reported emissions in our estimates, our efforts are largely dependent on the availability of gross reported
emissions by third-party data providers, such as NGERS, Bloomberg and US EIA. Where we rely on estimation using emissions
factors, we prioritise the use of gross emissions where available, such as Exiobase (extracted from the PCAF database)
and Australian National Greenhouse Accounts. Due to the scale of our portfolio, we may not always be able to confirm that
emissions data is presented on a gross basis, this may improve over time with the adoption of mandatory reporting standards.

Limitations with diversified companies and data availability
To estimate our financed emissions, we generally allocate each customer to a specific sector, using ANZSIC codes.
Where emissions intensity multipliers are used, we calculate the emissions for that customer based on their allocated sector.
Accordingly, if a customer is diversified across business activities, the estimate of their emissions may lead to an under- or
overstatement of financed emissions at the sector level.
Financed emissions calculations use point-in-time financial data and also use emissions data reflecting a 12-month period.
There can be lags in customer emissions data which impacts alignment of timing with financial data.

Financed emissions restatement policy
Financed emissions methodologies and data continue to evolve. We may consider changing our baseline or restating prior
periods in circumstances including, but not limited to, changes in calculation methodologies or scope of goals and targets,
new information, changes in data availability and assurance expectations, identification of material discrepancies, or significant
changes in our or our customers’ business model.

1 Absolute emissions for Australian agriculture and power generation includes Scope 1 only.
2 The PCAF Standard provides a set of NACE codes for identification of these sectors. NACE is the abbreviation for the Statistical Classification of Economic
Activities in the European Community.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Where we identify such a circumstance, through the preparation of our disclosures, we consider materiality in determining
whether or not to change our baseline or restate prior periods. Factors that are considered include materiality in relation to
the Bank’s total reported financed emissions for the relevant period, and in relation to reported progress towards a relevant
sector-level goal or target. Our assessment of materiality is guided by consideration of the Corporate GHG Protocol, which

FINANCIAL REPORT

Reporting period

Following the PCAF data hierarchy, we prioritise the use of customer-level emissions
data, followed by activity and economic estimation. For our business lending portfolio, we
use reputable third-party data providers to increase our coverage of customer emissions
data. This includes Bloomberg, Australian Government Clean Energy Regulator National
Greenhouse and Energy Reporting Scheme (NGERS) and United States Energy Information
Administration (US EIA). For customers in-scope of our sector-level financed emissions
goals and targets, we also conduct primary research for customer emissions data.

In general, we calculate our financed emissions using estimates of customers’ Scope 1 and 2 emissions. 1 We consider the
availability of Scope 3 data and methodologies on a sector-by-sector basis. 2 This year we have continued to include Scope 3
emissions for our thermal coal mining, upstream oil and gas extraction, and heavy industry portfolios, reflecting a relatively
high coverage of customer-reported activity data or Scope 3 emissions across these sectors. Due to limited availability of
customer-reported emissions we do not include Scope 3 emissions for some business lending sectors prescribed by PCAF
for inclusion from 2023 onwards, such as other mining, other manufacturing and transport. We do not estimate Scope
3 emissions for commercial property and Australian housing due to limited available embodied carbon emissions data
and methodologies.

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Measurement – customer
emissions

For business loans and project finance, the attribution factor numerator is in-scope
drawn lending and the attribution factor denominator is total company equity plus debt or
enterprise value including cash (EVIC). For housing, the attribution factor is loan-to-value
ratio (LVR), which is the outstanding loan amount divided by the value at origination. For
Australian commercial property, the attribution factor is outstanding loan amount divided by
property value. For road transport, the attribution factor is outstanding loan amount divided
by the vehicle value at origination.

Scope – customer emissions

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Methodology choice

At this stage, we do not estimate emissions for the sovereign debt asset class due to the concentration of our exposure in
Australia and the lack of an established methodology for sub-sovereign and municipal counterparties. We do not calculate
insurance-associated emissions, as they are not relevant to our current business operations.

SUSTAINABILITY

Methodology decision

We have observed recent developments in financed emissions frameworks including the publication of ‘Part B’ of the PCAF
Standard, which includes a new methodology for facilitated emissions. In 2025 we explored methods to estimate facilitated
emissions, however we do not currently report facilitated emissions or include these in our goals or targets due to relatively low
materiality and methodology limitations.

CREATING VALUE

The scope of our financed emissions calculations

We continue to calculate the financed emissions of our business lending portfolio (including project finance), Australian
commercial property, Australian and New Zealand housing, New Zealand commercial property and Australian motor
vehicle finance. The in-scope portfolio excludes finance and insurance, and government administration and defence ANZSICs.
Portfolios not assessed include consumer finance (excluding Australian motor vehicle finance) and commercial property
outside of Australia and New Zealand.

OVERVIEW

This year we continued to evolve our internal capabilities to calculate our financed emissions to support our ability to report on
and deliver against our sector-level financed emissions goals and targets. This included simplifying the data we use to estimate
financed emissions for the Australian agriculture sector, updating the calculations for the Australian commercial property sector
to incorporate updates to Federal Government datasets and other changes to more closely align with the PCAF Standard.

Scope – financial products and services

Emissions measurement approach

Estimating emissions for the housing sector

We measure the emissions of our loans associated with CBA, Bankwest, ASB, Unloan and Residential Mortgage Group (RMG).

For the business lending portfolio (including project finance), we follow the PCAF business loans data hierarchy. This approach
enables the assignment of a data quality score from one to five (highest to lowest) which accounts for the varying levels of
estimations and uncertainty in a customer’s emissions. The table below outlines the options and formulas applied for any
sectors where the measurement framework applied is PCAF business loans. All sectors in the business lending asset class
follow this methodology. Where we have refined the approach for a sector, such as for Australian agriculture, more information
is provided below. The business lending asset class does not include commercial property, housing and motor vehicle finance.

New Zealand housing
We include material greenhouse gases related to New Zealand housing, reported in CO2-e, including: Scope 1 emissions
relating to the burning of natural gas, LPG, wood, and coal for cooking and heating; and Scope 2 emissions from the use of
grid electricity in the household.
To calculate New Zealand housing emissions, the following steps are used:
1. Collect New Zealand’s total residential energy consumption from the 2023 Ministry of Business, Innovation & Employment
Energy Balance Report.
2. Calculate energy consumption per dwelling by dividing the total energy consumption from step 1 by the number of
dwelling units in New Zealand, sourced from Stats NZ.

x Sector revenue emissions factor 3
5

3. Calculate the estimated energy consumption for each household by assigning the relevant benchmarks.

Emissions are estimated based on sector-level economic emissions
intensity factors (tCO2-e/$lent). This method is applied to all sectors
where customer emissions, production or financial data is not available.

3. Calculate energy consumption per square metre by dividing the energy consumption per dwelling from step 2 by the
average floor area. The average floor area is calculated using building consent data from Stats NZ.
4. Calculate an emissions factor per square metre by multiplying the energy consumption per square metre calculated in step
3 by the relevant emissions factor from the latest Ministry for the Environment Measuring Emissions Guidance (2025).

6. Attribute our share of the household emissions by multiplying the household emissions by the LVR, or the outstanding loan
value divided by the value of the security at origination.
7. Where floor area is known we assign a PCAF score of 4, where unknown we assign a PCAF score of 5.

emissions sourced from databases such as NGERS and Bloomberg.

agriculture and sectors for which we have set financed emissions goals or targets. Refer to the PCAF Database Terms of Use for more information at:
https://db.carbonaccountingfinancials.com/docs/PCAF%20Database_Terms%20of%20Use_042023.pdf. For information on emissions factors for
Australian agriculture, refer to page 397. For information on emissions factors for sectors for which we have set financed emissions goals or targets, refer to
pages 400 – 405. For our New Zealand portfolio, sector revenue and sector asset emissions factors are derived using data from Statistics New Zealand, Ministry
for Environment, and Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority.

1 HELs and HELOCs include Viridian Line of Credits (VLOCs), ASB Orbit and ASB HomePlus products.
2 The inclusion of guarantor properties in the Australian housing portfolio has been assessed as immaterial.

they have similar climate zones. For NT and WA regions where the climate zone was 1, 2 or 3, QLD data was used. For WA with climate zone 4 or greater, NSW

Estimating emissions for business lending

OVERVIEW

For the Australian and New Zealand housing portfolios, we measure the emissions for on-balance sheet loans for the purchase
and refinance of residential property. Home Equity Loans (HELs) and Home Equity Lines of Credit (HELOCs) 1 are not included
in financed emissions for this sector given these products are considered consumer loans for general purposes, with unknown
use of proceeds. Australian home loans used to construct or renovate a house are also included once construction is complete,
and the customer has received their final draw down. New Zealand housing includes home loans used to construct or renovate
a house at the date of the first draw down. Due to data limitations we currently include guarantor properties in our Australian
housing portfolio. 2

points to a 5% of total inventory threshold as a ‘rule of thumb’ for determining materiality, but also notes that the full context
should be considered. As such, where a recalculation falls beneath that 5% threshold, we may also consider the circumstances
of the recalculation to guide our assessment. In circumstances where a restatement impacts a sector-level goal or target, or
our progress towards a sector-level goal or target, we then provide an explanation and drivers of this change. Restatements
are brought to the attention of the relevant management-level governance committee prior to inclusion in external reporting.

Estimating emissions for the Australian motor vehicle finance sector

Australian commercial property consists of secured and unsecured loans that are classified as being for the purchase and
refinance of commercial property based on the customer’s ANZSIC classification. 1 Due to data availability this includes
general corporate finance to customers whose primary business activity is commercial real estate.

Australian motor vehicle finance consists of financing for the specific purpose of purchasing motor vehicles, including
passenger vehicles, light commercial vehicles, motorcycles, buses and heavy vehicles. 1 Emissions from these vehicles are CO2
only, or CO2-e subject to data limitations. This is comprised of Scope 1 tailpipe emissions relating to fuel consumption; and
Scope 2 emissions from the use of grid electricity to power EVs.

1. Where National Australian Built Environment Rating System (NABERS) whole building data exists for a property, collect
electricity and gas consumption values from the NABERS ratings register.
2. Where NABERS whole building data does not exist for a property, but where building type and location are known:

b. Calculate the estimated energy consumption for each property by assigning the relevant benchmarks.

1. Source vehicle type 2, make and model from internal systems.
2. Collect average emissions intensity (gCO2 /vehicle km) for each vehicle make and model from reputable external sources
including Glass’s and the Green Vehicle Guide. 3 Where data is not available, emissions factors for each vehicle type derived
from Bureau of Infrastructure and Transport Research Economics (BITRE) are used. 4 Where vehicle type, make and model
are unknown but the vehicle has been verified as an EV or hybrid, then the emissions intensity is conservatively assumed
to be 120 gCO2 /vehicle km. 5
3. Collect average energy consumption (Wh/vehicle km) for each power EV make and model, from the Green Vehicle Guide.
Calculate the average Scope 2 emissions intensity (gCO2-e/vehicle km) by multiplying this by a relevant emissions factor. 6

3. Calculate an estimate of absolute emissions for each property by multiplying the energy consumption by a relevant
emissions factor. 3

4. Collect average annual distance travelled for each vehicle type from BITRE. 7

4. Attribute our share of the property emissions by multiplying the property emissions by the attribution factor which is the
outstanding loan value 4 divided by the value of the property. 5

6. Attribute our share of absolute emissions by multiplying the absolute emissions by the attribution factor (outstanding loan
value divided by the value of the asset at origination). Where value at origination is not known, for example, unsecured
loans, an attribution factor of 100% is assumed.

We assign a PCAF data quality score of 2 where NABERS whole building data exists, and a score of 5 in all other cases.
Our estimation of emissions for commercial buildings is dependent on the coverage and accuracy of available property-level
information. We expect to refine our approach over time, including incorporating new sources of property-level data as these
become available.

New Zealand commercial property consists of lending for specific corporate purposes, where the property is used for
commercial purposes such as retail, hotels, office space, industrial or large multi-family rentals.
We include material greenhouse gases related to New Zealand commercial property operations, reported in CO2-e, including:
Scope 1 emissions relating to the burning of gas; and Scope 2 emissions from the use of electricity in the building.
To calculate New Zealand commercial property emissions, the following steps are used:
1. Electricity and gas consumption values are provided on a per metre squared and per premise category from the Building
Research Association of New Zealand (BRANZ) Building Energy End-Use Study report.

Estimating emissions for the Australian agriculture sector
Australian agriculture consists of loans for the primary purpose of agriculture primary production. 8
Our financed emissions estimation for Australian agriculture includes Scope 1 emissions only. For livestock, this includes the
most material emissions sources of enteric fermentation, manure management and agricultural soils. For grains and cropping,
this includes agriculture soils, field burning of agriculture residues, urea application, liming and rice cultivation. Scope 2
emissions, land-use change and removals emissions are not included due to data and methodology availability. We continue to
explore introducing estimates of our land-use change and removals emissions.
For a small portion of our Australian agriculture customers, we are able to use customer reported emissions. In all other
cases we estimate financed emissions using economic emissions factors. From this year, we no longer apply the customer
activity-based emissions estimation method for this sector due to data limitations. We have restated the prior comparative
period to conform with this methodology change.
The following steps are used to calculate Australian agriculture emissions using economic emissions factors:

3. An emissions intensity per square metre for each premise type is calculated by multiplying the energy consumption
per square metre (from step 1) by a relevant emissions factor from the latest Ministry for the Environment Measuring
Emissions Guidance (2025).

2. Convert these revenue emissions factors to commodity specific asset emissions factors (tCO2e/$) by multiplying the
revenue emissions factors by a commodity asset turnover ratio. 10

4. Calculate an estimate of absolute emissions for each building by multiplying the emissions intensity by the floor area
of each building.

6. We assign a PCAF score of 4 where property data is available, and a score of 5 in all other cases.

4. We assign a PCAF data quality score of 1 or 2 where customer reported emissions data exists, and a score of 5 in all other cases.
Agriculture data is complex, our estimation for Australian agriculture is dependent on publicly available data for estimation and
coverage and accuracy of available customer information. We may refine our approach over time, including incorporating new
sources of customer emissions as these become available.

Estimating emissions for the New Zealand commercial property sector

Where the vehicle make and model is known we assign a PCAF score of 3. Where the vehicle make and model is unknown but
the specific vehicle type is known (for instance, buses or verified passenger cars) then we assign a PCAF score of 4. In all other
cases we assign a PCAF score of 5.

applying the average Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions per customer where building data is available.

5. Calculate an estimate of absolute emissions for each vehicle by multiplying the distance travelled by the emissions intensity.

CREATING VALUE

and Statistical Area Level 4. 2

2025 ANNUAL REPORT

Financed emissions sector methodology

400

401

Sector-level financed emissions goal and target methodology
This methodology is used for the sector-level goal and target graphs on pages 116–129 of the Strategy section.

Where customers have diversified business activities across multiple sector-level goals and/or targets, we seek to isolate the
relevant sectoral component of their emissions. Subject to data availability, the relevant sectoral component of their emissions
is calculated using customer-reported emissions or with reference to their production in that sector-level goal or target sector.

For each sector, this involves setting boundaries to define the emissions a customer is responsible for and collecting the required
data to measure the emissions. We use ANZSIC codes as well as business knowledge to assign customers to the most appropriate
part of the value chain. The table on page 405 defines the sector scope for each of our sector-level goals and targets.

Approach by sector

Selecting goal and target metrics

The goal metric is an intensity metric (kgCO2-e/m2) calculated by dividing attributed Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions
(kgCO2-e) from Australian housing (as described on page 395) by attributed living area (m2). Attributed living area is calculated
as household floor space (as provided by an external data provider) multiplied by the outstanding loan value divided by the
value of the security at origination. Where living area data is not available we apply a portfolio average based on property
specific attributes such as state, building type and number of bedrooms.

We have found:

• Emissions intensity goals or targets are appropriate when the output in question may in fact grow, but at a decreasing
emissions intensity. These intensity goals or targets should be based on physical metrics, for example kWh for power
generation or m2 for housing.
For emissions intensity goals or targets, an aggregation method is required to produce a portfolio metric. For power
generation, heavy industry, aviation and road transport we use the attributed generation or production method. This approach
divides the total attributed emissions by the total attributed activity. For Australian housing and Australian commercial
property, we calculate a portfolio emissions intensity by dividing the total attributed emissions by the total attributed living
area or floor area, respectively.

When selecting reference scenarios, three criteria are important to us:
1. Consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C.
2. From credible, independent and reputable sources and are informed by science, with limited or no overshoot 1 and
a conservative reliance on negative emissions technologies.
3. Providing geographical and sectoral relevance.

The goal metric is an intensity metric (kgCO2-e/m2). Where building data is available, this is calculated by dividing attributed
Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions (kgCO2-e) from Australian commercial property (as described on page 396) by attributed floor
area (m2). Attributed floor area is calculated as floor space (where available from NABERS) multiplied by the attribution factor,
or outstanding loan value divided by the value of the security.2 Where floor area data is not available, we apply an average floor
area, sourced from the 2024 CBBS, based on property specific attributes such as location and building type. Where building
data is not available, emissions intensity is estimated using the sub-sectoral average.3
The selected reference scenario is from the SBTi Buildings target-setting tool (Version 1.0), using the SBTi tool’s Australian
pathways for ‘office’, ‘retail shopping mall’, and ‘other’ for office, retail and industrial buildings, respectively. We have changed
our base year from 2022 to 2024 as the 2024 CBBS does not provide the information necessary to update 2022 figures
or use 2023 figures. We use national average emissions intensity for each building type (office: 62.7 kgCO2-e/m2, retail:
60.2 kgCO2-e/m2, industrial: 37.2 kgCO2-e/m2) for the SBTi tool base year of 2024, derived from the 2024 CBBS and
National Greenhouse Accounts emissions factors published in 2024.

1 Office: CBBS ‘offices’, Retail: CBBS ‘retail and wholesale trade buildings’, Industrial: CBBS ‘warehouses’, ‘factories & other secondary production buildings’,
‘agricultural and aquacultural buildings’ and ‘other industrial buildings not elsewhere classified’.
1 The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) defines a ‘No overshoot’ 1.5°C pathway as: those that give at least 50% probability based on current
knowledge of limiting global warning to below 1.5°C. The IPCC defines a ‘Limited overshoot’ 1.5°C pathway as: those that limit warming to below 1.6°C and
return to 1.5°C by 2100.
2 Including exposures to customers that operate in Australia but also have global operations.

2 In line with PCAF, where we are unable to use value at origination due to data availability, the value of the security is fixed at the most recent value available at
the time the financed emissions goal for this sector was set. In addition, we have excluded aged valuations data for certain exposures in order to ensure the
valuation is relevant to our baseline year for the target.
3 Financed emissions for these exposures without building data are estimated in accordance with our financed emissions methodology outlined on
pages 392–399.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

To address this and in the absence of a consistent set of Australia-specific sectoral reference scenarios aligned with the IEA
NZE (2021) – our reference scenario for global portfolios – we have sought alternative reference scenarios for portfolios
with exposures concentrated in Australia. 2 This includes scenarios produced by Science Based Targets Initiative (SBTi) tools,

Australian commercial property consists of secured and unsecured loans that are classified as being for the purchase and
refinance of commercial property based on the customer’s ANZSIC classification. Due to data availability this includes general
corporate finance to customers whose primary business activity is commercial real estate. Separate goals are presented
for three key sub-sectors: office, retail and industrial. This recognises each sub-sector’s individual materiality and differing
energy consumption patterns, and enables decarbonisation strategies that are most relevant to each sub-sector. Where CBA
had less than $5 million of exposures to a customer at the reporting date, the customer was excluded from the scope of the
goal in order to focus data collection efforts on more material exposures. Where building data is available, we assign each
in-scope exposure to a sub-sector based on a mapping between our internal building type classification and the building
type classifications used within the 2024 CBBS. 1 Where building data is not available, we assign the in-scope exposure to a
sub-sector based on the distribution of the portfolio with building data. In our 2024 Climate Report we referred to the 2022
CBBS. This year we have updated our approach to incorporate the 2024 CBBS.

FINANCIAL REPORT

We analyse various scenarios which are widely used by the industry. In May 2021, the International Energy Agency (IEA)
published the Net Zero Emissions by 2050 scenario (NZE 2050) which shows a pathway for the global economy to achieve
net zero emissions 20 years earlier than the previous Sustainable Development Scenario (SDS). We have selected the IEA NZE
(2021), which is consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C, as our reference scenario for thermal coal mining, power
generation, upstream oil and gas extraction, aviation and shipping. The NZBA identifies the IEA NZE (2021) as a scenario
that meets the objectives of the NZBA commitment. A global 1.5°C-aligned reference scenario does not necessarily reflect
Australia’s starting point or emissions trajectory.

Australian commercial property – Office, retail and industrial

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

Selecting reference scenarios

The selected reference scenario is from the SBTi Residential Buildings target-setting tool (consultation draft, Version
1), using our financed emissions intensity (38.8 kgCO2-e/m2) for the base year of 2021. We have used our own portfolio
emissions intensity for the base year as an appropriate Australian average figure was not available at the time the goal was set.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

• Absolute emissions goals or targets are most appropriate in sectors where science indicates the output has to be
reduced substantially and, in some cases, completely by 2050 to limit global warming to 1.5°C. Data limitations may
also support setting an interim goal or target based on absolute emissions, for example in the shipping sector.

Australian housing
SUSTAINABILITY

In line with Net Zero Banking Alliance (NZBA) guidance, we set goals and targets using absolute emissions or physical
intensity. However, we may choose to use other metrics, for example absolute financing, where relevant.

CREATING VALUE

Defining the sector scope

Diversified customers

OVERVIEW

We take a sector-level approach to measure and track the alignment of our financed emissions with pathways to limiting
global warming to 1.5°C. For sectors where we have set sector-level financed emissions goals or targets, we aim to measure
customer emissions across a defined component of the sector scope that captures the most material emissions within the
value chain, and set reduction goals and targets that are consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C, based on science.
In order to achieve this, there are a series of additional methodological considerations for our sector-level financed emissions
goals and targets. The table on page 405 summarises key choices.

In 2024, the IEA published an updated World Energy Outlook which included an updated NZE scenario. We reviewed the
emissions trajectory of that scenario and have chosen to retain the 2021 version as our reference scenario for thermal coal
mining, upstream oil and gas extraction, as well as for our more recent goal and target for the aviation and shipping sectors.
We will continue to monitor the latest climate science to ensure relevance of our selected reference scenarios and we expect to
update them over time. This includes, at minimum, an annual review process to assess updates to relevant reference scenarios
and target setting tools. In accordance with NZBA guidance we expect to perform a review of each target every five years from
the time it was set. Certain circumstances, such as significant updates to the relevant science or government policies, may
also trigger a review.

COMMONWEALTH BANK
2025 ANNUAL REPORT

3.

Mission Possible Partnership (MPP) and United Nations Principles of Responsible Investment-commissioned Inevitable Policy
Response (IPR). These scenarios build on the IEA NZE (2021).

402

403

The power generation sector includes the generation of electricity. These are our customers who generate significant revenue
from electricity generation. This includes customers with more than 5% of revenue from thermal coal electricity generation.
Customers with less than $1 million of exposures are generally excluded. For power generation, the alignment metric is
kgCO2 /MWh, an intensity metric reflects the critical role of power generation in the economy. Where data is not available,
emissions are estimated using the customer’s reported generation and grid specific physical emissions intensity factors from
the relevant regional body, for example National Greenhouse Accounts for Australia or equivalent source outside of Australia.
Zero Scope 1 emissions are assumed for renewable customers unless customer reported emissions are available.

The shipping sector includes customers that operate ocean-going vessels, and ocean-going vessel lessors to such operators.¹
Where CBA had less than $5 million of TCE to a customer at the reporting date, the customer was excluded from the scope of
the target to focus data collection efforts on more material exposures.

Transport – Australian road (passenger and light commercial vehicle finance)
The Australian road (passenger and light commercial vehicle) sector includes lending for the finance of passenger vehicles
and light commercial vehicles. 1 Passenger vehicles include both cars and motorcycles. We have chosen a combined goal,
recognising that many light commercial vehicles are used primarily as passenger vehicles and the decarbonisation profile is
expected to be similar across these vehicle types.

Our sector-level financed emissions goal for road transport is a Scope 1 intensity goal, to align to the scope of the reference
scenario. However, we include Scope 2 absolute emissions for this sector within our financed emissions disclosure.

Transport – Aviation
The aviation sector includes customers that are commercial airline operators that offer scheduled services, and aircraft
lessors to such operators. 3 Where CBA had less than $5 million of TCE to a customer at the reporting date, the customer
was excluded from the scope of the goal in order to focus data collection efforts on more material exposures.

However, we include Scope 2 (Scope 3 ‘indirect electricity’ for lessors) absolute emissions for this sector, which are generally
minimal, within our financed emissions disclosure.

The steel sector includes production of steel with iron and steel mills. For steel, the alignment metric is tCO2-e/tonne steel
production. An intensity metric reflects the role of metals production in a decarbonising world. This goal has been set without
a baseline as there were no customers with drawn lending at the time the goal was set.
Our sector-level financed emissions goal for steel is a Scope 1 and 2 intensity goal, to align to the scope of the reference
scenario. There were no customers with drawn exposure in this sector as at 30 June 2022, 30 June 2023 or 30 June 2024.
If there were, we would include Scope 3 absolute emissions for such customers within the heavy industry sector presented
in our financed emissions disclosure.
The selected reference scenario is from the SBTi Steel target-setting tool (consultation draft, version 2.2), using an emissions
intensity of 1.91 tCO2 /tonne steel for the base year of 2021, sourced from the World Steel Association. 3 The scrap ratio
numbers used in the tool are sourced from the IEA with 31% for the base year and 37% in the goal year (2030). 4

Heavy industry – Alumina and aluminium
The alumina and aluminium sector includes the refining of bauxite to form alumina and the smelting of alumina to produce
aluminium. This excludes secondary production such as aluminium recycling. For both alumina and aluminium, the alignment
metric is tCO2-e/tonne aluminium production. This follows industry convention in expressing alumina production in terms
of aluminium equivalents. We use the industry-accepted factor of 1.9 tonnes alumina production per 1.0 tonne of aluminium
for this conversion. An intensity metric reflects the role of metals production in a decarbonising world.
For Scope 1 and 2, where a company does not report emissions for alumina and aluminium separately, we estimate emissions
to alumina and aluminium by applying a location-specific emissions factor for alumina emissions intensity and aluminium
emissions intensity to available data on the customer’s refining and smelting production activity, respectively.
Our sector-level financed emissions goals for alumina and aluminium are Scope 1 and 2 intensity goals, to align to the
scope of the reference scenario. However, we include Scope 3 absolute emissions for these customers within the heavy
industry sector presented in our financed emissions disclosure. Where a customer does not report Scope 3 emissions these
are estimated by multiplying the customer’s reported Scope 1 and 2 emissions by a Scope 3 emissions factor. The Scope
3 emissions factor represents the ratio of Scope 1 and 2 emissions to Scope 3 emissions for the portfolio of customers
in the alumina and aluminium sector for which we have a complete set of reported Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions.
The selected reference scenarios are the MPP Oceania 1.5°C alumina and aluminium pathways.

We track the portfolio emissions intensity against the IEA NZE (2021) global pathway for aviation. A global reference scenario
is appropriate given the global nature of our portfolio.

2 Note that emissions by vehicle type are reported by BITRE on a CO 2 -e basis. BITRE reported that CO 2 represented over 98% of direct road transport emissions
in 2022. See page 429 for source.
3 The decision to include lessors was made because of the materiality of these customers and reflecting common practice in the sector to lease assets rather than
own directly. We acknowledge the PCAF Standard is not prescriptive on approaches to attribute our lessor customers’ Scope 3 Category 13 (Downstream Leased
Assets) emissions. We believe this is the most appropriate methodology available at this time given the data available and the nature of our portfolio.
4 Subject to data availability, reported emissions may include other activities such as ground operations.

1 The decision to include lessors was made because of the materiality of these customers and reflecting common practice in the sector to lease assets rather
than own directly. We acknowledge the PCAF Standard is not prescriptive on approaches to attribute our lessor customers’ Scope 3 Category 13 (Downstream
Leased Assets) emissions. We believe this is the most appropriate methodology at this time given the data available and the nature of our portfolio.
2 Subject to data availability, reported emissions may include other activities such as land-based operations.
3 World Steel Association, Sustainability Indicators. See page 429 for source.
4 See page 429 for source and license.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

1 Motor vehicle finance includes commercial asset finance and consumer personal loans. Legacy Bankwest personal loans are excluded due to data limitations.

FINANCIAL REPORT

Attribution follows the PCAF business lending methodology. For lessors where the lessee is known, the attribution factor
denominator is the total equity and debt or EVIC of the lessee and the attribution factor numerator is drawn lending exposure
to the lessee. As lending in our aviation portfolio generally relates to specific aircraft within an airline’s fleet, we may seek
to improve the accuracy of our financed emissions estimates for this sector over time by substituting airline level data with
aircraft specific data and adapting our attribution approach accordingly.

Heavy industry – Steel

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

For aviation the goal metric is an intensity metric (gCO2 /revenue passenger km) calculated by dividing attributed
‘tank-to-wake’ emissions 4 (Scope 1 for operators and Scope 33 for lessors), in gCO2, by attributed revenue passenger
kilometres (RPK). This reflects the continued role of aviation in a decarbonising world. We source emissions and RPK data
from airline sustainability reporting for exposures to airlines or lessors where the lessee is known. Where data is not available,
including for lessors where the lessees are unknown, emissions intensity is estimated using the portfolio average. Where CO2
data is unavailable, we use CO2-e.

We track the portfolio emissions against the IEA NZE (2021) global pathway for shipping. A global reference scenario
is appropriate given the global nature of our portfolio.

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

The selected reference scenario is the IPR RPS (2021), which models a 34% reduction in annual emissions per ‘car’ between
2020 and 2030. This emissions intensity reduction is applied to the 2020 Australian national average emissions intensity
(262 gCO2 /vehicle km) for passenger vehicles (motorcycles and cars) and light commercial vehicles which has been calculated
using data published by BITRE. 2

Attribution follows the PCAF business lending methodology. For lessors where the lessee is known, the attribution factor
denominator is the total equity and debt or EVIC of the lessee and the attribution factor numerator is drawn lending exposure
to the lessee.

SUSTAINABILITY

The goal metric is an intensity metric (gCO2 /vehicle km) calculated by dividing the attributed Scope 1 emissions (gCO2) from
motor vehicle finance (as described on page 397) by attributed distance travelled (vehicle km). Distance travelled currently
reflects average distance travelled by vehicle type based on data published by BITRE. Attributed distance travelled reflects
distance travelled multiplied by the attribution factor, where the attribution factor is the outstanding loan amount divided by
the value of the security at origination. Where valuation data is unavailable, 100% attribution is assumed.

Our sector-level financed emissions target for shipping is a Scope 1 (Scope 3 ‘tank-to-wake’ for lessors) target, to align to the
scope of the reference scenario. However, we include Scope 2 (Scope 3 ‘indirect electricity’ for lessors) absolute emissions for
this sector, which are generally minimal, within our financed emissions disclosure.

CREATING VALUE

We track the portfolio emissions intensity against the IEA NZE (2021) however, we calculate the power generation target
against the IEA SDS for OECD nations, based on total electricity and heat sectors CO2 emissions divided by total electricity
generation. Our 2030 target of 105 kgCO2 /MWh is 33% below the equivalent figure in the global IEA NZE (2021) scenario.
We originally tracked towards this value based on the IEA SDS for OECD nations and chose to retain it as our 2030 target
as it represents a lower emissions intensity than the IEA NZE (2021) transition pathway in 2030.

For shipping the target metric is absolute financed emissions, calculated based on attributed ‘tank-to-wake’ emissions 2
(Scope 1 for operators and Scope 3 1 lessors), in MtCO2. Where CO2 data is unavailable, we use CO2-e. We have selected an
absolute emissions metric for this interim target due to data limitations. For exposures to shipping companies, we source
emissions data from customers’ sustainability reporting. For lessors, where the lessee is known we source emissions data
from the lessee’s sustainability reporting. Where data is not available, including for lessees, emissions are estimated using
the average emissions intensity per dollar of drawn lending for customers with available emissions data.

OVERVIEW

Transport – Shipping

Australian residential

HELs and HELOCs

We calculate our upstream oil and gas extraction targets using the percentage decrease of emissions from combustion
activities, oil and natural gas respectively between 2020 and 2030 from the IEA NZE (2021) scenario.

Thermal coal mining
The thermal coal mining sector includes companies that mine thermal coal. We include all thermal coal mining customers with
more than 5% of their revenue coming directly from the sale of thermal coal in our 2030 target. For thermal coal mining the
target metric is absolute emissions. Where customer-level emissions data is not available, the customer’s reported production
data is multiplied by a physical emissions intensity factor 2 to estimate Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions. Scope 3 emissions are
estimated by multiplying production by National Greenhouse Accounts energy content and emissions factors for bituminous
coal. Where a customer reports coal emissions, we assign emissions to thermal coal mining using a ‘pro-rata’ share based
on the customer’s production of thermal coal as a proportion of total coal: coal emissions x (thermal coal production/total
coal production).
We calculate our thermal coal mining target using the percentage decrease of emissions from coal combustion activities
between 2020 and 2030 from the IEA NZE (2021) scenario. Our E&S Framework details our commitments regarding thermal
coal mining financing. We have set a 2030 interim target for thermal coal mining, to reduce our financed emissions in thermal
coal mining by 100% from our 2020 baseline. This represents a faster reduction than the IEA NZE (2021) scenario.

Sector scope

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

Metric

Inclusions criteria

SUSTAINABILITY

Emissions
scope

Sector

Upstream oil and gas extraction
Upstream oil extraction refers to entities whose principal operations include the exploration and development of oil fields for
the purposes of extracting and producing crude oil. This does not include midstream or downstream activities. Upstream
gas extraction refers to entities whose principal operations include exploration, ownership, development and management
of gas fields, that are used for the purpose of natural gas production. For upstream oil and gas extraction the target metric
is absolute emissions. Where a customer reports emissions, we assign emissions to upstream oil and gas extraction using a
‘pro-rata’ share based on the customer’s production of oil and gas and the emissions factor for each activity, respectively,
expressed in ‘million barrels of oil equivalent’. Where customer-level emissions data is not available, the customer’s reported
production data is multiplied by an activity emissions intensity factor 2 to estimate Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions. Scope 3
emissions are estimated by first converting production, in barrels of oil equivalent, to energy content using an energy content
factor from third-party sources such as Energy Institute’s Statistical Review of World Energy and then multiplying the energy
content by emissions factors from the National Greenhouse Accounts.

Sector-level goal and target setting

CREATING VALUE

The selected reference scenario is from the SBTi Cement target-setting tool (Version 2.1.2), using an emissions intensity
of 0.708 tCO2-e Scope 1 emissions/tonnes of cement, for the base year of 2021, sourced from the Department of Climate
Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (DCCEEW). 1 While this baseline and resulting sector-level pathway only accounts
for Scope 1 emissions, we adopt a conservative approach by comparing it against our annual intensity metric which is inclusive
of both Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions.

Sector-level goal and target methodology

OVERVIEW

The cement sector includes manufacturing portland, natural and other hydraulic cement from crushed limestone and clay or
shale. For cement, the alignment metric is tCO2-e/tonne cement production. An intensity metric reflects the role of cement
production in buildings and construction. We define cement as cementitious product, which aligns with the GHG Protocol’s
‘specific CO2 per ton of cementitious product’ definition. This means that our sector-level financed emissions goal for cement
is a Scope 1 and Scope 2 intensity goal. This aligns with the boundary of the reference scenario used for the goal. Emissions
and production associated with imported clinker and clinker purchased from third parties (typically reported as Scope 3)
are not included in the goal. However, we include Scope 3 absolute emissions for cement sector customers within the heavy
industry sector presented in our financed emissions disclosure.

Approach to climate scenario analysis

Scenario modelling approach and key assumptions
Long-term scenarios and CRMA
Modelling
approach

– Agriculture (dairy and livestock)
– Air and road transport
– Heavy industry

Input used in climate scenario analysis

• Regional segments for more granular macroeconomic inputs were selected by considering materiality as

City Statistical Areas (GCCSAs) and Statistical Area Level 4s (SA4s):

Relevance to CBA’s assessment of • The Bank may be exposed to both physical and transition risks through the provision
of deposit and lending services to retail, business and institutional customers.
our resilience to climate-related
changes, developments
• The long-term scenarios are based on the NGFS scenarios which are a set of
or uncertainties
climate-related scenarios designed to help financial institutions and policymakers
assess the risks associated with climate change and the transition to a lower-carbon
economy. They are therefore directly relevant for use in assessing the resilience of
the Bank’s strategy and business model in the short, medium and long term, to the
impacts of a changing climate.

– Sydney
– Rest of New South Wales
– Rest of Victoria
– South-East Queensland (Greater Brisbane, Gold Coast, Sunshine Coast and Toowoomba)

• The acute event scenarios assessed the impact of different acute physical climate
events (cyclone and drought) and a transition event (NSW electricity disruption),
on a diverse range of the Bank’s portfolios (retail, non-retail and agriculture).
Climate-related physical or
transition risks

• The assessment of financial risk for each of the scenarios focused primarily on either
the physical risks or transition risks relevant to that scenario.

• The Delayed transition and Net zero 2050 scenarios are aligned to the Paris
Agreement’s overarching goal to hold the increase in the global average temperature
to well-below 2°C above pre-industrial levels.

Time horizons used

• The long-term scenarios extend out to 2050, with the CRMA assessing risks at the
following time horizons:
– Current term (within the next 12 months).
– Short term (~3 years) to align with the Bank’s strategic planning cycle.
– Medium term (~10 years) to align with the timeframe of when the NGFS physical
impacts begin to diverge for the low versus high warming futures.
– Long term (~30 years) to align with the Bank’s home loan portfolio where home
loans can be contractually agreed to for up to 30 years.
• The acute event scenario time horizons vary by scenario based on the time expected
for the economic- and credit-related impacts to flow through to the Bank.

Scope of
operations

• Macroeconomic inputs associated with each long-term scenario were applied to all retail and non-retail
portfolios of the Bank, including ASB.
• The CRMA assessed the impacts of climate change on our financial, non-financial and strategic risk
types. This included consideration of impacts on our business model, financial and non-financial risk,
strategy, and climate-related risks and opportunities.

Reporting period • The scenario analysis and CRMA were carried out during the last quarter of the 2025 reporting period.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Alignment to the latest
international agreement
on climate change

– Adelaide
• The macroeconomic variables were then input to the Bank’s suite of stress testing models to arrive at
balance sheet and credit loss results extending out to 2050.

FINANCIAL REPORT

• For the strategic and non-financial risk types in the CRMA the scenario factored in both
the transition and physical risks, where relevant or material. For example, under the
physical risk scenario (Current policies) conduct risk may be heightened due to decisions
the Bank may make in relation to pricing and lending practices in high physical risk
zones. Under the high transition risk scenario (Delayed transition) conduct risk is also
expected to be heightened for the same reasons in the short and medium term due to
physical impacts from existing greenhouse gas in the atmosphere.

– Western Australia

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

• The long-term scenarios included a high transition risk scenario (Net zero 2050);
higher transition risk scenario (Delayed transition) and a high physical risk scenario
(Current policies) to enable the assessment of a range of plausible climate physical
and transition risk outcomes and to address the uncertainty of potential developments
associated with the trajectory of climate change.

– Rest of Queensland

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

– Melbourne

• Acute event scenarios were also run to consider severe but plausible climate-related
events that could impact the profitability of the Bank in any given year.
Diversity of scenarios

SUSTAINABILITY

Input/assumption

– Non-renewable power generation

CREATING VALUE

We use a combination of credible, long-term global climate scenarios that provide consistent inputs and assumptions for
differing futures, as well as a range of acute scenarios to explore the impact of individual severe physical and transition
events that could impact the Bank in any given year. This approach helps us to consider the changing impacts on the Bank’s
financial prospects and risk profile over time, due to long-term chronic physical risks; increasingly frequent and severe acute
physical risks; evolving transition risks arising from regulatory change, technological advancements and evolving stakeholder
expectations; and opportunities that may arise to improve climate resilience and finance the transition. Depending on the
nature of changes in the scenarios, in some cases, we may look to rerun previous analyses using updated information.

• The Bank selected three NGFS scenarios (Net zero 2050, Delayed transition and Current policies) for
long-term scenario analysis and engaged an economic advisory firm (Oxford Economics) to develop
macroeconomic forecasts aligned to the NGFS scenarios using their proprietary models. Tailoring of
these variables was performed at a national level, with more granular analysis performed for a selection
of higher transition and physical risk exposed industries and regions to capture parts of the Bank’s
portfolio which may not follow national-level trends.

OVERVIEW

Climate scenario analysis is a rapidly evolving field, with global scenarios regularly produced and updated. We use
assumptions and inputs drawing from credible, global climate scenarios produced by the NGFS and obtain input from external
experts when necessary. We review our choice of scenarios to check, in our view, they have plausible and appropriately severe
outcomes; are consistent with legislative requirements and market practices, including use by peer banks; and they have
appropriate sector and geographic data readily available.

Profit and loss and balance sheet assumptions

• The macroeconomic variables outlined above are key drivers of credit risk models. Credit losses are modelled to respond
in line with changes in the macroeconomic variables.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

• The Bank’s financials, including profit and loss and balance sheet, were modelled assuming no change in current market
dynamics. This gives a high-level view of the relative differences in how the balance sheet may evolve under the different
long-term scenarios. Balance sheet growth and as a result, profitability, is aligned to economic growth as presented in
the scenarios.

FINANCIAL REPORT

National average house prices are assumed to continue growing steadily, at similar rates,
for differing reasons under each scenario. House prices under the Delayed transition and
Net zero scenarios are driven by household income as interest rates are assumed to be
unaffected. Under current policies, house prices remain high due to continued housing
supply shortages which are exacerbated by more frequent severe weather events that
damage property at a time when construction activity is in high demand.

engineering services.

COMMONWEALTH BANK

Long-term scenarios – key variables and assumptions

410

411

Long-term drought impacting the agriculture portfolio

Series of severe cyclones over South-East Queensland
• An external actuarial consulting and analytics firm was engaged to model the paths of two severe
(Category 4) cyclones making landfall in the South-East Queensland (QLD) region in consecutive
years, providing estimated damages at a property level associated with wind, storm surge and cyclone
induced flooding.

• Macroeconomic and financial variables corresponding to the scenario were designed in collaboration with
environmental risk teams and agribusiness credit risk experts. Property value parameters were adjusted
based on their allocation to low or high impact regions.

• Each of these Category 4 cyclones is currently individually assessed as 1-in-200-year events, however the
probability of two severe cyclone events in successive years in this location is currently extremely low.

• To model non-retail credit losses, the Bank’s industry subject matter experts considered the impact of
damages and business disruption on the credit profile of industries with higher inherent risk of impact from
floods and cyclones. Adjusted credit ratings were run through the Bank’s non-retail stress testing model to
assess expected credit losses.

Data as at

The cut-off date for the data used for modelling was December 2024.

Scope of
operations

• The retail scope of this scenario included CBA and Bankwest residential mortgages and Viridian Line of
Credit (VLOC) accounts within the cyclone impacted regions in South-East QLD.

Timeframe

A 10-year timeframe was selected to reflect a long-term drought, with similar duration to Australia’s
millennium drought.

Data as at

The cut-off date for the data used for modelling was December 2024.

Scope

• The scope of this scenario was limited to the CBA Australian agriculture portfolios, and included adjacent
industries such as food wholesaling and manufacturing. Forestry and fishing industries were excluded.

Key inputs
and
assumptions

• Damage from cyclone events – Damage ratios were modelled by an external provider and applied in year
one and year two. The modelling considered a range of factors including distance from cyclone path,
windspeed and topography.
• Insurance coverage (retail) – The likelihood of insurance was modelled by an external provider. This data
was complemented by underinsurance assumptions derived from research.
• Insurance coverage (non-retail) – Overall insurance rate assumptions were internally developed and
allocated using random sampling.

• Mortgage repayment stress – The ability of borrowers to make mortgage repayments was stressed
through factoring in the extent to which costs for demolition, repair and alternate property rental during
repair are covered by insurance, and the repair period. Customers were assumed to have defaulted where
the costs to repair and sell the property exceeded the equity in the property, or where the customer was
in negative equity.

• Residential building value declines – Aligned to damage ratios applied to each property. A linear return to
pre-cyclone values over the repair period is assumed.
• Residential land value declines – Varied by flood risk ratings for each property up to reductions of 35%.
Land values were assumed to recover within a year in Brisbane, quicker than regional areas which were
assumed to recover in five years.

• Business disruption – Infrastructure damage, including to roads and rail lines, interrupts supply chains
causing local production to decline by:
– 10% across non-impacted areas of QLD for three months after each cyclone.
– 30% in moderate and severe impacted zones for three months after each cyclone.

• Farm productivity – Assumed to decline by ~10% in year one, then 20% in year two and
50% in years three to 10.
• Cash rate – Debt financing is important to the farm sector, both to fund new investment and to help
manage variability in farm revenue. The event is therefore assumed to occur during a time when the cash
rate is increasing for reasons unrelated to the drought. The Australian cash rate is assumed to steadily
increase during years one to four settling at 7% from years four to seven and then declining to 5%
by year 10.
• Property value impacts – Properties in high-impact regions initially maintain their value due to
government support and no action taken by banks. Deterioration in value commences towards the end of
year four and continues until the cumulative reduction peaks at ~40% in year eight. The low-impact areas
are assumed to increase in value by 10%.
• International commodity prices – Assumed to remain stable.
• Government support – Assumed in the form of a mix of income assistance, concessional loans, tax
relief, and water and transport subsidies. However, after four years it is assumed to be insufficient to
compensate for the combination of rising interest rates and declining revenue.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

• Insurance companies and re-insurers – Assumed to remain solvent.
• Government support – Assumed to be limited.

Key inputs
and
assumptions

This scenario only assessed the impact of physical climate risks.

FINANCIAL REPORT

• Unemployment rates – A significant rise in unemployment rates was assumed to occur in the cyclone
years with an increase of 8% and 20% from 2024 by the end of year two for Brisbane and regional
South-East QLD, respectively. Unemployment improves from year three as infrastructure is repaired and
jobs are created during the rebuild.

Physical or
transition risk

DIRECTORS’ REPORT

• Insurability declines – Following each cyclone, insurance premiums were increased by ~20% and take-up
rates were reduced.

• Regional exclusions are shown in grey on page 153, and are based on the PMT-CSI tool and the Water
Resources Institute, Aqueduct (Version 4) dataset, where regions were assessed as having low or
negligible risk. By design of the scenario, NT, TAS and North-West WA were also excluded (also shown
in grey on page 153).

CLIMATE DISCLOSURES

• The non-retail scope focused on cyclone impacted regions across the following industries: commercial
property; agriculture and forestry; mining oil and gas; manufacturing; transport and storage; business
services; construction; wholesale trade and retail trade.

SUSTAINABILITY

A five-year timeframe was selected to reflect the time expected for the economic and credit-related
impacts to flow through to customers.

• For adjacent agriculture industries such as food manufacturing and wholesaling, ANZSIC rating movements
were directly provided by agribusiness subject matter experts and applied to the non-retail stress test model
to estimate expected credit losses.

CREATING VALUE

• The impact of macroeconomic and financial variables was assessed to arrive at final agriculture ANZSIC-level
credit rating movements. These ANZSIC rating movements and relevant macroeconomic variables were
subsequently processed through the Bank’s non-retail stress testing model to estimate expected credit
losses for customers in both high-impact and low-impact regions.

• Property-level damage and regional assumptions were run through the Bank’s home loans stress testing
models to assess expected credit losses.

Timeframe

• The selection of the drought affected areas and the segmentation of these areas into lower and higher
drought impact was based on data from both the Bank’s productivity mapping tool and climate stress index
tool (PMT-CSI tool) and the Water Resources Institute, Aqueduct (Version 4) dataset, which identifies areas
expected to suffer from high water stress in the future under their 2030 pessimistic scenario.

approach
